topics.title: Introduction to Parallel Computing: Overview, Importance, Types of Parallelism, and Architectures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST759 - PARALLEL ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Parallel Computing: Overview, Importance, Types of Parallelism, and Architectures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Parallel Computing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Parallel Computing** is a computational paradigm in which multiple processing elements (cores, processors, or computing nodes) cooperate to solve a single problem by dividing the workload into smaller sub-tasks that are executed **simultaneously** and **coordinated** via communication and synchronization mechanisms.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology for **PECST759 (Parallel Algorithms)**, parallel computing is the foundation upon which every subsequent module (PRAM models, sorting, searching, graph algorithms) is built. The course outcome **CO1** directly maps to the ability to *analyze the need, scope, and architectural backdrop of parallelism*.

**Key Terminology (Board-Exam Favourites):**

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Node** | A single computational unit containing processor(s), memory, and I/O |
| **Processor / Core** | The physical execution engine within a node |
| **Task** | A logical unit of work assigned to a processor |
| **Granularity** | The size of a task (fine, medium, coarse) |
| **Speedup (S)** | Ratio of sequential execution time to parallel execution time |
| **Efficiency (E)** | Speedup divided by number of processors |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Restaurant Kitchen 🍳

Imagine a single chef cooking a 10-course meal **sequentially** — this is **serial computing**. Now imagine **5 chefs** working in parallel, each preparing 2 courses simultaneously — this is **parallel computing**.

- The **recipe** = the **algorithm**
- The **kitchen** = the **parallel machine**
- The **chefs** = the **processors/cores**
- **Sharing a common pantry** = **shared memory**
- **Each chef has their own pantry, but they pass dishes to each other** = **distributed memory**
- **Coordination of plating** = **synchronization**

> [!NOTE]
> Just as 5 chefs cannot cook faster if 80% of the time is spent waiting for one chef to chop vegetables (a sequential bottleneck), a parallel program cannot scale beyond a limit defined by its **serial portion** — captured elegantly by **Amdahl's Law**.

## 1.3 Why Parallel Computing? — The Motivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Moore's Law Slowdown (Post-2005):** Clock frequencies plateaued around **3–4 GHz** due to physical limits (power wall, memory wall, ILP wall). The industry shifted to **multi-core** and **many-core** architectures. This is *why* parallelism is no longer optional.

The 3 fundamental reasons for moving to parallel computing are:

1. **Performance** — Solve larger problems in less wall-clock time.
2. **Scale** — Address problems too large for a single machine's memory.
3. **Throughput / Economics** — Higher total work per unit cost through concurrent job execution.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Types of Parallelism

Parallelism exists at multiple levels of the hardware/software stack:

### A) Bit-Level Parallelism
Increasing the **word size** (8 → 16 → 32 → 64 bits) so more operations are done per cycle. A 64-bit adder adds 64 bits in one clock — 8× faster than an 8-bit adder for the same job.

### B) Instruction-Level Parallelism (ILP)
Pipelining, superscalar issue, out-of-order execution, branch prediction. The processor itself discovers parallelism among instructions (e.g., Intel Hyper-Threading).

### C) Data Parallelism (SIMD-style)
> [!NOTE]
> **Data Parallelism = Same Operation, Different Data.** Apply the function `f` to every element of an array simultaneously. Example: adding two vectors of size 1,000,000 across 1,000 cores → each core handles 1,000 elements.

### D) Task Parallelism (MIMD-style)
Different operations on different data. E.g., one thread renders a UI, another fetches data from the network, a third processes a payment. **Functional decomposition.**

### E) Pipeline Parallelism
Stages of computation overlap. The classic **MIPS 5-stage pipeline** (IF → ID → EX → MEM → WB) is hardware pipeline parallelism.

## 2.2 Flynn's Taxonomy of Computer Architectures (HIGH-YIELD FOR KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Michael Flynn (1966)** classified architectures based on the multiplicity of **Instruction Streams** and **Data Streams**.

| Class | Instruction Stream | Data Stream | Real-World Example | KTU Board Tag |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **SISD** | Single | Single | Traditional uniprocessor (Intel 486) | Classic Von-Neumann |
| **SIMD** | Single | Multiple | GPU shader cores, Intel SSE/AVX, vector processors | Data-parallel machines |
| **MISD** | Multiple | Single | Rare — fault-tolerant pipelines, systolic arrays | Often in theory only |
| **MIMD** | Multiple | Multiple | Multicore CPUs, clusters, supercomputers | Most modern machines |

## 2.3 Memory Organization (Sub-Classification of MIMD)

### a) Shared Memory (SM) — Tightly Coupled
- **Uniform Memory Access (UMA):** Symmetric Multiprocessors (SMPs), e.g., dual-socket Intel Xeon.
- **Non-Uniform Memory Access (NUMA):** AMD EPYC, modern HPC nodes. Access time depends on memory location.

### b) Distributed Memory (DM) — Loosely Coupled
- Each node has private memory; **message passing** (MPI) is the programming model.
- Examples: Cluster of workstations, supercomputers like Sunway TaihuLight.

### c) Hybrid (Distributed-Shared)
- A cluster of **SMP** nodes. Most modern supercomputers (TOP500 leaders) follow this pattern.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Speedup, Efficiency, Amdahl, Gustafson)

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Isolation Rule:** Use `\vert` instead of vertical pipe `|` in table rows to avoid markdown table breakage.

| Metric | Formula | Description | Symbol Legend |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Speedup** | $S(n) = \dfrac{T_s}{T_p(n)}$ | Ratio of sequential to parallel time | $T_s$ = sequential time, $T_p$ = parallel time |
| **Efficiency** | $E(n) = \dfrac{S(n)}{n}$ | Useful work per processor | $n$ = number of processors |
| **Cost** | $C(n) = n \cdot T_p(n)$ | Product of processors and parallel time | — |
| **Cost-Optimal** | $C(n) = \Theta(T_s)$ | Cost matches best sequential algorithm | — |
| **Amdahl's Speedup** | $S(n) = \dfrac{1}{(1 - p) + \dfrac{p}{n}}$ | Bounded by serial fraction | $p$ = parallel fraction, $(1-p)$ = serial fraction |
| **Amdahl's Limit** | $S_\infty = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ | Max speedup as $n \rightarrow \infty$ | — |
| **Gustafson's Speedup** | $S(n) = n - (1-p)(n-1)$ | Scaled (weak) speedup | $p$ = parallel fraction |
| **Karp-Flatt Metric** | $e = \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{S(n)} - \dfrac{1}{n}}{1 - \dfrac{1}{n}}$ | Experimentally derived serial fraction | — |
| **Isoefficiency** | $T(n) = \Theta(W)$ | Relation of work to problem size $W$ | — |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Weather/Climate Modelling:** WRF, GFS — domain-decomposed across 100,000s of cores.
- **Cryptanalysis:** AES key search — embarrassingly parallel.
- **Bioinformatics:** Sequence alignment (BLAST, Smith-Waterman) on GPU clusters.
- **Deep Learning:** Backpropagation across GPUs/TPUs using data-parallel SGD.
- **Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD):** Finite-element mesh partitioning via METIS.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Amdahl's Law

> [!NOTE]
> **Amdahl's Law (1967)** states that the speedup of a parallel program is limited by its **inherently serial** portion.

### Assumptions
- Let $p$ be the **parallel fraction** of the code (can be sped up by parallelization).
- Let $(1 - p)$ be the **serial fraction** (cannot be parallelized).
- Let $n$ be the number of processors executing the parallel fraction in parallel.
- Let $T_s$ be the total sequential execution time on a single processor.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Decompose the total sequential time into serial and parallel components.

$$
T_s = T_{serial} + T_{parallel}
$$

**Step 2:** Normalize by setting $T_s = 1$. This is a standard convention that simplifies all subsequent algebra.

$$
1 = (1 - p) + p
$$

This means the serial portion takes $(1-p)$ units of time and the parallel portion takes $p$ units of time.

**Step 3:** When running on $n$ processors, only the **parallel fraction** speeds up. The serial portion still executes on a single processor.

$$
T_{parallel}^{(n)} = \frac{p}{n}
$$

**Step 4:** The total parallel execution time becomes the sum of the unchanged serial portion and the accelerated parallel portion.

$$
T_p(n) = (1 - p) + \frac{p}{n}
$$

**Step 5:** By definition, **Speedup $S(n)$ = Sequential Time / Parallel Time**.

$$
S(n) = \frac{T_s}{T_p(n)} = \frac{1}{(1 - p) + \dfrac{p}{n}}
$$

**Step 6:** To find the **theoretical upper bound**, take the limit as the number of processors approaches infinity.

$$
S_\infty = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{1}{(1 - p) + \dfrac{p}{n}} = \frac{1}{1 - p}
$$

**Step 7 (Insight):** Even with infinite processors, the speedup is capped. For example:
- $p = 0.95$ → $S_\infty = 1/0.05 = 20\times$
- $p = 0.99$ → $S_\infty = 1/0.01 = 100\times$
- $p = 0.90$ → $S_\infty = 1/0.10 = 10\times$

This is the famous **"1% of serial code wastes 90% of the speedup"** insight.

## 3.2 Derivation of Gustafson's Law (Scaled Speedup)

> [!NOTE]
> **Gustafson-Barsis Law (1988)** counters Amdahl by observing that with more processors, **users solve larger problems** rather than fixing the problem size.

**Step 1:** Express the parallel runtime on $n$ processors. The user now scales the **workload** linearly.

$$
T_p(n) = (1 - p) \cdot s + p \cdot s
$$

where $s$ is the scaled execution time on a single processor. On the new system, the **serial** part runs in time $(1-p)\cdot s$, and the **parallel** part takes time $p$ (since it has $n$ processors available).

**Step 2:** The corresponding single-processor time scales the same way.

$$
T_s = (1 - p)\cdot s + n \cdot p \cdot s
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the ratio.

$$
S_{G}(n) = \frac{(1 - p)\cdot s + n \cdot p \cdot s}{(1 - p)\cdot s + p \cdot s}
$$

**Step 4:** Divide numerator and denominator by $s$.

$$
S_{G}(n) = \frac{(1 - p) + n \cdot p}{(1 - p) + p} = (1 - p) + n \cdot p
$$

**Step 5:** Rearrange algebraically to expose the linear form.

$$
S_{G}(n) = n - (1 - p)(n - 1)
$$

This is the **linear (weak) speedup** result. Unlike Amdahl, Gustafson predicts that doubling processors roughly doubles the achieved work.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Amdahl's Law)

> **Problem (KTU Style):** A program has a parallel fraction of $p = 0.90$. Compute the speedup for $n = 4, 16, 64, 256$ processors.

**Solution:**

$$
S(n) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.90) + \dfrac{0.90}{n}} = \frac{1}{0.10 + \dfrac{0.90}{n}}
$$

| Processors $n$ | Computation | Speedup $S(n)$ | Efficiency $E(n) = S(n)/n$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 4 | $\frac{1}{0.10 + 0.225} = \frac{1}{0.325}$ | $\approx 3.08$ | $0.77$ |
| 16 | $\frac{1}{0.10 + 0.05625} = \frac{1}{0.15625}$ | $\approx 6.40$ | $0.40$ |
| 64 | $\frac{1}{0.10 + 0.01406}$ | $\approx 8.73$ | $0.14$ |
| 256 | $\frac{1}{0.10 + 0.00352}$ | $\approx 9.66$ | $0.04$ |
| $\infty$ | $\frac{1}{0.10}$ | $10.00$ | $0.00$ |

**Observation:** The speedup asymptotically approaches the Amdahl limit of $10\times$ regardless of how many processors we add. Efficiency drops sharply — this is the well-known **diminishing returns of parallelization**.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (High-Yield for 14-Mark Questions)

| Feature | Shared Memory | Distributed Memory | Hybrid |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Programming Model** | Threads (Pthreads, OpenMP) | MPI | MPI + OpenMP |
| **Communication** | Implicit (shared vars) | Explicit (send/recv) | Both |
| **Synchronization** | Locks, barriers, atomics | Barrier in MPI, point-to-point | Both |
| **Scalability** | Limited (~$10^2$ cores) | Very high (~$10^6$ cores) | Highest |
| **Memory Cost** | Single unified address space | Local memory per node | Distributed |
| **Latency** | Low (bus/cache-coherent) | High (network) | Mixed |
| **Example** | Symmetric Multiprocessor | Beowulf cluster | Sunway TaihuLight |
| **Pros** | Easy programming, fast comm. | Scalable, fault-tolerant | Best of both |
| **Cons** | Bottleneck, false sharing | Complex programming | Most complex setup |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.3 Amdahl's Speedup Behaviour (Conceptual Curve)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Serial fraction 1 minus p]:::start --> B{Dominant Constraint}:::decision
    B -- Strong serial --> C[Speedup capped at 1 over 1 minus p]:::cap
    B -- Weak serial --> D[Speedup grows roughly linearly]:::linear
    C --> E[Diminishing returns]:::warn
    D --> F[Gustafson scaled regime]:::good
    E --> G[Use Amdahl]:::pick
    F --> H[Use Gustafson]:::pick

    classDef start fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#800000,color:#1a1a1a
    classDef decision fill:#fff2b3,stroke:#806600,color:#1a1a1a
    classDef cap fill:#ff8080,stroke:#990000,color:#1a1a1a
    classDef linear fill:#80ff80,stroke:#006600,color:#0a1a0a
    classDef warn fill:#ffcc66,stroke:#995c00,color:#1a1a1a
    classDef good fill:#66ccff,stroke:#003366,color:#0a1a2a
    classDef pick fill:#d4b3ff,stroke:#4a148c,color:#0a1a2a
```

## 4.4 Performance Metrics Triangle (KTU Favourite Visual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SPEEDUP[Speedup S]:::metric -->|divide by n| EFF[Efficiency E]:::metric
    EFF -->|overhead O| COST[Cost C]:::metric
    COST -->|processors times time| SPEEDUP
    SPEEDUP --> AMDAHL[Amdahl Limit]:::law
    EFF --> KARP[Karp Flatt Metric]:::law
    COST --> ISOE[Cost Optimal]:::law
    AMDAHL --> APPS[Architectural Design]:::goal
    KARP --> APPS
    ISOE --> APPS

    classDef metric fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003d80,color:#0a1a2a
    classDef law fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#5b2a6b,color:#0a1a2a
    classDef goal fill:#ffdf80,stroke:#805500,color:#1a1a1a
```

> [!NOTE]
> These four Mermaid diagrams map the conceptual landscape: (1) overall taxonomy, (2) shared vs distributed memory layout, (3) Amdahl vs Gustafson decision flow, and (4) the metrics-laws-applications triangle. Together they form a complete mental model for the topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** 
**Define parallel computing. List any two reasons why it has become essential in modern processor design.**

**Model Answer:**
Parallel computing is a computation paradigm where multiple processing elements work cooperatively and simultaneously on sub-tasks of a single problem. It is essential because:

1. **Power wall:** Increasing clock frequency beyond ~3–4 GHz causes exponential power dissipation; parallelism offers performance via integration of cores.
2. **Memory wall & ILP wall:** Single-thread performance has plateaued; only multi-core and many-core paths remain.
3. **Problem scale:** Modern scientific and big-data workloads (climate modelling, deep learning) cannot fit on a uniprocessor's memory.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** 
**Define parallel computing. List any two reasons why it has become essential in modern processor design.**

**Model Answer:**
Parallel computing is a computation paradigm where multiple processing elements work cooperatively and simultaneously on sub-tasks of a single problem. It is essential because:

1. **Power wall:** Increasing clock frequency beyond ~3–4 GHz causes exponential power dissipation; parallelism offers performance via integration of cores.
2. **Memory wall & ILP wall:** Single-thread performance has plateaued; only multi-core and many-core paths remain.
3. **Problem scale:** Modern scientific and big-data workloads (climate modelling, deep learning) cannot fit on a uniprocessor's memory.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
**Explain the difference between data parallelism and task parallelism with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Data Parallelism:** The *same* operation is applied to *different* data items simultaneously. Example: adding two arrays element-wise using 4 cores — core 1 handles indices 0–249, core 2 handles 250–499, and so on.
- **Task Parallelism:** *Different* operations are performed on potentially *different* data simultaneously. Example: in a web server, one thread serves HTTP requests while another thread writes logs to disk and a third runs garbage collection.

The key difference is in *what* is parallelized: the **data** vs. the **function**.

## 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **Module 1 KTU directive:** Answer **either** (A) **or** (B) in full.

### Question A (14 Marks) — Architecture and Metrics Focus

**[KTU University Exam – 2024, Model]**

**Q. (a) [7 Marks]** Classify parallel architectures using **Flynn's Taxonomy**. With a neat diagram, explain **SISD**, **SIMD**, **MISD**, and **MIMD**. Give one real-world example for each.

**Model Answer:**

Flynn's Taxonomy classifies computer systems by the multiplicity of **instruction** and **data** streams:

**SISD (Single Instruction, Single Data):**
- Single instruction stream, single data stream.
- Conventional uniprocessor / Von-Neumann machine.
- Example: Intel 486, classical desktop PC.
- Throughput limited by clock speed; no inherent parallelism.

**SIMD (Single Instruction, Multiple Data):**
- A single instruction operates on multiple data elements simultaneously.
- All processing units execute the same operation in lockstep but on different data.
- Examples: GPU shader cores, Intel SSE/AVX vector units, Cray-1 vector processor, modern SIMD co-processors.

**MISD (Multiple Instruction, Single Data):**
- Multiple instruction streams process a single data stream.
- Rare; primarily a theoretical class.
- Examples: fault-tolerant aerospace pipelines, classical **systolic arrays** (e.g., for matrix multiplication).

**MIMD (Multiple Instruction, Multiple Data):**
- Each processor fetches its own instructions and operates on its own data.
- Most modern parallel systems (multicore CPUs, clusters, supercomputers).
- Sub-divided into **shared memory** (SMP, NUMA) and **distributed memory** (clusters).

**Diagram:**

```
       Inst Stream       Data Stream
       ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐
SISD   │ Single   │    │ Single   │   -> Classical uniprocessor
SIMD   │ Single   │    │ Multiple │   -> GPUs, vector units
MISD   │ Multiple │    │ Single   │   -> Systolic arrays
MIMD   │ Multiple │    │ Multiple │   -> Multicore, clusters
       └──────────┘    └──────────┘
```

**Q. (b) [7 Marks]** A program has a parallel fraction of $p = 0.95$. The program is run on a multiprocessor with $n = 64$ processors. 
- (i) Compute the speedup using Amdahl's Law. 
- (ii) What is the maximum achievable speedup with infinite processors? 
- (iii) Compute the efficiency.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Speedup using Amdahl's Law:**

$$
S(64) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.95) + \dfrac{0.95}{64}} = \frac{1}{0.05 + 0.01484375} = \frac{1}{0.06484375} \approx 15.42
$$

**(ii) Maximum speedup (as $n \to \infty$):**

$$
S_\infty = \frac{1}{1 - 0.95} = \frac{1}{0.05} = 20
$$

**(iii) Efficiency:**

$$
E(64) = \frac{S(64)}{n} = \frac{15.42}{64} \approx 0.241
$$

**Interpretation:** The 5% serial portion is the dominant performance bottleneck — adding more than ~40 processors yields diminishing returns.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Memory Models and Gustafson Focus

**[KTU University Exam – 2024, Model]**

**Q. (a) [7 Marks]** Compare **Shared Memory** and **Distributed Memory** multiprocessor architectures. Mention the programming model, communication mechanism, and at least two advantages and disadvantages of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Shared Memory (SM) | Distributed Memory (DM) |
|---|---|---|
| **Programming Model** | Threads (Pthreads, OpenMP) | Message Passing (MPI, PVM) |
| **Communication** | Implicit via shared variables | Explicit via send/receive calls |
| **Memory Layout** | One global address space | Local memory per node |
| **Synchronization** | Locks, semaphores, barriers | Point-to-point, collective ops |
| **Scalability** | Limited to a few hundred cores | Scales to millions of cores |
| **Hardware Example** | Dual-socket Intel Xeon | Beowulf cluster, supercomputers |

**Advantages of Shared Memory:**
1. Easier programming — no need to explicitly marshal data.
2. Low communication latency (memory bus / cache-coherence).

**Disadvantages of Shared Memory:**
1. Scalability is limited by memory bandwidth (bus contention).
2. Complex hardware (coherence protocols), expensive to build.

**Advantages of Distributed Memory:**
1. Highly scalable — adding nodes is straightforward.
2. Fault-tolerant — failure of one node doesn't crash the system.

**Disadvantages of Distributed Memory:**
1. Programming is harder — the developer must manage communication.
2. Higher latency due to network transfers.

**Q. (b) [7 Marks]** State and derive **Gustafson's Law**. For a problem where $p = 0.80$ and $n = 32$, compute the scaled speedup. Comment on why Gustafson's result differs from Amdahl's prediction.

**Model Solution:**

**Statement:** Gustafson's Law (1988) — when more processors are available, users typically scale the **problem size** to use the additional resources, achieving a **scaled (weak) speedup**:

$$
S_G(n) = n - (1 - p)(n - 1)
$$

**Derivation Recap (from Section 3.2):**
- $T_p(n) = (1 - p) \cdot s + p \cdot s$ (the parallel time stays fixed because the user works on a larger problem)
- $T_s = (1 - p) \cdot s + n \cdot p \cdot s$ (the single processor would take this scaled time)
- $S_G = T_s / T_p = n - (1 - p)(n - 1)$.

**Computation for $p = 0.80$ and $n = 32$:**

$$
S_G(32) = 32 - (1 - 0.80)(32 - 1) = 32 - 0.20 \times 31 = 32 - 6.2 = 25.8
$$

**Comparison with Amdahl's Law (fixed problem):**

$$
S_{A}(32) = \frac{1}{0.20 + 0.025} = \frac{1}{0.225} \approx 4.44
$$

**Comment:** The huge difference ($25.8\times$ vs $4.44\times$) arises because Gustafson assumes the user **scales the workload** to keep all $n$ processors busy, whereas Amdahl assumes the workload is **fixed**. In real engineering practice (weather forecasting, training larger neural networks), Gustafson's assumption is often more accurate.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit confusion in Amdahl:** Many students forget that $T_s$ is normalized to 1. Always state this explicitly before substituting.
> 2. **Mixing Amdahl with Gustafson:** Don't use Gustafson's formula in a problem that says "fixed problem size" — that's Amdahl.
> 3. **Efficiency formula:** $E = S/n$, not $E = n/S$. A common mistake.
> 4. **Flynn's diagram:** MIMD is not a single class — examiners expect the **shared vs distributed** sub-classification for full marks.
> 5. **Skip the verbal explanation:** Numerical answers alone fetch partial marks; always **interpret** the result (e.g., "efficiency drops, so we have overhead").

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Introduction to Parallel Computing**

- ⭐ **Definition:** Parallel computing = multiple processors + simultaneous execution + coordination.
- ⭐ **Three motivations:** Performance, Scale, Throughput/Economics.
- ⭐ **Power wall (2005):** Why we went multi-core.
- ⭐ **Four parallelism types:** Bit-level, ILP, Data, Task. (Pipeline is a special case of task/ILP.)
- ⭐ **Flynn's Taxonomy:** SISD, SIMD, MISD, MIMD. **MISD is rare/theoretical.**
- ⭐ **MIMD sub-types:** Shared (UMA, NUMA) vs Distributed vs Hybrid.
- ⭐ **Speedup:** $S = T_s / T_p$.
- ⭐ **Efficiency:** $E = S / n$, ranges $(0, 1]$.
- ⭐ **Amdahl's Law:** $S = 1 / ((1-p) + p/n)$; **limit** = $1/(1-p)$.
- ⭐ **Gustafson's Law:** $S = n - (1-p)(n-1)$; assumes **scaled** workload.
- ⭐ **Karp-Flatt Metric:** extracts experimentally the serial fraction $e$.
- ⭐ **Cost-Optimal:** Parallel cost matches the best sequential algorithm cost.
- ⭐ **Real-world:** GPU = SIMD, Multicore CPU = MIMD-SMP, Cluster = MIMD-DM.
- ⭐ **Communication types:** Shared memory (implicit) vs Message passing (explicit).
- ⭐ **Programming standards:** OpenMP (SM), MPI (DM), CUDA/OpenCL (GPU).
- ⭐ **KTU favourite phrases:** "diminishing returns," "scalability bottleneck," "Amdahl's pessimism," "weak vs strong scaling."

> **End of Module 1 — Introduction to Parallel Computing notes (KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 output).**

topics.title: Parallel Programming Models: PRAM, BSP, LogP, and Memory Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST759 - PARALLEL ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Parallel Programming Models: PRAM, BSP, LogP, and Memory Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Parallel Programming Models: PRAM, BSP, LogP, and Memory Models

## 1.1 Introduction to Parallel Programming Models

A **Parallel Programming Model** is an abstract machine interface that sits between the hardware architecture and the software, providing a conceptual framework to express parallelism, communication, and synchronization among processing elements. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PECST759 – Parallel Algorithms**, the foundational models every B.Tech student must master are **PRAM**, **BSP**, **LogP**, and the **Memory Models** (Shared, Distributed, Hybrid, and Consistency).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> "Parallel Programming Models – PRAM, BSP, LogP, Memory Models – this topic carries an expected weightage of 10-12 marks in the End Semester Evaluation (ESE) under Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) questions."

### 1.2 Formal Definition

A *parallel programming model* is a **formal abstraction** that specifies:
1. **How computation is decomposed** across multiple processors.
2. **How processors communicate** data.
3. **How synchronization** between processors is enforced.
4. **How memory is accessed** (uniform vs. non-uniform, shared vs. distributed).

Mathematically, a parallel model $M$ is defined as the tuple:
$$ M = (P, M, C, S) $$
where:
- $P$ = set of $p$ processors $\left\{P_1, P_2, \dots, P_p\right\}$
- $M$ = memory subsystem (shared, distributed, hybrid)
- $C$ = communication mechanism (shared variables / message passing)
- $S$ = synchronization primitive (locks, barriers, semaphores)

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The KTU University Library System**
> Imagine a KTU Central Library serving 1,000 students (processors) preparing for exams:
> - **PRAM** = A single huge reading room with a giant shared whiteboard. Everyone reads and writes to the same whiteboard instantly, but in reality only one student can physically hold a marker at a time. The model *ignores* this real conflict.
> - **BSP** = The library divides the study into rounds. Each round, students read quietly (compute), then walk to friends to discuss (communicate), and then a whistle blows for everyone to align to the next round (barrier).
> - **LogP** = A delivery courier model. Each message takes $L$ time to arrive, costs $o$ effort to send, and the courier can only carry one message every $g$ time units — modeling real network delays.
> - **Memory Models** = The decision of whether the library has a *single shared reading room* (UMA), *multiple reading rooms with different access times* (NUMA), or *separate reading rooms with inter-library courier* (Distributed).

### 1.4 Need for Parallel Programming Models

| Real Hardware Challenge | How Models Help |
|---|---|
| Clock speed bottleneck | Abstract multiple virtual processors |
| Memory hierarchy complexity | Model communication costs precisely |
| Synchronization overhead | Quantify barrier/lock latency |
| Algorithm portability | Allow cost analysis independent of vendor |
| Performance prediction | Use cost functions like $T_p = T_{\text{comp}} + T_{\text{comm}} + T_{\text{sync}}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Course Outcome Mapping (KTU 2024):**
> - **CO1:** *Understand the structure of parallel processing platforms, including parallel programming models, algorithms, and complexity theory.*
> This topic directly satisfies **CO1** of Module 1 at the **Remember (L1) and Understand (L2)** levels of Bloom's Taxonomy.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 PRAM – Parallel Random Access Machine

The **PRAM model** is the most classical and idealized parallel computation model. It was introduced as a natural extension of the sequential RAM model.

### 2.1.1 PRAM Architecture (Conceptual)

- $p$ processors $P_1, P_2, \dots, P_p$
- A single, globally shared memory of $M$ cells
- All processors execute in **lockstep synchrony** (SIMD-style)
- Each processor has its own local program counter
- Communication happens *implicitly* through shared memory

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard PRAM Cost Metrics:**
> - $T_p$ = parallel time with $p$ processors
> - $W$ = total work = $p \cdot T_p$
> - $T_{\text{seq}}$ = best sequential time
> - **Speedup** $S_p = T_{\text{seq}} / T_p$
> - **Efficiency** $E_p = S_p / p = T_{\text{seq}} / W$

### 2.1.2 PRAM Sub-Models (Exclusive vs Concurrent Access)

The PRAM family is classified based on how concurrent reads and writes to the *same* memory cell are handled:

| Sub-Model | Read Conflict | Write Conflict | Strength | KTU Exam Focus |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **EREW** (Exclusive Read, Exclusive Write) | Not allowed | Not allowed | Most restrictive, most realistic | Most common in ESE questions |
| **CREW** (Concurrent Read, Exclusive Write) | Allowed | Not allowed | Models broadcast reads | Frequently asked |
| **ERCW** (Exclusive Read, Concurrent Write) | Not allowed | Allowed (with rule) | Rarely used | Rare |
| **CRCW** (Concurrent Read, Concurrent Write) | Allowed | Allowed (with rule) | Most powerful, most idealized | Common in complexity proofs |

### 2.1.3 CRCW Sub-Variants (Write Conflict Resolution)

When multiple processors write concurrently, we must define what value "wins":

1. **Common CRCW** – All writes must be the *same* value; otherwise undefined.
2. **Arbitrary CRCW** – Any one of the written values wins (non-deterministic).
3. **Priority CRCW** – Processor with *lowest index* wins.
4. **Sum/Combining CRCW** – The values are *combined* (e.g., summed via OR/AND).

### 2.1.4 Brent's Theorem (Work–Time Framework)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Brent's Theorem (Foundational Result):**
> If a PRAM algorithm performs $W$ total work in $T$ parallel steps, then for any $p$ processors:
> $$ T_p \le \frac{W}{p} + T_{\infty} $$
> where $T_{\infty}$ is the critical path length (span).
> Equivalently, the algorithm can be scheduled in $O(W/p + T)$ time on $p$ processors.

This theorem is the **single most important KTU-asked formula** from PRAM theory.

### 2.1.5 PRAM Strengths and Weaknesses

- ✅ **Strengths:** Simple, elegant, hides communication cost, great for complexity analysis.
- ❌ **Weaknesses:** Ignores memory contention, ignores communication latency, unrealistic for large $p$.

---

## 2.2 BSP – Bulk Synchronous Parallel Model

The **BSP model** was proposed by Leslie Valiant (1990) as a bridge between PRAM (too abstract) and real hardware (too complex).

### 2.2.1 BSP Components

A BSP computer consists of three components:
1. **$p$ processor/memory pairs** – each has local memory and computation ability.
2. **A communication network** – delivers point-to-point messages.
3. **A global synchronization mechanism** – supports barrier synchronization.

### 2.2.2 The Superstep (Core Concept)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition – Superstep:**
> A *superstep* is a unit of BSP execution consisting of:
> (i) **Computation phase** – each processor performs local compute operations.
> (ii) **Communication phase** – processors send/receive messages via the network.
> (iii) **Barrier synchronization** – all processors wait until everyone finishes.

BSP algorithms are written as a sequence of $S$ supersteps.

### 2.2.3 BSP Parameters (The "Cost Quartet")

BSP performance is characterized by four parameters $p, g, L, s$:

| Parameter | Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of processors | $p$ | Parallelism available | integer |
| Computation speed | $s$ | Time per local operation | seconds/flop |
| Communication gap | $g$ | Minimum gap between sends | seconds/word |
| Barrier latency | $L$ | Time to synchronize all processors | seconds |

### 2.2.4 BSP Cost Formula (Critical for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BSP Cost Equation (Valiant 1990):**
> For an algorithm consisting of $S$ supersteps, with $W_i$ work and $h_i$ messages in the $i$-th superstep:
> $$ T_{\text{BSP}} = \sum_{i=1}^{S} \left( W_i \cdot s + h_i \cdot g + L \right) $$
> Or more compactly: $T_{\text{BSP}} = W \cdot s + g \cdot H + S \cdot L$, where $H = \sum h_i$ is total message volume.

The term $g \cdot H$ is called the **communication cost**, and $S \cdot L$ is the **synchronization cost**.

### 2.2.5 BSP Characteristics

- ✅ **Strengths:** Accounts for communication and synchronization explicitly; portable; analytically tractable.
- ❌ **Weaknesses:** Coarse-grained supersteps; barrier overhead is fixed.

---

## 2.3 LogP Model

The **LogP model** was proposed by Culler, Karp, Patterson, and Sahay (1993) as a refinement of BSP that captures more realistic features of distributed-memory multiprocessors.

### 2.3.1 The LogP Parameters (Four Lowercase)

| Parameter | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Latency** | $L$ | Upper bound on time to send a small message from one processor to another |
| **Overhead** | $o$ | Time a processor is *occupied* in sending or receiving a message |
| **Gap** | $g$ | Minimum time between consecutive message sends or receives by one processor |
| **Processors** | $P$ | Total number of processors |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constraint in LogP:** $g \ge o$. This is the **key assumption** that distinguishes LogP from a simpler latency-based model — the network has finite bandwidth.

### 2.3.2 LogP Cost Formula

For a problem requiring $P$ processors and a total of $H$ message-sends, the time complexity is bounded by:

$$ T_{\text{LogP}} \ge \left\lceil \frac{H}{P} \right\rceil \cdot g + L \cdot (\text{critical path depth}) $$

A simpler form often used in KTU problems:

$$ T_{\text{LogP}} = \max\left(T_{\text{compute}}, \left\lceil \frac{H}{P} \right\rceil \cdot g + L \cdot D \right) $$

where $D$ is the depth of the communication dependency graph.

### 2.3.3 BSP vs LogP (Comparison Table)

| Feature | BSP | LogP |
|---|---|---|
| Synchronization | Explicit barrier per superstep | Implicit, point-to-point |
| Granularity | Coarse (supersteps) | Fine (per-message) |
| Latency modeling | $L$ per superstep | $L$ per message |
| Gap modeling | $g$ per superstep | $g$ per message, must satisfy $g \ge o$ |
| Use case | Bulk data, scientific | Fine-grained irregular |

---

## 2.4 Memory Models

### 2.4.1 Physical Memory Architecture

| Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Shared Memory (UMA)** | Uniform Memory Access; all CPUs see same memory with same latency | SMP servers, early multicore |
| **Distributed Memory** | Each processor has private memory; communicate via messages | Clusters, MPI machines |
| **Hybrid (NUMA)** | Mixture — multiple shared-memory nodes connected | Modern multi-socket servers |
| **Distributed Shared Memory (DSM)** | Software abstraction of shared memory over distributed hardware | TreadMarks, IVY |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NUMA Access Time Equation:**
> $$ T_{\text{access}} = T_{\text{local}} + \alpha \cdot \text{HopCount} $$
> where $T_{\text{local}}$ is local memory access time, $\alpha$ is per-hop latency.

### 2.4.2 Memory Consistency Models

These define the legal orderings of memory operations observed by different processors.

| Model | Guarantee | Strength |
|---|---|---|
| **Sequential Consistency (SC)** | All processors see *one total order* consistent with each processor's program order | Strongest, most expensive |
| **Causal Consistency** | Only causally related operations are ordered; concurrent ops may differ | Weaker, faster |
| **PRAM Consistency** | All processors see writes from a *single* processor in the order issued | Weaker still |
| **Weak Consistency** | Synchronization operations act as fences; ordinary ops unordered between fences | Common in DSM |
| **Release Consistency (RC)** | Acquire/Release semantics; further splits weak consistency | Modern standard |
| **Processor Consistency** | Per-processor FIFO order, but writes from different processors can be observed in any order | Intermediate |

### 2.4.3 Key Equation: Speedup and Efficiency

The fundamental metrics that apply to *all* the above models:

$$ S_p = \frac{T_1}{T_p} \quad , \quad E_p = \frac{S_p}{p} = \frac{T_1}{p \cdot T_p} \quad , \quad \text{Cost} = p \cdot T_p $$

For an *optimal* parallel algorithm: $E_p = O(1)$ (constant efficiency).

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Model | Industry Use Case |
|---|---|
| PRAM | Theoretical algorithm design (e.g., $NC$-complexity analysis for VLSI design) |
| BSP | Google Pregel (graph processing), Apache Hama, scientific simulations |
| LogP | Network protocol design, MPI performance tuning, distributed databases |
| Memory Models | Compiler optimization (data placement), Cache-coherency protocol design, OS kernel design |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Overview

| Property | PRAM | BSP | LogP | Memory Models |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Memory Type | Shared | Distributed + Local | Distributed | Shared / Distributed / Hybrid |
| Synchronization | Implicit (lockstep) | Barrier per superstep | Implicit / point-to-point | Memory fences |
| Communication Cost | $0$ (idealized) | $h \cdot g$ per superstep | $L + h \cdot g$ | Depends (cache-coherent vs message passing) |
| Cost Formula | $W = p \cdot T_p$ | $W s + H g + S L$ | $H \cdot g + L \cdot D$ | $T_{\text{local}} + \alpha \cdot \text{Hops}$ |
| Realism | Lowest | Medium | High | Variable |
| Year Introduced | 1978 (Fortune-Wyllie) | 1990 (Valiant) | 1993 (Culler et al.) | 1980s–1990s |

---

## 3.2 Derivations of Core KTU Formulas

### 3.2.1 Derivation of Brent's Theorem (Work-Time Law)

**Statement:** Given a parallel computation with total work $W$ and critical path length $T_{\infty}$, it can be executed in $T_p$ time on $p$ processors where:
$$ T_p \le \frac{W}{p} + T_{\infty} $$

**Proof Sketch (used in KTU 14-mark answers):**

**Step 1 — Decompose work into $T_{\infty}$ time steps:**
On a PRAM with unbounded processors, the computation finishes in $T_{\infty}$ parallel steps. Let $W_i$ be the work done in step $i$ (with $1 \le i \le T_{\infty}$). Then total work is:
$$ W = \sum_{i=1}^{T_{\infty}} W_i $$

**Step 2 — Apply work-stealing or greedy scheduling:**
For any single time step $i$, the $W_i$ operations can be distributed across the $p$ processors. The time needed for step $i$ on $p$ processors is:
$$ t_i = \left\lceil \frac{W_i}{p} \right\rceil \le \frac{W_i}{p} + 1 $$

**Step 3 — Sum over all steps:**
$$ T_p = \sum_{i=1}^{T_{\infty}} t_i \le \sum_{i=1}^{T_{\infty}} \left( \frac{W_i}{p} + 1 \right) $$

**Step 4 — Separate the sums:**
$$ T_p \le \frac{1}{p} \sum_{i=1}^{T_{\infty}} W_i + \sum_{i=1}^{T_{\infty}} 1 $$

**Step 5 — Substitute $\sum W_i = W$ and $\sum 1 = T_{\infty}$:**
$$ T_p \le \frac{W}{p} + T_{\infty} \quad \blacksquare $$

**Interpretation:** The first term $\frac{W}{p}$ is the *work-balance cost*; the second $T_{\infty}$ is the *critical-path cost*. Brent's theorem is the foundation of **work–span analysis**.

---

### 3.2.2 Derivation of BSP Cost Formula

**Given:** $S$ supersteps, each with $W_i$ local work, $h_i$ messages sent.

**Step 1 — Time for one superstep:**
Each superstep has three sub-costs: computation, communication, barrier.

$$ T_{\text{superstep}_i} = \underbrace{W_i \cdot s}_{\text{local compute}} + \underbrace{h_i \cdot g}_{\text{network traffic}} + \underbrace{L}_{\text{barrier}} $$

**Step 2 — Total BSP time across $S$ supersteps:**
$$ T_{\text{BSP}} = \sum_{i=1}^{S} T_{\text{superstep}_i} $$

**Step 3 — Factor the common barrier cost:**
Barriers occur at the end of every superstep, so total barrier time is $S \cdot L$:
$$ T_{\text{BSP}} = s \sum_{i=1}^{S} W_i + g \sum_{i=1}^{S} h_i + S \cdot L $$

**Step 4 — Define aggregate work and message volume:**
Let $W = \sum W_i$ (total work) and $H = \sum h_i$ (total message volume):
$$ T_{\text{BSP}} = s \cdot W + g \cdot H + L \cdot S $$

**Step 5 — Identify the three regimes:**

- **Computation-bound:** $s W \gg g H$ and $s W \gg L S$ → design more efficient sequential code.
- **Communication-bound:** $g H \gg s W$ → reduce messages, use larger payloads.
- **Synchronization-bound:** $L S$ dominates → merge supersteps to reduce $S$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (BSP, KTU-style):**
> Suppose $p = 16$, $s = 10^{-9}$ s/flop, $g = 10^{-7}$ s/word, $L = 10^{-5}$ s. An algorithm has $W = 10^9$ flops, $H = 10^6$ words, $S = 10$ supersteps. Find $T_{\text{BSP}}$.
>
> *Solution:*
> $T_{\text{BSP}} = (10^{-9})(10^9) + (10^{-7})(10^6) + (10^{-5})(10)$
> $T_{\text{BSP}} = 1 + 0.1 + 0.0001 = 1.1001$ seconds.
> Here, *computation dominates* (1 s out of 1.1 s).

---

### 3.2.3 Derivation of LogP Time Lower Bound

**Given:** $P$ processors, $H$ total messages, $L$ per-message latency, $g$ per-message gap, $o$ overhead.

**Step 1 — Time for one processor to send $H/P$ messages:**
By the gap parameter, a single processor can send at most one message every $g$ time. So the *per-processor send time* is:
$$ T_{\text{send,per}} = \left\lceil \frac{H}{P} \right\rceil \cdot g $$

**Step 2 — Time for the *last* message to arrive:**
After the last message is sent, it must travel for time $L$. The critical path of the algorithm (longest chain of dependent messages) has depth $D$, so end-to-end latency contribution is $L \cdot D$.

**Step 3 — Combine with overlap:**
The total time is the larger of compute and communication:
$$ T_{\text{LogP}} = \max\left( T_{\text{compute}},\ \left\lceil \frac{H}{P} \right\rceil \cdot g + L \cdot D \right) $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Special Case (Pipeline saturation):**
> If messages are pipelined and $g \ge o$, the bottleneck is sending rate:
> $$ T_{\text{LogP}} \approx \frac{H}{P} \cdot g + L \cdot D \quad \text{(for large } H \text{)} $$

---

### 3.2.4 Derivation of Speedup Upper Bound (Amdahl's Law — connection)

Even though this is from a different topic, it's the *direct consequence* of the cost equations above:

**Step 1 — Decompose problem into serial and parallel parts:**
$$ T_p = T_s + \frac{T_p^{\text{par}}}{p} $$

**Step 2 — Form speedup ratio:**
$$ S_p = \frac{T_1}{T_p} = \frac{T_s + T_p^{\text{par}}}{T_s + \frac{T_p^{\text{par}}}{p}} $$

**Step 3 — Take limit $p \to \infty$:**
$$ S_{\infty} = \frac{1}{f_s} \quad \text{where} \quad f_s = \frac{T_s}{T_1} $$

This is **Amdahl's Law** and is a frequently asked supplementary question in KTU exams.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Comparing Two Algorithms

**Problem (KTU 14-mark style):** Two algorithms $A_1$ and $A_2$ are designed for the same problem on a PRAM with $p$ processors. Algorithm $A_1$ has $T_{A_1} = n/p + \log p$, while $A_2$ has $T_{A_2} = n/p^2 + p$. For what values of $p$ is $A_1$ better than $A_2$?

**Solution:**

Set $T_{A_1} = T_{A_2}$:
$$ \frac{n}{p} + \log p = \frac{n}{p^2} + p $$

Rearrange:
$$ n\left(\frac{1}{p} - \frac{1}{p^2}\right) = p - \log p $$

$$ n \cdot \frac{p-1}{p^2} = p - \log p $$

Solving the inequality $T_{A_1} < T_{A_2}$:
$$ \frac{n}{p} + \log p < \frac{n}{p^2} + p $$

$$ n\left(\frac{1}{p} - \frac{1}{p^2}\right) - p + \log p < 0 $$

For large $n$, the dominant term is $n/p$, so $A_1$ wins when $n \gg p^2$. In KTU, students should sketch the asymptotic behavior, plot or tabulate values for $p = 2, 4, 8, 16, 32$ with a fixed $n$, and identify the crossover point.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Always state the *asymptotic regime* explicitly. The phrase "for $n$ sufficiently large, algorithm $A_1$ achieves $O(n/p)$ work and dominates $A_2$" gets full marks.

---

## 3.4 Summary of Derivation Logic

| Formula | Origin of Each Term |
|---|---|
| Brent's: $T_p \le W/p + T_{\infty}$ | Work-balance + critical-path |
| BSP: $T = sW + gH + LS$ | Compute + communication + barrier |
| LogP: $T = H g / P + L D$ | Send-rate saturation + latency-depth |
| Amdahl: $S_{\infty} = 1/f_s$ | Serial fraction dominates |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams below follow the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards**:
> - Node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `pramNode`, not `end`).
> - No markdown bold/italics inside double-quoted labels.
> - Raw uppercase alphanumeric text used inside node labels for clarity.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow: PRAM Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1["Processor 1"] -->|reads/writes| SMem[Shared Global Memory]
    P2["Processor 2"] -->|reads/writes| SMem
    P3["Processor 3"] -->|reads/writes| SMem
    P4["Processor p"] -->|reads/writes| SMem
    SMem -->|returns data| P1
    SMem -->|returns data| P2
    SMem -->|returns data| P3
    SMem -->|returns data| P4
    CLK["Global Clock Lockstep"] -.->|synchronizes| P1
    CLK -.->|synchronizes| P2
    CLK -.->|synchronizes| P3
    CLK -.->|synchronizes| P4
```

**Block-Level Interpretation:**
- All processors share one common memory space.
- A global clock drives all processors in lockstep.
- Communication is implicit (via reads/writes to shared cells).
- This is the **Single Instruction Multiple Data (SIMD)** archetype.

---

## 4.3 BSP Superstep Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start Superstep i] --> B[Local Computation Phase]
    B --> C[Communication Phase send and receive messages]
    C --> D[Barrier Synchronization wait for all]
    D --> E{All processors done}
    E -->|Yes| F[End Superstep i]
    E -->|No| D
    F --> G[Start Superstep i plus 1]
    G --> B
```

**Block-Level Interpretation:**
- Each superstep has 3 logical phases.
- The barrier ensures no processor enters the next superstep early.
- Total cost = sum over all supersteps of (work + gap × messages + latency).

---

## 4.4 LogP Communication Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S[Sender Processor] -->|occupies for o time| OP1[Send Overhead o]
    OP1 -->|travels for L time| NET[Network with Latency L]
    NET -->|occupies for o time| OP2[Receive Overhead o]
    OP2 --> R[Receiver Processor]
    S -.->|next send must wait g time| S
    R -.->|next receive must wait g time| R
```

**Block-Level Interpretation:**
- Each message incurs **two overheads** (sender and receiver) and **one latency** (network).
- The **gap $g$** between consecutive sends/receives models finite bandwidth.
- The constraint $g \ge o$ prevents unrealistic pipeline rates.

---

## 4.5 Memory Architecture Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ROOT[Parallel Computer Memory] --> SHM[Shared Memory]
    ROOT --> DSM[Distributed Memory]
    ROOT --> HYB[Hybrid Memory]

    SHM --> UMA[UMA Uniform Memory Access]
    SHM --> NUMA[NUMA Non Uniform Memory Access]

    DSM --> MPI[MPI Clusters]
    DSM --> DSHM[Distributed Shared Memory Software Layer]

    HYB --> CC[Cache Coherent NUMA ccNUMA]
    HYB --> MCL[Multi Core SMP Nodes Linked]
```

**Block-Level Interpretation:**
- Real systems span this tree.
- The memory model chosen by an algorithm must match the architecture.
- NUMA is dominant in modern HPC servers and cloud VMs.

---

## 4.6 Memory Consistency Model Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SC[Sequential Consistency] --> CC[Causal Consistency]
    CC --> PRC[PRAM Consistency]
    CC --> WC[Weak Consistency]
    PRC --> PC[Processor Consistency]
    WC --> RC[Release Consistency]
    RC --> RCSC[Release Consistency with Scope]
```

**Block-Level Interpretation:**
- Arrow direction = relaxation (looser guarantee).
- Top of the hierarchy is the strongest (most expensive) consistency.
- Bottom is fastest, hardest for programmers.
- **KTU Exam Note:** Sequential Consistency is the gold standard; Release Consistency is the practical industrial choice.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Define PRAM model. List and briefly explain its four variants based on concurrent read/write access.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Parallel Random Access Machine (PRAM)** is an idealized parallel computation model consisting of $p$ processors connected to a single shared global memory. All processors execute instructions in lockstep synchrony, and communication happens implicitly through shared memory reads and writes.

Based on how concurrent access to the *same* memory cell is handled, the four PRAM variants are:

1. **EREW (Exclusive Read Exclusive Write):** No two processors may read or write the same cell simultaneously — most restrictive and realistic.
2. **CREW (Concurrent Read Exclusive Write):** Multiple processors may read the same cell simultaneously, but writes remain exclusive.
3. **ERCW (Exclusive Read Concurrent Write):** Exclusive reads but concurrent writes are allowed (with a defined conflict-resolution rule).
4. **CRCW (Concurrent Read Concurrent Write):** Both concurrent reads and writes are allowed — most powerful but unrealistic without a write-conflict rule (Common / Arbitrary / Priority / Sum).

*(Award: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing and explaining the four variants.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: What is the BSP model? Define the four parameters of BSP.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Bulk Synchronous Parallel (BSP)** model, proposed by Leslie Valiant in 1990, is a parallel computation model consisting of:
- $p$ processor-memory pairs
- A point-to-point communication network
- A barrier synchronization mechanism

BSP computation is organized into a sequence of **supersteps**, each having three phases: local computation, communication, and barrier synchronization.

The four BSP parameters are:

1. **$p$** — number of processors (parallelism).
2. **$g$** — *gap*; minimum time between consecutive message injections (communication throughput inverse).
3. **$L$** — *latency*; time for barrier synchronization across all processors.
4. **$s$** — *local computation speed*; time per local operation.

*(Award: 1 mark for BSP definition + 2 marks for parameters.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q (a)** Explain the PRAM model in detail. Discuss its variants and the role of Brent's theorem in PRAM analysis. **(7 marks)**

**Q (b)** With a neat diagram, explain the BSP model. Derive its cost equation and discuss how the three regimes (compute-bound, communication-bound, synchronization-bound) affect performance. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q (a)

**Definition (1 mark):**
PRAM is an idealized parallel model with $p$ processors sharing a common memory and executing in lockstep synchrony. It is a natural parallel extension of the sequential RAM model.

**Architecture (1 mark):**
- $p$ identical processors $P_1, P_2, \dots, P_p$
- Single shared memory of unbounded size
- Each processor has its own program counter and local registers
- Memory access takes unit time (assumption)

**Variants (3 marks):**
Explain EREW, CREW, ERCW, CRCW as detailed in Section 2.1.2 above. Include the CRCW sub-variants (Common, Arbitrary, Priority, Sum).

**Brent's Theorem (2 marks):**
State and explain:
$$ T_p \le \frac{W}{p} + T_{\infty} $$
where $W$ is total work, $T_{\infty}$ is critical path (span), and $T_p$ is the parallel time on $p$ processors.

**Role in PRAM analysis:** Brent's theorem bridges the gap between work-optimal and time-optimal algorithms. It allows algorithm designers to first design work-efficient algorithms and then derive the time bound for any $p$. It is the foundation of the **work-span model**.

---

#### Model Solution to Q (b)

**BSP Diagram (2 marks):**
[Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.3 above. In an exam, draw three sequential boxes labelled "Computation", "Communication", "Barrier" and a loop arrow returning to "Computation".]

**Superstep concept (1 mark):**
A superstep is a logical unit of BSP execution with three phases. Total $S$ supersteps per algorithm.

**Parameters (1 mark):**
List $p, g, L, s$ with definitions.

**Cost Derivation (2 marks):**
[Show the step-by-step derivation from Section 3.2.2 above.]
$$ T_{\text{BSP}} = s \cdot W + g \cdot H + L \cdot S $$

**Three Regimes (1 mark):**
1. **Compute-bound:** $sW$ dominates. → Optimize sequential code, vectorization.
2. **Communication-bound:** $gH$ dominates. → Reduce message count, batch communications, use larger messages.
3. **Synchronization-bound:** $LS$ dominates. → Merge supersteps to reduce $S$, increase granularity.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q (a)** Describe the LogP model in detail. Explain its four parameters and discuss the constraint $g \ge o$ with an example. **(7 marks)**

**Q (b)** Compare and contrast shared, distributed, and hybrid memory models. Explain the concept of memory consistency with reference to Sequential Consistency and Release Consistency. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q (a)

**LogP Definition (1 mark):**
LogP is a model of distributed-memory parallel machines proposed by Culler, Karp, Patterson, and Sahay (1993). It captures key network characteristics: latency, overhead, gap, and processor count.

**Four Parameters (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $L$ (Latency) | Upper bound on time for a small message to travel from sender to receiver |
| $o$ (Overhead) | Time a processor is *occupied* (busy) in sending or receiving a single message |
| $g$ (Gap) | Minimum time between successive message sends (or receives) by one processor |
| $P$ (Processors) | Number of processor-memory pairs |

**The Constraint $g \ge o$ (2 marks):**
- **Why it exists:** The gap $g$ represents the *minimum time* the network needs between messages; the overhead $o$ is just the *time the CPU is busy*. Realistically, $g$ must be at least $o$ — otherwise the network could accept messages faster than the CPU can prepare them, which is unphysical.
- **Example:** If $o = 1 \mu s$ (CPU time per message), then $g \ge 1 \mu s$. If $g = 2 \mu s$, the network is "slower" than the CPU; the model correctly captures this bottleneck.

**Cost Equation (1 mark):**
$$ T_{\text{LogP}} = \max\left( T_{\text{compute}},\ \left\lceil \frac{H}{P} \right\rceil \cdot g + L \cdot D \right) $$

---

#### Model Solution to Q (b)

**Three Memory Models (3 marks):**

| Model | Pros | Cons | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Shared (UMA) | Easy programming, uniform timing | Memory contention, scalability limit | SMP servers |
| Distributed | Scalable, no contention | Hard to program, message passing | MPI clusters |
| Hybrid (NUMA) | Balanced | Complex coherence | Modern multi-socket servers |

**Memory Consistency (4 marks):**

- **Sequential Consistency (SC):** Lamport (1979). "There exists a *single total order* of all memory operations that is consistent with each processor's program order." Strongest, easiest for programmers; expensive.
- **Release Consistency (RC):** A weak consistency model. Memory operations are tagged *acquire* or *release*. Only when a *release* is followed by an *acquire* do the two processors synchronize. This is the model used in modern C++ (`std::memory_order_release/acquire`) and Java.

**Compare:** SC is one total order; RC is a partial order based on synchronization operations. RC is significantly cheaper but harder to reason about.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes (High Mark Loss Areas):**
>
> 1. **Confusing BSP $L$ with LogP $L$:** In BSP, $L$ is the *barrier* latency (a synchronization cost). In LogP, $L$ is the *message* latency. **Both models use the symbol $L$ for different things.** Examiners will deduct 1-2 marks if you mix them up.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the CRCW write rule:** When asked about CRCW, students often write only "concurrent write" without specifying *which rule* (Common/Arbitrary/Priority/Sum). The rule changes algorithmic power — this is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction in KTU.
>
> 3. **Brent's Theorem misapplication:** A common error is writing $T_p = W/p$ without the $T_{\infty}$ term. The $T_{\infty}$ term is **always required** — it is the critical-path contribution.
>
> 4. **BSP superstep confusion:** Some students think BSP has $L$ *per* message. No — $L$ is per *barrier*, i.e., per *superstep*. A superstep with 100 messages costs $100g + L$, not $100L + g$.
>
> 5. **NUMA vs UMA:** Students often call any multi-core system "distributed". NUMA is *logically shared* but *physically distributed* — this nuance is worth marks.
>
> 6. **Memory Consistency mix-up:** Weak Consistency is **not** the same as PRAM Consistency. Weak uses *fences*; PRAM uses per-processor FIFO write order. Examiners frequently test this.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 1 — Parallel Programming Models)**

### 🔹 PRAM
- [ ] PRAM = $p$ processors + 1 shared memory + global lockstep clock
- [ ] Four variants: **E**REW, **C**REW, **E**RCW, **C**RCW
- [ ] CRCW needs a write rule: Common / Arbitrary / Priority / Sum
- [ ] **Brent's Theorem:** $T_p \le W/p + T_{\infty}$
- [ ] Speedup $S_p = T_1 / T_p$; Efficiency $E_p = S_p / p$

### 🔹 BSP
- [ ] BSP = $p$ processors + network + barriers
- [ ] Execution = sequence of **supersteps**
- [ ] Each superstep: Compute → Communicate → Barrier
- [ ] **Parameters:** $p, g, L, s$
- [ ] **Cost Equation:** $T_{\text{BSP}} = sW + gH + LS$
- [ ] Three regimes: compute-bound, communication-bound, synchronization-bound

### 🔹 LogP
- [ ] **Parameters:** $L$ (latency), $o$ (overhead), $g$ (gap), $P$ (processors)
- [ ] **Constraint:** $g \ge o$ (always)
- [ ] LogP = fine-grained; BSP = coarse-grained
- [ ] **Cost:** $T_{\text{LogP}} = \max(T_{\text{compute}}, \lceil H/P \rceil \cdot g + LD)$

### 🔹 Memory Models
- [ ] **UMA:** uniform access time; **NUMA:** non-uniform; **Distributed:** separate memories
- [ ] **Consistency Hierarchy:** SC ⊃ Causal ⊃ PRAM ⊃ Processor ⊃ Weak ⊃ Release
- [ ] **Sequential Consistency** = single total order; **Release Consistency** = acquire/release semantics
- [ ] Amdahl's Law: $S_{\infty} = 1/f_s$ (where $f_s$ is the serial fraction)

### 🔹 Key Formula Quick-Reference

$$ T_p \le \frac{W}{p} + T_{\infty} \quad \text{(Brent)} $$

$$ T_{\text{BSP}} = sW + gH + LS $$

$$ T_{\text{LogP}} \ge \frac{H}{P} \cdot g + L \cdot D $$

$$ S_p = \frac{T_1}{T_p}, \quad E_p = \frac{S_p}{p} $$

$$ T_{\text{NUMA}} = T_{\text{local}} + \alpha \cdot \text{HopCount} $$

### 🔹 Common KTU Traps
- [ ] Symbol $L$ means different things in BSP and LogP — don't mix
- [ ] EREW ≠ CREW — concurrent reads are crucial for many parallel algorithms
- [ ] Brent's theorem requires the $T_{\infty}$ term — never omit it
- [ ] Weak Consistency ≠ PRAM Consistency — know the difference
- [ ] Barrier cost in BSP is $L$ per *superstep*, not per *message*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Metrics: Speedup, Efficiency, Scalability, Amdahl's Law, and Gustafson's Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST759 - PARALLEL ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Performance Metrics: Speedup, Efficiency, Scalability, Amdahl's Law, and Gustafson's Law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Why Performance Metrics Matter in Parallel Computing

In sequential computing, a single metric — *execution time* — is often sufficient to judge performance. The moment we introduce **parallelism**, the picture changes dramatically. A parallel program runs on **p** processors, and now we must answer harder questions:

- How much *faster* did we actually get? → **Speedup**
- How *well* did we utilize the added hardware? → **Efficiency**
- What happens when we throw *more* processors at the same problem? → **Scalability**
- Is there a *theoretical ceiling* to how parallel we can ever become? → **Amdahl's Law**
- Can we sidestep that ceiling by *growing* the problem? → **Gustafson's Law**

These five metrics form the **evaluation backbone of every parallel algorithm** studied under KTU's PECST759 syllabus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 explicitly demands mastery of these metrics because they reappear in **every** subsequent module — PRAM models, mapping, parallel sorting, graph algorithms, and even the end-semester case study all rely on them.

---

## 1.2 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Terminology)

Let:
- $T_s$ = Execution time of the **best sequential algorithm** on a single processor.
- $T_p$ = Execution time of the **parallel algorithm** on **p** processors.
- $T_1$ = Execution time of the parallel algorithm on a **single** processor (often the serial runtime of the same algorithm).
- $T_\infty$ = Execution time with an **unlimited** number of processors.

### Speedup (S)
$$S(p) = \frac{T_s}{T_p}$$

The factor by which the parallel algorithm is faster than the best sequential one. **Speedup is dimensionless.**

### Efficiency (E)
$$E(p) = \frac{S(p)}{p} = \frac{T_s}{p \cdot T_p}$$

A normalized value between 0 and 1 (ideally) that indicates the *fraction of time* each processor is doing useful work. Expressed as a percentage: $E \times 100\%$.

### Cost (or Parallel Cost / Work)
$$C(p) = p \cdot T_p$$

The **total processor-time product** consumed. A parallel algorithm is *cost-optimal* if $C(p) = \Theta(T_s)$.

### Scalability
The ability of a parallel algorithm to **maintain efficiency** as both the number of processors $p$ **and** the problem size $n$ increase together.

---

## 1.3 The Real-World Analogy: The Laundry Problem

Imagine **washing 100 shirts** in a hostel.

| Scenario | Time |
|---|---|
| 1 person washes 100 shirts alone | 100 minutes (this is $T_s$) |
| 2 people share the work | 50 minutes |
| 4 people share the work | 25 minutes |
| 10 people share the work | 10 minutes — but… |
| 1 shirt is *stitched/ironed* by ONE tailor (serial) | +5 minutes no matter what |

**Key Insights from the Analogy:**
- $S = 100 / 10 = 10$ with 10 workers. **Speedup = 10.**
- $E = 10 / 10 = 1$ or **100%** — every worker is fully utilized.
- But the **stitching step** is the *serial fraction*. No matter how many washers you hire, you still wait 5 minutes. This is **Amdahl's Law** in action.
- If you keep increasing workers in a small laundry room, they start bumping elbows. **Scalability** breaks down.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Mapping:** Workers = processors, shirts = problem size, stitching = serial fraction, bumping elbows = communication overhead.

---

## 1.4 Categories of Speedup

| Type | Condition | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **Linear / Perfect Speedup** | $S(p) = p$ | Ideal but rarely achieved in practice. |
| **Super-linear Speedup** | $S(p) > p$ | Possible due to cache effects, hardware memory hierarchies, or beneficial search-space partitioning. |
| **Sub-linear Speedup** | $S(p) < p$ | The common, realistic case due to overhead. |
| **Negative Speedup** | $S(p) < 1$ | Adding processors *slows* the program (severe overhead). |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often report super-linear speedup as a "violation of Amdahl's Law." It is **not** — it merely indicates that the *best sequential algorithm* for comparison has worse cache locality than the parallel version.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposing a Parallel Program: Serial vs. Parallel Fractions

Any parallel program can be split into two conceptual parts:

1. **Inherently Serial Component (f):** The fraction of work that **cannot** be parallelized. Examples: input reading, initial setup, final aggregation, file I/O.
2. **Parallelizable Component (1 − f):** The fraction that **can** be distributed across processors.

This decomposition is the foundation of both Amdahl's and Gustafson's Laws.

---

## 2.2 Amdahl's Law — *Fixed-Problem-Size* Speedup

**Gene Amdahl, 1967.** Validated for a **fixed workload** as the number of processors grows.

### The Argument
Suppose serial portion takes fraction $f$ of $T_s$ and parallel portion takes $(1-f)$ on one processor. On $p$ processors, the parallel part ideally runs $\frac{1-f}{p}$ of the time, but the serial part remains $f$.

$$T_p = f \cdot T_s + \frac{(1-f) \cdot T_s}{p}$$

$$S_{\text{Amdahl}}(p) = \frac{T_s}{T_p} = \frac{1}{f + \frac{1-f}{p}}$$

### Limiting Behavior
$$\lim_{p \to \infty} S_{\text{Amdahl}}(p) = \frac{1}{f}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Killer Insight:** Even if **99%** of your code is parallel ($f = 0.01$), the **maximum possible speedup is $1/0.01 = 100\times$**, no matter how many processors you buy. This is the **Amdahl Ceiling** or **Strong-Scaling Limit**.

---

## 2.3 Gustafson's Law — *Scaled-Problem-Size* Speedup

**John Gustafson & Edwin Barsis, 1988.** Realized that in practice, we rarely run a *fixed* problem on more hardware. Instead, **scientists increase the problem size** to use the extra machines.

### The Argument
Let the **parallel runtime on p processors be fixed** at $T_p$. As $p$ grows, the *user* enlarges the problem so that the parallel region also takes more time, but the serial part remains constant. Define:
- $s$ = serial execution time.
- $p$ = number of processors.
- $H$ = parallel portion execution time on $p$ processors.

Then $T_p = s + H$, and the scaled speedup is:

$$S_{\text{Gustafson}}(p) = s + p \cdot H = p + (1 - p) \cdot s$$

If we express the serial fraction of the **enlarged** workload as $f'$:

$$S_{\text{Gustafson}}(p) = p - f'(p - 1) = f' + (1 - f') \cdot p$$

### Key Difference from Amdahl
For Amdahl, $f$ is **constant**; for Gustafson, the serial fraction $f'$ **shrinks** as problem size grows. Hence, **linear or even super-linear speedup becomes feasible** for large $n$.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is your **single-source cheat sheet** for Module 1 derivations. Memorize it cold.

| # | Metric | Formula | Variables | Valid Range / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Speedup** | $S(p) = \dfrac{T_s}{T_p}$ | $T_s, T_p$ in seconds | $S \in [0, p]$ (typical) |
| 2 | **Efficiency** | $E(p) = \dfrac{S(p)}{p} = \dfrac{T_s}{p \cdot T_p}$ | $S, p$ | $0 \leq E \leq 1$ (ideal = 1) |
| 3 | **Cost / Work** | $C(p) = p \cdot T_p$ | $p, T_p$ in proc-sec | Cost-optimal iff $C = \Theta(T_s)$ |
| 4 | **Cost-Optimality** | $C(p) = T_s \cdot E(p)$ | All | Efficacious if $= T_s$ |
| 5 | **Amdahl's Speedup** | $S_A(p) = \dfrac{1}{f + \dfrac{1-f}{p}}$ | $f$ = serial fraction | $\lim_{p\to\infty} S_A = 1/f$ |
| 6 | **Amdahl Ceiling** | $S_{\max} = 1/f$ | $f$ | Achieved as $p \to \infty$ |
| 7 | **Gustafson's Speedup** | $S_G(p) = p + (1-p) \cdot f'$ | $f'$ = serial frac. (scaled) | Linear in $p$ if $f' \to 0$ |
| 8 | **Scaled Gustafson** | $S_G(p) = f' + (1-f') \cdot p$ | $f', p$ | Same as above, alternate form |
| 9 | **Overhead** | $T_o(p) = p \cdot T_p - T_s$ | $p, T_p, T_s$ | Must be small for high efficiency |
| 10 | **Iso-Efficiency** | $W = K \cdot \frac{T_o}{T_s - T_o \cdot \text{ratio}}$ | Complex | Defines scalability curve |

> **Mnemonic Trap:** $E = S/p$ — *Efficiency is Speedup per Processor.*

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility: Why These Metrics Live in HPC

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **High-Performance Computing (HPC)** | Evaluating MPI/OpenMP programs on supercomputers (e.g., PARAM, Fugaku). |
| **GPU Computing (CUDA / OpenCL)** | Deciding kernel launch configurations; measuring occupancy. |
| **Cloud Computing** | Right-sizing VM instances for cost-effective scaling. |
| **Data Science / ML** | Deciding how many GPUs to allocate for training ResNet/BERT. |
| **Embedded / IoT** | Multi-core microcontrollers (ARM Cortex-M7, ESP32). |
| **Database Systems** | Parallel query optimization in PostgreSQL, Spark SQL. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Amdahl's Law from First Principles

**Step 1.** Let the total sequential runtime be $T_s$, normalized to $T_s = 1$ for simplicity (units cancel anyway).

$$T_s = 1 \quad \text{(normalized time)}$$

**Step 2.** Decompose $T_s$ into serial and parallel fractions:

$$T_s = f \cdot T_s + (1 - f) \cdot T_s = f + (1 - f) = 1$$

Here $f$ is the fraction of time spent on inherently serial code.

**Step 3.** When we run on $p$ processors, the serial part **cannot** be parallelized — it still takes $f \cdot T_s$.

**Step 4.** The parallel part is divided among $p$ processors. Assuming **perfect load balancing** (no overhead), each processor handles a share of $\frac{1}{p}$. So the parallel portion takes $\frac{(1-f) \cdot T_s}{p}$.

**Step 5.** The total parallel runtime is the **sum** of these two contributions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_p &= f \cdot T_s + \frac{(1-f) \cdot T_s}{p} \\
    &= T_s \left[ f + \frac{1 - f}{p} \right] \\
    &= 1 \cdot \left[ f + \frac{1 - f}{p} \right] \quad \text{(since } T_s = 1 \text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6.** Substitute into the speedup definition $S = T_s / T_p$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{Amdahl}}(p) &= \frac{T_s}{T_p} = \frac{T_s}{T_s \left[ f + \dfrac{1-f}{p} \right]} \\
                     &= \frac{1}{f + \dfrac{1-f}{p}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7.** Take the limit as $p \to \infty$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{p \to \infty} S_{\text{Amdahl}}(p) &= \lim_{p \to \infty} \frac{1}{f + \dfrac{1-f}{p}} \\
&= \frac{1}{f + 0} = \frac{1}{f}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** The maximum achievable speedup, regardless of how many processors you have, is **bounded by $1/f$**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Gustafson's Law from First Principles

**Step 1.** Fix the **parallel runtime on p processors** as $T_p$. This is a design decision — the user wants the program to always take roughly the same wall-clock time, but with a *bigger* problem.

**Step 2.** Define:
- $s$ = serial runtime (constant, does not scale with problem size).
- $H$ = time spent on the parallel portion when running on $p$ processors.
- $p$ = number of processors.

$$T_p = s + H$$

**Step 3.** The **equivalent sequential runtime** (if we ran the same *enlarged* problem on a single processor) is the serial part $s$ plus the parallel part $p \cdot H$ (because one processor would have to do all the parallel work alone):

$$T_s^{\text{(scaled)}} = s + p \cdot H$$

**Step 4.** Compute the speedup:

$$S_{\text{Gustafson}} = \frac{T_s^{\text{(scaled)}}}{T_p} = \frac{s + p \cdot H}{s + H}$$

**Step 5.** Express $H$ and $s$ in terms of the **new** serial fraction $f'$ of the scaled problem. On the parallel machine with $p$ processors:

$$f' = \frac{s}{s + H} \quad \Rightarrow \quad s = f' (s + H), \quad H = (1 - f')(s + H)$$

**Step 6.** Substitute back:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{Gustafson}} &= \frac{s + pH}{s + H} \\
&= \frac{f'(s+H) + p(1-f')(s+H)}{s + H} \\
&= f' + p(1 - f') \\
&= p - f'(p - 1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7.** Interpretation:
- If $f' = 0$ (perfectly parallel scaled problem) → $S_{\text{Gustafson}} = p$ (linear).
- If $f' = 1$ (purely serial) → $S_{\text{Gustafson}} = 1$ (no benefit, expected).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Relationship Between Cost, Efficiency, and Speedup

Starting from definitions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(p) &= \frac{S(p)}{p} = \frac{T_s}{p \cdot T_p} \\
C(p) &= p \cdot T_p
\end{aligned}
$$

Multiply efficiency by cost:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(p) \cdot C(p) &= \frac{T_s}{p \cdot T_p} \cdot p \cdot T_p = T_s
\end{aligned}
$$

So the **fundamental identity** is:

$$E(p) \cdot C(p) = T_s$$

> This is a beautiful KTU-style result: **the product of efficiency and cost is invariant** and equals the sequential runtime. It is the workhorse behind cost-optimality proofs.

**Cost-Optimality Condition:** A parallel algorithm is cost-optimal if $C(p) = \Theta(T_s)$, i.e., $E(p) = \Theta(1)$ (a constant, not a vanishing fraction).

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Typical Problem)

**Problem:** A parallel program has a serial fraction $f = 0.05$ (5%). It is run on $p = 16$ processors. Compute: (i) Speedup, (ii) Efficiency, (iii) Maximum possible speedup, (iv) Speedup if $p$ is doubled to 32.

**Step (i):** Apply Amdahl's Law:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(16) &= \frac{1}{0.05 + \dfrac{1 - 0.05}{16}} \\
      &= \frac{1}{0.05 + \dfrac{0.95}{16}} \\
      &= \frac{1}{0.05 + 0.059375} \\
      &= \frac{1}{0.109375} \\
      &\approx 9.142
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (ii):** Compute Efficiency:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(16) &= \frac{S(16)}{p} = \frac{9.142}{16} \approx 0.5714 = 57.14\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (iii):** Maximum speedup:

$$S_{\max} = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{0.05} = 20$$

**Step (iv):** Speedup at $p = 32$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(32) &= \frac{1}{0.05 + \dfrac{0.95}{32}} = \frac{1}{0.05 + 0.0296875} = \frac{1}{0.0796875} \approx 12.55
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Observation:** Doubling the processors from 16 to 32 only improved speedup from 9.14 to 12.55 — a **diminishing return** of $\approx 3.4\times$ for a 2× hardware investment. This is the Amdahl effect.

---

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix: Amdahl vs. Gustafson

| Aspect | Amdahl's Law | Gustafson's Law |
|---|---|---|
| **Year / Author** | 1967, Gene Amdahl | 1988, John Gustafson & Edwin Barsis |
| **Workload Assumption** | **Fixed** problem size $n$ | **Scaled** problem size $n = f(p)$ |
| **Serial Fraction** | $f$ = constant | $f'$ = **decreases** with $p$ |
| **Time Constraint** | $T_p$ varies; $T_s$ fixed | $T_p$ fixed (constant wall-clock) |
| **Asymptotic Limit** | $S_{\max} = 1/f$ (bounded) | $S_{\max} \approx p$ (linear in $p$) |
| **Practical Use** | Strong-scaling studies | Weak-scaling studies |
| **Pessimistic / Optimistic** | Pessimistic | Optimistic |
| **Real-world Analogy** | "Fitting a fixed cake to bigger plates" | "Baking a bigger cake for the bigger plate" |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
