topics.title: Case Studies of Parallel Addition, Multiplication, Reduction, and Prefix Sum in Modern Computing Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST759 - PARALLEL ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Case Studies of Parallel Addition, Multiplication, Reduction, and Prefix Sum in Modern Computing Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition: Parallel Primitive Operations

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Parallel Algorithms (PECST759), a **Parallel Primitive** is defined as a fundamental, associative, data-parallel computation that can be efficiently distributed across multiple processing units to achieve logarithmic or sub-linear time complexity on associative data structures. The four case-study primitives under Module 2 are:

1. **Parallel Addition (Σ-Reduction)**: A parallel computation that computes the sum of *n* data elements distributed across *p* processors, leveraging the associativity of the addition operator to form a logarithmic-depth reduction tree.
2. **Parallel Multiplication (Π-Reduction)**: A parallel computation that computes the product of *n* data elements by cascading pairwise products through a binary tree, exploiting the associativity of multiplication.
3. **Parallel Reduction**: A generalized parallel operation that reduces *n* elements to a single aggregated result using any associative binary operator ⊕ (e.g., sum, max, min, XOR, bitwise-OR).
4. **Parallel Prefix Sum (Scan)**: A parallel computation that, given a sequence $[x_0, x_1, \ldots, x_{n-1}]$ and an associative operator ⊕, produces the output sequence $[y_0, y_1, \ldots, y_{n-1}]$ where $y_i = x_0 \oplus x_1 \oplus \cdots \oplus x_i$ (inclusive scan) or $y_i = x_0 \oplus x_1 \oplus \cdots \oplus x_{i-1}$ (exclusive scan).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: All four primitives must satisfy **work-optimality** ($T_1 = W = \Theta(n)$ or $\Theta(n^2)$ for dense multiplication) and **logarithmic span** ($T_\infty = O(\log n)$) to be considered efficient parallel algorithms suitable for the Work-Depth (W-D) model of analysis.

### 1.2 Real-World Analogies (Intuition)

| Primitive | Real-World Analogy | Key Insight |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------|
| Parallel Addition | A class of 1024 students each holding a number; the teacher pairs them up to add (1024→512→256→128→...→1) in just 10 rounds. | Pairing + halving = **logarithmic rounds** |
| Parallel Multiplication | Calculating compound interest on a loan over 12 months where each month's principal depends on the previous — partial products can be built in a tree. | Tree structure exploits **associativity** |
| Parallel Reduction | Tournament knockout: 64 teams play, 32 winners, 16 winners, ..., 1 champion. | One "champion" from N items in $\log_2 N$ rounds |
| Parallel Prefix Sum | Cumulative rainfall chart: to know total rainfall from Jan to July, you need running sums — but in parallel, you can build this chart in $\log_2 N$ steps. | **All-prefixes** problem — *not just one answer* |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Associativity Matters**: Without associativity, we cannot rearrange the order of operations. $(a+b)+c \neq a+(b+c)$ is FALSE in general (e.g., floating-point arithmetic, matrix multiplication, string concatenation). Associativity is the **fundamental enabler** of all four parallel primitives.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

The following constants and metrics form the foundation of parallel complexity analysis in KTU 2024:

- **Work (W or $T_1$)**: Total number of primitive operations performed by the algorithm across all processors.
- **Span (S or $T_\infty$)**: Length of the longest chain of dependent operations (critical-path length / depth).
- **Parallel Time with p processors ($T_p$)**: Execution time on *p* processors (bounded by Brent's Theorem).
- **Speedup ($S_p$)**: $S_p = T_1 / T_p$.
- **Efficiency ($E_p$)**: $E_p = S_p / p$.
- **Cost ($C_p$)**: $C_p = p \cdot T_p$. An algorithm is **cost-optimal** if $C_p = T_1$.

> [!TIP]
> **Cost-Optimal Rule of Thumb**: For primitive operations on *n* elements, the optimal number of processors is $p^* = W / S = n / \log n$. Using more processors yields $\text{speedup} < p$ (sub-linear); using fewer yields longer execution time.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Decomposition of Each Primitive

#### 2.1.1 Parallel Addition — Algorithm Logic
- **Step 1**: Distribute the *n* data elements across *p* processors in chunks of size $n/p$.
- **Step 2**: Each processor computes a **local sum** of its chunk sequentially — this is the *sequential base case*.
- **Step 3**: The *p* local sums are then combined in a **binary reduction tree** of depth $\log_2 p$.
- **Step 4**: The single global sum is obtained at the root of the tree.
- **Why it works**: Addition is associative and commutative → reordering produces correct result.

#### 2.1.2 Parallel Multiplication — Algorithm Logic
- **Step 1**: Similar local products computed in chunks.
- **Step 2**: Products are susceptible to **floating-point rounding** → order of operations affects bit-level result.
- **Step 3**: For **dense polynomial multiplication** (Karatsuba/FFT-based), the work is $O(n^2)$ or $O(n \log n)$ respectively, and the parallel depth is $O(\log n)$ for the reduction phase.
- **Why it works**: Multiplication over $\mathbb{R}$ is associative (with FP caveats) and over a ring/group it forms a valid reduction.

#### 2.1.3 Parallel Reduction — Generalization
- **Step 1**: Define a generic associative operator $\oplus$ with identity element *e* (e.g., 0 for +, 1 for ×, $-\infty$ for max, $+\infty$ for min).
- **Step 2**: Apply the same binary tree reduction, but with $\oplus$ in place of $+$.
- **Step 3**: Result is the aggregated value $x_0 \oplus x_1 \oplus \cdots \oplus x_{n-1}$.
- **Why it works**: The associativity of $\oplus$ guarantees correctness regardless of tree shape.

#### 2.1.4 Parallel Prefix Sum — Two Classic Algorithms

**Algorithm A — Hillis-Steele (Inclusive Scan)**
- **Step 1**: At step *k* (for $k = 1, 2, \ldots, \lceil \log_2 n \rceil$), every element at index $i$ adds the value at index $i - 2^{k-1}$ to itself (if $i \geq 2^{k-1}$).
- **Step 2**: After $\log_2 n$ steps, the array contains all prefix sums.
- **Step 3**: Work = $O(n \log n)$ (NOT work-optimal), Depth = $O(\log n)$.

**Algorithm B — Blelloch (Work-Efficient Exclusive Scan)**
- **Step 1**: **Up-sweep (reduce) phase**: Build a binary tree bottom-up, computing partial sums at each internal node.
- **Step 2**: Set the root to 0; this becomes the identity for the exclusive scan.
- **Step 3**: **Down-sweep phase**: Traverse the tree top-down, propagating prefix sums to leaves.
- **Step 4**: Work = $O(n)$, Depth = $O(\log n)$ — **work-optimal**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Primitive | Sequential Work $T_1 = W$ | Parallel Span $T_\infty$ | Parallel Time $T_p$ | Cost-Optimal $p^*$ | Speedup on $p^*$ | Applications |
|-----------|--------------------------|--------------------------|---------------------|--------------------|------------------|--------------|
| Parallel Addition (n elements) | $\Theta(n)$ | $\Theta(\log n)$ | $O(n/p + \log n)$ | $n / \log n$ | $\Theta(n / \log n)$ | Vector sums, dot products, histograms |
| Parallel Multiplication (n elements) | $\Theta(n)$ | $\Theta(\log n)$ | $O(n/p + \log n)$ | $n / \log n$ | $\Theta(n / \log n)$ | Polynomial evaluation, product of matrices |
| Dense Matrix Multiply (n × n) | $\Theta(n^3)$ | $\Theta(\log n)$ | $O(n^3/p + \log n)$ | $n^3 / \log n$ | $\Theta(n^3 / \log n)$ | Linear algebra, deep learning (GEMM) |
| Parallel Reduction (n elements) | $\Theta(n)$ | $\Theta(\log n)$ | $O(n/p + \log n)$ | $n / \log n$ | $\Theta(n / \log n)$ | ArgMax, ArgMin, sum, any, all |
| Prefix Sum (Hillis-Steele) | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | $\Theta(\log n)$ | $O((n \log n)/p + \log n)$ | $n$ | $\Theta(\log n)$ | Quick-sort partitioning, radix sort |
| Prefix Sum (Blelloch) | $\Theta(n)$ | $\Theta(\log n)$ | $O(n/p + \log n)$ | $n / \log n$ | $\Theta(n / \log n)$ | Stream compaction, sparse matrices |
| Brent's Theorem Bound | $T_p \leq T_1/p + T_\infty$ | — | — | — | — | Scheduling, load balancing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Notation Rule**: The Work-Depth model uses the asymptotic "big-O" of work, span, and parallel time. KTU examiners expect you to write all three values ($T_1$, $T_\infty$, $T_p$) explicitly in the answer sheet.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **GPU Computing (NVIDIA CUDA / Tensor Cores)**: Reduction and prefix sum are implemented in the CUB library and used internally by every deep-learning framework (PyTorch, TensorFlow) for softmax, layer normalization, and back-propagation.
- **Distributed Systems (Apache Spark, MapReduce)**: Tree-reduction RDD operations like `.reduce()` and `.aggregate()` use the same parallel reduction pattern across cluster nodes.
- **FPGA & Hardware Accelerators**: Custom ASICs (Google TPU, Apple Neural Engine) implement parallel prefix sum in hardware for the softmax and attention operations in transformers.
- **Database Query Engines**: Parallel aggregation in columnar databases (DuckDB, ClickHouse) uses work-efficient scan operations for SQL `SUM`, `AVG`, `COUNT`.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Parallel Time for Parallel Addition of *n* Elements Using *p* Processors

**Given**: A sequence $A[0 \ldots n-1]$ to be summed using *p* processors.

**Step 1 — Work Decomposition**: The total work (sequential time) is
$$W = T_1 = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} 1 = n \quad \text{(one addition per element)}$$

**Step 2 — Local Sequential Sum per Processor**: Each of the *p* processors handles a chunk of size $n/p$. The local sum takes
$$T_{\text{local}} = n/p \quad \text{operations}$$

**Step 3 — Cross-Processor Reduction**: After local sums, we have *p* partial sums. The reduction of *p* values into one final sum requires a binary tree of depth
$$T_{\text{tree}} = \log_2 p$$

**Step 4 — Total Parallel Time**: The two phases execute sequentially (second depends on first), so
$$T_p = T_{\text{local}} + T_{\text{tree}} = n/p + \log_2 p$$

**Step 5 — Substituting $p = n / \log n$ (Cost-Optimal Choice)**:
$$T_{p^*} = \frac{n}{n / \log n} + \log_2 \left( \frac{n}{\log n} \right) = \log n + \log_2 n - \log_2 \log n = O(\log n)$$

**Step 6 — Cost Calculation**:
$$C_{p^*} = p^* \cdot T_{p^*} = \frac{n}{\log n} \cdot O(\log n) = O(n) = T_1$$

This proves the algorithm is **cost-optimal** on $p^* = n / \log n$ processors. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Brent's Theorem Bound for Work-Depth Algorithms

**Theorem Statement**: For any Work-Depth algorithm with work $W$ and depth $D$, the running time on $p$ processors is bounded by
$$T_p \leq \frac{W}{p} + D$$

**Step-by-Step Proof Sketch**:

**Step 1 — Conceptualization**: The Work-Depth model schedules the algorithm as a DAG of *W* nodes with critical path length *D*.

**Step 2 — Greedy Schedule Simulation**: Run the algorithm on *p* processors. At each time step, at most *p* nodes can be ready (those whose predecessors have all completed). The number of completed nodes after step *t* is at most $p \cdot t$.

**Step 3 — Lower Bound on Steps**: To complete all *W* nodes, we need at least $\lceil W / p \rceil$ steps, so
$$T_p \geq \left\lceil \frac{W}{p} \right\rceil$$

**Step 4 — Upper Bound from Critical Path**: No scheduler can complete the work faster than the critical path, so
$$T_p \geq D$$

**Step 5 — Brent's Combined Bound**: The actual running time is dominated by the larger of the two constraints, giving
$$T_p \leq \frac{W - D}{p} + D \leq \frac{W}{p} + D$$

**Step 6 — Tightness**: The bound is tight — for any $p$, $W$, $D$ satisfying $1 \leq p \leq W$, an algorithm exists achieving $T_p = \lceil W/p \rceil + D$. $\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Hillis-Steele Prefix Sum — Work and Span

**Step 1 — Initialization**: Input array $X[0 \ldots n-1]$; assume $n = 2^k$ for simplicity.

**Step 2 — Iteration $k = 1$**: For all $i \geq 1$,
$$X[i] \leftarrow X[i] + X[i-1]$$
This produces 1-step prefix sums. **Work per iteration**: $n - 1 \approx n$.

**Step 3 — Iteration $k = 2$**: For all $i \geq 2$,
$$X[i] \leftarrow X[i] + X[i-2]$$
This produces 2-step prefix sums. **Work per iteration**: $\approx n$.

**Step 4 — General Iteration $k$**: For all $i \geq 2^{k-1}$,
$$X[i] \leftarrow X[i] + X[i - 2^{k-1}]$$
**Work per iteration**: $\approx n$.

**Step 5 — Total Iterations**: After $k = \log_2 n$ iterations, the array contains the full prefix sums.

**Step 6 — Total Work**:
$$W = \sum_{k=1}^{\log_2 n} n = n \cdot \log_2 n = \Theta(n \log n)$$

**Step 7 — Span (Critical Path Length)**:
$$D = \log_2 n \quad \text{(each iteration takes constant time, } \log_2 n \text{ iterations)}$$

**Step 8 — Parallel Time on p Processors** (Brent's Theorem):
$$T_p \leq \frac{n \log n}{p} + \log n$$

**Step 9 — Cost-Optimality Check**: $C_p = p \cdot T_p = n \log n + p \log n$. This is $\Theta(n \log n)$, which exceeds $T_1 = \Theta(n \log n)$ only by a constant. So Hillis-Steele is asymptotically cost-optimal on $p \leq n$ processors, but the constant is large. Blelloch is preferred for cache efficiency. $\blacksquare$

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Modern GPU Reduction Case Study (NVIDIA CUB / Thrust)

**Architecture Context**: Modern NVIDIA GPUs (e.g., A100) have **108 SMs (Streaming Multiprocessors)**, each with **128 FP32 cores** → total **13,824 cores**. The warp size is **32 threads**; threads in a warp execute in **SIMT (Single-Instruction Multiple-Thread)** lockstep.

**Step 1 — Thread-Block Level Reduction (Shuffle-Based)**: Within a warp (32 threads), the reduction uses warp-shuffle instructions (`__shfl_down_sync`). For 32 elements:
$$T_{\text{warp}} = 5 \quad \text{shuffle steps} = \log_2 32$$

**Step 2 — Block-Level Reduction**: For a block of *B* threads (e.g., 256), the algorithm performs warp-level reductions, then writes warp results to shared memory, then one warp reduces the final 32 values.
$$T_{\text{block}} = \log_2 B = \log_2 256 = 8 \quad \text{steps}$$

**Step 3 — Grid-Level Reduction (Two-Pass)**: Each of the 108 SMs produces one partial sum → 108 values → second kernel reduces → final 1 value.
$$T_{\text{grid}} = 2 \cdot \log_2 B = 16 \quad \text{steps (typically)}$$

**Step 4 — Effective Span**: For $n = 2^{30}$ elements on an A100 GPU,
$$T_\infty = \log_2 n = 30 \quad \text{steps}$$

**Step 5 — Effective Parallel Time** (Brent's with $p = 13{,}824$):
$$T_p = \frac{n}{p} + \log_2 n = \frac{2^{30}}{13{,}824} + 30 \approx 77{,}820 + 30 = 77{,}850 \quad \text{cycles}$$

**Step 6 — Memory-Bandwidth Bound**: For FP32, the actual bottleneck is memory bandwidth, not compute. On A100, the HBM3 bandwidth is **2 TB/s**, so the lower bound is
$$T_{\text{mem}} = \frac{n \cdot 4 \text{ bytes}}{2 \cdot 10^{12} \text{ bytes/s}} = \frac{2^{30} \cdot 4}{2 \cdot 10^{12}} \approx 2.15 \text{ ms}$$

The GPU reduction achieves **~80% of memory bandwidth peak** in optimized implementations (CUB `DeviceReduce::Sum`). $\blacksquare$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Parallel Reduction Tree — 8 Elements

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0["X0"]:::leaf
    A1["X1"]:::leaf
    A2["X2"]:::leaf
    A3["X3"]:::leaf
    A4["X4"]:::leaf
    A5["X5"]:::leaf
    A6["X6"]:::leaf
    A7["X7"]:::leaf
    B0["P0+P1"]:::level1
    B1["P2+P3"]:::level1
    B2["P4+P5"]:::level1
    B3["P6+P7"]:::level1
    C0["P0to3"]:::level2
    C1["P4to7"]:::level2
    D0["P0to7"]:::root
    A0 --> B0
    A1 --> B0
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    A4 --> B2
    A5 --> B2
    A6 --> B3
    A7 --> B3
    B0 --> C0
    B1 --> C0
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    C0 --> D0
    C1 --> D0
    classDef leaf fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2,color:#000
    classDef level1 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,color:#000
    classDef level2 fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#7B1FA2,color:#000
    classDef root fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#388E3C,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram**: 8 input leaves combine in 3 levels ($\log_2 8 = 3$). All leaves at the same level execute in parallel (synchronously). Edges denote data dependency.

### 4.2 Parallel Multiplication Tree — Dense 4×4 Block

```mermaid
graph TD
    M00["a00*b00"]:::mul
    M01["a01*b10"]:::mul
    M10["a10*b01"]:::mul
    M11["a11*b11"]:::mul
    M02["a02*b20"]:::mul
    M12["a12*b21"]:::mul
    M22["a22*b22"]:::mul
    M03["a03*b30"]:::mul
    M13["a13*b31"]:::mul
    M23["a23*b32"]:::mul
    M33["a33*b33"]:::mul
    R00["c00"]:::root
    R11["c11"]:::root
    R22["c22"]:::root
    R33["c33"]:::root
    M00 --> R00
    M01 --> R00
    M10 --> R00
    M11 --> R00
    M11 --> R11
    M02 --> R11
    M12 --> R11
    M22 --> R22
    M12 --> R22
    M22 --> R22
    M03 --> R33
    M13 --> R33
    M23 --> R33
    M33 --> R33
    classDef mul fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#0277BD,color:#000
    classDef root fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram**: Each output element $c_{ij}$ of a matrix product is the sum of *n* products. The products can be computed in parallel, and the sum forms a reduction tree of depth $\log_2 n$.

### 4.3 Hillis-Steele Prefix Sum — Step-by-Step Evolution

```mermaid
graph LR
    S0["Step 0: X0 X1 X2 X3 X4 X5 X6 X7"]:::step
    S1["Step 1: X0 X0+X1 X1+X2 X2+X3 X3+X4 X4+X5 X5+X6 X6+X7"]:::step
    S2["Step 2: X0 X0+X1 X0to2 X1to3 X2to4 X3to5 X4to6 X5to7"]:::step
    S3["Step 3: X0 X0+X1 X0to2 X0to3 X0to4 X1to5 X2to6 X3to7"]:::step
    S4["Step 4: X0 X0+X1 X0to2 X0to3 X0to4 X0to5 X0to6 X0to7"]:::root
    S0 --> S1
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4
    classDef step fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#FF8F00,color:#000
    classDef root fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram**: At each step *k*, element $i$ receives the value from element $i - 2^{k-1}$. After $\log_2 n = 3$ steps, all prefix sums are computed (note: shows 3 steps for 8 elements; some elements need one more pass for full correctness — implementation-dependent).

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — GPU Parallel Reduction Pipeline

```mermaid
graph TD
    HOST["Host CPU Application"]:::host
    H2D["cudaMemcpy H to D"]:::transfer
    K1["Kernel 1: Per-Block Reduction (108 SMs)"]:::kernel
    SHM["Shared Memory Aggregation"]:::shm
    WSH["Warp Shuffle Reduction"]:::shm
    K2["Kernel 2: Final Reduction (1 SM)"]:::kernel
    D2H["cudaMemcpy D to H"]:::transfer
    RES["Host Result Buffer"]:::host
    HOST --> H2D
    H2D --> K1
    K1 --> SHM
    SHM --> WSH
    WSH --> K2
    K2 --> D2H
    D2H --> RES
    classDef host fill:#E8EAF6,stroke:#283593,color:#000
    classDef transfer fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#C2185B,color:#000
    classDef kernel fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064,color:#000
    classDef shm fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram**: This shows the **two-pass reduction strategy** used in NVIDIA's CUB library. Pass 1 reduces within each thread-block using shared memory and warp shuffles; Pass 2 reduces the 108 partial sums (one per SM) to a single value.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q1. Define parallel reduction. State its work and span for *n* elements using an associative operator. Why is associativity a necessary condition?**
**[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark)**: Parallel reduction is the process of combining *n* data elements into a single aggregated result using an associative binary operator $\oplus$ by repeatedly applying it in pairs across multiple processors.
- **Work and Span (1 mark)**: For *n* elements, the work $T_1 = \Theta(n)$ (each element participates in at least one operation) and the span $T_\infty = \Theta(\log n)$ (binary tree of depth $\log_2 n$).
- **Associativity Justification (1 mark)**: Associativity ensures that $(a \oplus b) \oplus c = a \oplus (b \oplus c)$, so the result is independent of the order in which pairs are combined. Without associativity, different parallel schedules would yield different results, making the algorithm non-deterministic and incorrect.

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q2. Distinguish between the Hillis-Steele and Blelloch prefix-sum algorithms in terms of work, span, and stability. Why is Blelloch preferred for GPU implementations?**
**[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Hillis-Steele (1 mark)**: Work = $\Theta(n \log n)$, Span = $\Theta(\log n)$. Each iteration does $\sim n$ work over $\log n$ rounds, making it *not work-optimal* but simple and stable.
- **Blelloch (1 mark)**: Work = $\Theta(n)$, Span = $2 \log n$ (up-sweep + down-sweep). It is *work-optimal* but requires more complex bookkeeping (two phases, shared memory, or scratch space).
- **GPU Preference (1 mark)**: Blelloch is preferred because the work is closer to optimal ($\Theta(n)$ vs. $\Theta(n \log n)$), and on memory-bandwidth-bound GPU architectures, the lower work means fewer global memory accesses and better performance. Modern CUB `DeviceScan` uses Blelloch.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 2, KTU 2024 Scheme Model Paper]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Parallel Reduction Case Study on Multi-Core CPU*

**(a)** Explain Brent's Theorem. Using it, derive the parallel time $T_p$ for summing *n* numbers using *p* processors, assuming a binary tree reduction strategy. Identify the cost-optimal number of processors and the corresponding parallel time. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Consider a multi-core CPU with **8 cores**, each capable of issuing **2 SIMD operations per cycle** on **16-wide vectors** (so 16 × 8 × 2 = 256 parallel operations per cycle). For a reduction of **$n = 2^{20}$ FP64 elements**, calculate: (i) the sequential time in cycles, (ii) the parallel time using Brent's bound, (iii) the achieved speedup, and (iv) the efficiency. Comment on whether this configuration is cost-optimal. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution (14 marks):**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Brent's Theorem Statement (2 marks)**: For any Work-Depth algorithm with work $W$ and depth $D$, the running time on *p* processors satisfies
$$T_p \leq \frac{W}{p} + D$$

2. **Derivation of $T_p$ for Sum (3 marks)**: Total work $W = n$ (one addition per element). Tree reduction depth $D = \log_2 p$. Applying Brent's theorem:
$$T_p = \frac{n}{p} + \log_2 p$$

3. **Cost-Optimal Processors (2 marks)**: Cost = $C_p = p \cdot T_p = n + p \log_2 p$. For cost-optimality, $C_p = T_1 = n$, so $p \log_2 p$ must be small. Differentiating $f(p) = p \log_2 p$ and setting to equilibrium with sequential chunk $n/p$:
$$p^* = \frac{n}{\log_2 n} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_{p^*} = O(\log n)$$

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

1. **Sequential Time (1 mark)**: $T_1 = n = 2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576$ cycles (one FP64 add per element, sequential).

2. **Parallel Time with Brent's Bound (2 marks)**: With $p = 256$ parallel lanes (effective processors),
$$T_p = \frac{2^{20}}{256} + \log_2 256 = 4096 + 8 = 4104 \quad \text{cycles}$$

3. **Speedup (1 mark)**:
$$S_p = \frac{T_1}{T_p} = \frac{1{,}048{,}576}{4104} \approx 255.5$$

4. **Efficiency (1 mark)**:
$$E_p = \frac{S_p}{p} = \frac{255.5}{256} \approx 0.998 \approx 99.8\%$$

5. **Cost-Optimality Comment (2 marks)**: The cost-optimal processor count is $p^* = n / \log_2 n = 2^{20} / 20 \approx 52{,}429$. Since $p = 256 \ll p^*$, the system is **operating below** the cost-optimal processor count. It is cost-optimal in the sense that all available parallelism is being used productively, but the algorithm could still benefit from more processors. The high efficiency ($\sim$100%) shows no wasted work — this is **over-provisioned but efficient** (no contention, no idle cores). It is asymptotically cost-optimal because $C_p = 256 \times 4104 = 1{,}050{,}624 \approx T_1$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Parallel Prefix Sum Case Study on Modern GPU*

**(a)** Describe the Blelloch work-efficient parallel prefix-sum algorithm with the up-sweep and down-sweep phases. Show the array state after each phase for $n = 8$ elements initialized to $[3, 1, 7, 0, 4, 1, 6, 3]$. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A modern GPU implements the Blelloch scan using **warp-shuffle instructions**. For a single warp (32 threads) performing a Kogge-Stone style parallel scan on integers, derive: (i) the work per thread, (ii) the depth in shuffle steps, and (iii) the parallel time. Compare with the Hillis-Steele algorithm and state which is preferred for warp-level scan. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution (14 marks):**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Blelloch Algorithm Description (2 marks)**: 
   - **Up-sweep (reduce) phase**: Build a binary tree bottom-up; each internal node stores the sum of its two children. Depth = $\log_2 n$.
   - **Down-sweep phase**: Set the root to 0 (identity for exclusive scan). For each internal node, set the right child to the parent's value, and the left child to the parent's previous value minus the right child's value. Depth = $\log_2 n$.

2. **Initial Array (1 mark)**: $[3, 1, 7, 0, 4, 1, 6, 3]$

3. **After Up-sweep (2 marks)**: Internal node values for tree of 8 leaves:
   - Level 1 (pairs): $[4, 7, 5, 9]$
   - Level 2 (quads): $[11, 14]$
   - Level 3 (octet, root): $[25]$
   - Final tree-aggregated array state: leaves $[3,1,7,0,4,1,6,3]$, internal nodes (in-order) $[4, 7, 5, 9, 11, 14, 25]$ (last is the total).

4. **After Down-sweep (Exclusive Scan) (2 marks)**: Setting root to 0 and propagating prefix sums:
   - Result (exclusive): $[0, 3, 4, 11, 11, 15, 16, 22]$
   - Verification: inclusive scan would be $[3, 4, 11, 11, 15, 16, 22, 25]$ ✓

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

1. **Kogge-Stone on a Warp (1 mark)**: A Kogge-Stone adder is a parallel prefix network with $\log_2 n$ stages, each performing $n/2$ additions with stride $2^{k-1}$. For $n = 32$ threads in a warp:

2. **Work per Thread (2 marks)**: Each thread performs exactly $\log_2 32 = 5$ additions (one per stage), each of which is a single `__shfl_up_sync` followed by an `add`. So work per thread = **5 cycles** (or 5 instructions).

3. **Depth in Shuffle Steps (2 marks)**: The 5 stages execute **sequentially** in the warp (each warp-shuffle is synchronous across the warp). So depth = **5 cycles**.

4. **Parallel Time (1 mark)**: Since all 32 threads execute in SIMT lockstep, parallel time = 5 cycles (same as depth, since the warp IS the parallelism unit).

5. **Comparison with Hillis-Steele (1 mark)**: 
   - Hillis-Steele: Work = $32 \times 5 = 160$ operations, Depth = 5.
   - Kogge-Stone (warp-shuffle): Work = 32 × 5 = 160 operations, Depth = 5.
   - **Both are equivalent in work and depth** for $n=32$! This is because for $n = \text{warp size}$, the work overhead of Hillis-Steele equals the Kogge-Stone overhead. However, for $n > 32$ (multi-warp), Hillis-Steele does $n \log n$ work (sub-optimal) while Kogge-Stone (extended) can be implemented with Brent-scheduled work-optimal. **Kogge-Stone is preferred** for warp-level scan due to its symmetry and ability to use `__shfl_up_sync` directly.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose 1-3 Marks Each)**:
> 1. **Confusing $T_1$ with $T_p$**: $T_1$ is the SEQUENTIAL time (work), $T_p$ is the parallel time. Many students write $T_1 = n/p$ — **WRONG**. $T_1 = n$.
> 2. **Forgetting the identity element**: When writing the down-sweep of Blelloch, students forget to set the root to 0. This makes the scan non-exclusive. **Lose 1 mark**.
> 3. **Missing the $D$ term in Brent's bound**: A common error is writing $T_p = W/p$ without the $D$ (depth) additive term. The full bound is $W/p + D$.
> 4. **Confusing speedup and efficiency**: Speedup is dimensionless and can exceed 1; efficiency is a ratio in $[0, 1]$ for standard (non-super-linear) parallel algorithms.
> 5. **Not stating associativity explicitly**: KTU examiners require you to mention the operator's associativity property in any reduction/scan answer — failing to do so costs a mark.
> 6. **Writing Hillis-Steele work as $O(n)$**: It is $O(n \log n)$. Many students confuse it with Blelloch.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist** — Read this 30 minutes before the exam.

- **Four primitives** in Module 2: Parallel Addition, Multiplication, Reduction, Prefix Sum (Scan).
- **All four are work-optimal** with $W = \Theta(n)$ and span $D = \Theta(\log n)$ — except dense multiplication $W = \Theta(n^3)$.
- **Cost-optimal processor count**: $p^* = W / D$ (general rule). For sums/reductions: $p^* = n / \log n$.
- **Brent's Theorem**: $T_p = W/p + D$. Always invoke this when asked to find parallel time.
- **Hillis-Steele scan**: $W = \Theta(n \log n)$, $D = \Theta(\log n)$. Not work-optimal but simple.
- **Blelloch scan**: $W = \Theta(n)$, $D = 2\log n$. Work-optimal; needs up-sweep + down-sweep.
- **GPU reduction architecture**: Two-pass — per-block reduction in shared memory, then cross-block reduction.
- **Modern GPU examples**: NVIDIA CUB (`DeviceReduce::Sum`, `DeviceScan::InclusiveScan`), AMD rocPRIM, Intel DPC++ `parallel_reduce`.
- **Associativity is non-negotiable**: All four primitives require an associative operator.
- **Floating-point caveat**: Multiplication is not strictly associative in FP arithmetic — order matters! (Exam question possibility).
- **Speedup ceiling**: For sums on $p = n/\log n$ processors, $S_p = \Theta(n/\log n)$.
- **Efficiency at cost-optimum**: $E_{p^*} = 100\%$ (asymptotically).
- **Example numerical values to memorize**:
  - Warp size = **32** (NVIDIA, AMD)
  - Log₂(32) = **5** (one warp reduction = 5 shuffle steps)
  - A100: **108 SMs**, **6,912 FP32 cores** (108 × 64)
  - A100 HBM3 bandwidth: **2 TB/s** = $2 \times 10^{12}$ bytes/s
  - Kogge-Stone adder depth = $\log_2 n$
- **Key KTU terms to use**: "associative operator", "binary tree reduction", "up-sweep / down-sweep", "work-depth model", "cost-optimal", "Brent's theorem", "span / depth / critical path".

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parallel Sorting Algorithms: Merge Sort, Quicksort, Bitonic Merge Sort, and Comparison
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST759 - PARALLEL ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Parallel Sorting Algorithms: Merge Sort, Quicksort, Bitonic Merge Sort, and Comparison

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Parallel Sorting: The Formal Definition

**Parallel Sorting** refers to the class of sorting algorithms that exploit **concurrency** by distributing the workload of ordering a sequence of $n$ elements across multiple processing elements ($p$ processors) operating simultaneously, with the primary goal of minimizing the **wall-clock parallel runtime** $T_p$ rather than the total work performed.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECST759)**, we model parallelism on an abstract **PRAM (Parallel Random Access Machine)** — specifically the **CREW (Concurrent Read, Exclusive Write)** model — unless stated otherwise. The central performance objective is captured by two metrics:

- **Parallel Time Complexity** : $T_p(n)$  
- **Speedup** : $S_p = \frac{T_1}{T_p}$  

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Sorting is treated as a *dense, deterministic, comparison-based* problem. The three reference algorithms — **Parallel Merge Sort**, **Parallel Quicksort**, and **Bitonic Merge Sort** — are *not* interchangeable; each maps to a different parallel architecture (PRAM vs. Sorting Network vs. Distributed Memory).

---

## 1.2 The Three Algorithms at a Glance

### (a) Parallel Merge Sort
A **divide-and-conquer** strategy lifted to the parallel domain. The input is recursively split into two halves that are sorted **independently and concurrently**, after which the two sorted halves are merged in parallel.

- **Architecture Fit:** Shared-memory PRAM (CREW).
- **Recurrence Shape:** $T(n) = T(n/2) + T_{\text{merge}}(n)$.

### (b) Parallel Quicksort
A **partition-based** strategy. A *pivot* is selected and broadcast; all processors concurrently classify their local elements into "less than" and "greater than" buckets; sub-problems are then solved in parallel.

- **Architecture Fit:** Shared-memory with low-contention partitioning.
- **Recurrence Shape:** $T(n) = T(n/2) + T_{\text{partition}}(n)$.

### (c) Bitonic Merge Sort
A **comparison-network** strategy in which a *bitonic sequence* (a monotonic-then-monotonic or cyclic-monotonic sequence) is repeatedly halved, swapped, and merged using a butterfly network of comparators. The network depth is fixed and independent of the input distribution.

- **Architecture Fit:** Fixed-connection network / Sorting network.
- **Recurrence Shape:** $T(n) = T(n/2) + \log_2 n$ comparator stages.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Exam-Result Office

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — Three Exam-Result Offices**
> 
> Imagine 8,000 answer sheets must be ranked by score:
> 
> - **Merge Sort** is like *two officers each handling 4,000 papers independently, then sitting together to merge the two sorted lists*. Parallelism is in the *divide* step.
> - **Quicksort** is like *choosing a "median" score first; all officers then instantly stamp each paper "Above" or "Below" in parallel, and recurse on the two piles*. Parallelism is in the *partition* step.
> - **Bitonic Sort** is like a *pre-wired assembly line with 8,000 fixed comparison stations*, where paper $i$ and paper $j$ are compared at a station whose position is determined solely by the indices $i$ and $j$ — *no scheduler, no decisions, pure wiring*. Parallelism is in the *wiring topology*.

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Board Exams

| Reason | Board-Exam Weight |
|---|---|
| Direct 14-mark questions on **Bitonic Sort** appear almost every semester | **High** |
| Algorithm **comparison** questions test syllabus integration | **High** |
| Recurrence + complexity derivations appear in Part A | **Medium** |
| Network diagram of 8-element bitonic sort is a guaranteed question | **Very High** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Metric You Must Memorize:** For $n$ elements, **Bitonic Sort uses exactly** $\frac{\log_2 n \cdot (\log_2 n + 1)}{2}$ **comparator stages** and **$\frac{n \log_2 n \cdot (\log_2 n + 1)}{4}$ comparators**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Parallel Merge Sort — The Operational Logic

**Step 1 — Recursive Divide (Parallel):**  
The array of $n$ elements is split into two halves of $n/2$ each. The recursion bottoms out when $n=1$.

**Step 2 — Parallel Merge (CREW PRAM):**  
Two sorted arrays $A$ and $B$ of sizes $m$ and $n$ are merged using the **Borodin–Hopcroft parallel merging algorithm**:
1. Find the **rank of every element of $A$ in $B$** in $O(\log n)$ time using binary search (executed *in parallel* for every element of $A$).
2. Compute the **final position** of each element using the *index-sum* trick.
3. Write the elements to the output array at the computed positions.

**Step 3 — Concatenation:**  
The merged result is now sorted globally.

### Key Properties
- **Work-optimal** when $p = O(n / \log n)$.
- **Stable** if the underlying merge is stable.
- **Deterministic** — no randomized pivot, unlike Quicksort.

---

## 2.2 Parallel Quicksort — The Operational Logic

**Step 1 — Pivot Selection:**  
A single processor (or a deterministic median-finder) picks a pivot $x$.

**Step 2 — Broadcast (CREW):**  
$x$ is broadcast to all processors in $O(\log p)$ time using a binary-tree broadcast.

**Step 3 — Parallel Partition:**  
Each processor inspects its local segment and routes elements into one of two buckets. This can be done in $O(n/p + \log p)$ time using a parallel prefix-sum (scan) operation.

**Step 4 — Recurse:**  
The two sub-problems of expected size $n/2$ are solved independently in parallel.

### Key Properties
- **Average case** is excellent: $O(\log n)$ parallel time with $n$ processors.
- **Worst case** is disastrous: $O(n)$ parallel time on a sorted input with bad pivot.
- **Not stable** in its standard form.
- **In-place** partitioning is possible (low extra memory).

---

## 2.3 Bitonic Merge Sort — The Operational Logic

This is the **most-tested** algorithm in this module. We define three key objects:

### Definition 1 — Bitonic Sequence
A sequence is **bitonic** if it is the cyclic rotation of a sequence that first *strictly increases* and then *strictly decreases*. Equivalently: a sequence $a_0, a_1, \dots, a_{n-1}$ is bitonic if there exists an index $i$ such that

$$a_0 \leq a_1 \leq \cdots \leq a_i \geq a_{i+1} \geq \cdots \geq a_{n-1}$$

### Definition 2 — Bitonic Merge
Given a bitonic sequence of length $n = 2^k$, a **bitonic merge** produces a sorted sequence by:
1. Comparing-and-swapping element $a_i$ with element $a_{i + n/2}$ for all $0 \leq i < n/2$.
2. Recursively applying the merge to both halves.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Zero-One Principle (used to prove correctness):** A comparison network correctly sorts all inputs **iff** it correctly sorts all $2^n$ binary inputs (sequences of 0s and 1s). This is the standard KTU proof technique.

### Definition 3 — Bitonic Sort
To sort $n = 2^k$ arbitrary elements, the algorithm recursively constructs bitonic sequences of length $2, 4, 8, \dots, n$ and merges them. The number of stages is:

$$\text{Stages}(n) = \frac{\log_2 n \cdot (\log_2 n + 1)}{2}$$

---

## 2.4 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the *cheat-sheet* you should be able to reproduce from memory in the examination hall. Use $\mid$ (rendered as `\mid`) wherever you would normally write an absolute-value bar to keep the markdown table intact.

| Algorithm | Parallel Time $T_p(n)$ | Processors $p$ | Work $W = p \cdot T_p$ | Work-Optimal? | Stability | Determinism |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Sequential Merge Sort** | $O(n \log n)$ | $1$ | $O(n \log n)$ | Yes (baseline) | Stable | Deterministic |
| **Parallel Merge Sort (CREW PRAM)** | $O(\log^2 n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n \log^2 n)$ | No | Stable | Deterministic |
| **Work-Optimal Parallel Merge Sort** | $O(\log n)$ | $O(n/\log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | **Yes** | Stable | Deterministic |
| **Parallel Quicksort (Average)** | $O(\log n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | **Yes** (avg) | Unstable | Randomized |
| **Parallel Quicksort (Worst)** | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n^2)$ | No | Unstable | Randomized |
| **Bitonic Sort** | $O(\log^2 n)$ | $O(n \log^2 n)$ | $O(n \log^3 n)$ | No | Unstable | Deterministic |
| **Bitonic Sort (Work-Optimal variant)** | $O(\log^2 n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n \log^2 n)$ | No (still sub-optimal) | Unstable | Deterministic |

### Speedup and Efficiency Formulas

$$S_p = \frac{T_{\text{seq}}}{T_p}, \qquad E_p = \frac{S_p}{p} = \frac{T_{\text{seq}}}{p \cdot T_p}$$

For **Bitonic Sort** with $n$ elements:

$$\text{Comparators} = \frac{n \log_2 n \cdot (\log_2 n + 1)}{4}, \qquad \text{Stages} = \frac{\log_2 n \cdot (\log_2 n + 1)}{2}$$

For **Parallel Merge Sort** with $p = n$:

$$T_p(n) = 2T_p(n/2) + T_{\text{merge}}(n) = O(\log^2 n)$$

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

| Algorithm | Where It Is Used in Industry |
|---|---|
| **Parallel Merge Sort** | Database engine sort operators (PostgreSQL, Spark `sortByKey`), external-memory sorts in distributed data centers. |
| **Parallel Quicksort** | In-memory sorts in Java's `Arrays.parallelSort` (uses a dual-pivot parallel quicksort under the hood). |
| **Bitonic Sort** | GPU sorting (since it maps to a fixed butterfly network with no branch divergence), FPGA-based hardware sorters, network-on-chip packet prioritization. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Parallel Time of Merge Sort (CREW PRAM)

We need to solve the recurrence:

$$T(n) = T\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + T_{\text{merge}}(n)$$

where $T_{\text{merge}}(n)$ is the parallel time to merge two sorted arrays of total size $n$.

**Step 1 — The Parallel Merge Step (Borodin–Hopcroft Algorithm).**  
Given two sorted arrays $A$ (size $m$) and $B$ (size $n$), with $m + n = N$:

- We assign **one processor to each element of $A$** (i.e., $m$ processors).
- Each processor performs a **binary search** of its element into $B$, taking $O(\log n)$ time.
- The rank output tells us the *position of the element in the merged output*.
- All writes are made in **parallel** (the merged positions are guaranteed disjoint → no exclusive-write violation).

Therefore:

$$T_{\text{merge}}(N) = O(\log N)$$

**Step 2 — Substituting Back into the Recurrence.**

$$T(n) = T\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + O(\log n)$$

**Step 3 — Solving the Recurrence by Expansion.**  
Let $n = 2^k$, so $k = \log_2 n$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(2^k) &= T(2^{k-1}) + \log_2(2^k) \\
&= T(2^{k-1}) + k \\
&= T(2^{k-2}) + (k-1) + k \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
&= T(1) + \sum_{i=1}^{k} i \\
&= 1 + \frac{k(k+1)}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Asymptotic Simplification.**  
Substituting $k = \log_2 n$:

$$T(n) = O\left(\frac{\log_2 n \cdot (\log_2 n + 1)}{2}\right) = O(\log^2 n)$$

This matches the row in the formula sheet.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $O(\log^2 n)$ and not $O(\log n)$?** Because the *merge* step itself takes $O(\log n)$ time, and the recursion has $\log n$ levels — we get a *product*, not a sum. To get $O(\log n)$ parallel time we must reduce the number of processors to $n / \log n$ (the work-optimal variant in the formula table).

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Average Parallel Time of Quicksort

**Step 1 — Expected Partition Sizes.**  
With a uniform-random pivot, the expected size of each sub-problem is $n/2$. The depth of the recursion tree is therefore $O(\log n)$ in expectation.

**Step 2 — Cost of Each Level.**  
At recursion level $d$, there are $2^d$ sub-problems of total size $n$. The cost to partition them in parallel (with $n$ processors) is $O(1)$ per level when each processor handles a single element of a sub-problem.

**Step 3 — Summing the Levels.**

$$T(n) = \sum_{d=0}^{\log_2 n} O(1) = O(\log n)$$

**Step 4 — Worst-Case Analysis.**  
If the pivot is always the minimum (or maximum), one sub-problem has size $n-1$ and the other has size $0$. The recursion depth becomes $n$:

$$T_{\text{worst}}(n) = T(n-1) + O(1) = O(n)$$

This is why the algorithm is paired with **randomized pivot selection** in practice.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Number of Comparator Stages in Bitonic Sort

**Claim:** To sort $n = 2^k$ elements, Bitonic Sort requires exactly $\dfrac{k(k+1)}{2}$ comparator stages.

**Proof by Induction on $k$.**

*Base Case ($k=1$, $n=2$):*  
Two elements are sorted with 1 stage and 1 comparator. Indeed, $\frac{1 \cdot 2}{2} = 1$. ✓

*Inductive Step:*  
Assume the claim holds for $k-1$, i.e., for $n/2$ elements. For $n$ elements:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Stages}(n) &= \text{Stages}(n/2) + \underbrace{\text{Stages to build bitonic seq. of length } n}_{\text{Step A}} + \underbrace{\text{Bitonic merge stage}}_{\text{Step B}}
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Step A** is the call to Bitonic Sort of half-size on pairs to produce a bitonic sequence of length $n$. This takes $\text{Stages}(n/2)$ time.
- **Step B** is a *single* bitonic merge stage of length $n$, which adds exactly $k$ stages (one for each level of the merge recursion: $n \to n/2 \to n/4 \to \cdots \to 1$).

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Stages}(n) &= \text{Stages}(n/2) + k \\
&= \frac{(k-1)k}{2} + k \\
&= \frac{k(k-1) + 2k}{2} \\
&= \frac{k^2 + k}{2} = \frac{k(k+1)}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

This completes the proof. $\blacksquare$

**Numerical Verification for $n=8$ ($k=3$):**

$$\text{Stages} = \frac{3 \cdot 4}{2} = 6, \qquad \text{Comparators} = \frac{8 \cdot 3 \cdot 4}{4} = 24$$

---

## 3.4 The Bitonic Merge Correctness (Zero-One Principle)

We need to show that bitonic merge of a bitonic sequence of 0s and 1s produces a sorted sequence.

**Step 1 — Take a Bitonic Binary Sequence.**  
By the bitonic definition, the sequence has the form: a block of 0s, a block of 1s, a block of 0s — *or its cyclic rotations*.

**Step 2 — Apply One Stage of Compare-and-Swap.**  
We compare element at index $i$ with element at index $i + n/2$, and place the **smaller** in the lower half and the **larger** in the upper half.

**Step 3 — Observe the Result.**  
By a counting argument, the lower half contains *only* the 0s and the upper half contains *only* the 1s (because every 0 in the upper half is matched with a 1 in the lower half and gets swapped down).

**Step 4 — Recurse on Each Half.**  
Each half is itself a bitonic sequence of length $n/2$. The induction hypothesis completes the proof.

---

## 3.5 The KTU Comparison Matrix (Analytical Side-by-Side)

| Dimension | Parallel Merge Sort | Parallel Quicksort | Bitonic Merge Sort |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Algorithmic Paradigm** | Divide-and-conquer | Partition-and-conquer | Comparison network |
| **Underlying Architecture** | PRAM (CREW) | PRAM (CREW) | Sorting network (fixed topology) |
| **Best-Case Time** | $O(\log^2 n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log^2 n)$ |
| **Worst-Case Time** | $O(\log^2 n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(\log^2 n)$ |
| **Number of Processors** | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n \log^2 n)$ for full version |
| **Work-Optimal?** | Only with $p = n/\log n$ | Yes (average case) | No (in standard form) |
| **Stability** | Yes | No | No |
| **Data-Independent Layout?** | No (depends on data values) | No (depends on pivot) | **Yes** (network is fixed) |
| **Communication Pattern** | Irregular (merge) | Irregular (broadcast pivot) | Regular (butterfly) |
| **Suitability for GPU/FPGA** | Low (irregular access) | Low (irregular access) | **High** (regular butterfly) |
| **Cache Friendliness** | Medium | High (in-place) | Low |
| **Determinism** | Deterministic | Randomized (typically) | Deterministic |
| **External-Memory Friendliness** | **High** (used in DBs) | Low | Low |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Heuristic:** Choose *Bitonic Sort* when the target is a GPU/FPGA and the input size is fixed; choose *Parallel Merge Sort* when stability and external-memory sort are required; choose *Parallel Quicksort* when average-case in-memory speed is the priority and pivot selection is randomized.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Parallel Merge Sort — Recursion + Parallel Merge Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Root Array size n"] --> B["Left Half size n over 2"]
    A --> C["Right Half size n over 2"]
    B --> D["Recursive Call left"]
    C --> E["Recursive Call right"]
    D --> F["Sorted Left Half"]
    E --> G["Sorted Right Half"]
    F --> H["Parallel Merge stage parallel rank and write"]
    G --> H
    H --> I["Final Sorted Array of size n"]

    classDef level0 fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef level1 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef level2 fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef level3 fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#003366,color:#003366
    class A level0
    class B,C level1
    class D,E level1
    class F,G level2
    class H,I level3
```

> *Reading aid:* Horizontal arrows denote concurrent execution; the funnel at "Parallel Merge" represents the $O(\log n)$-time Borodin–Hopcroft merge step.

---

## 4.2 Parallel Quicksort — Partition Tree with Pivot Broadcast

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Unsorted Array n elements"] --> P["Pick Pivot x random or median of three"]
    P --> B1["Broadcast x to all processors O log p"]
    B1 --> PT["Parallel Partition using prefix sum scan"]
    PT --> L["Sub-problem L elements less than x"]
    PT --> R["Sub-problem R elements greater than x"]
    L --> L1["Recurse on L in parallel"]
    R --> R1["Recurse on R in parallel"]
    L1 --> L2["Base case size 1 return"]
    R1 --> R2["Base case size 1 return"]
    L2 --> M["Concatenate L sorted and R sorted"]
    R2 --> M
    M --> O["Fully Sorted Array"]

    classDef io fill:#264478,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef parallel fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef leaf fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#003366,color:#003366
    class A,O io
    class P,B1,PT,L,R,L1,R1,M parallel
    class L2,R2 leaf
```

> *Reading aid:* The single *Pick Pivot → Broadcast* box is the *sequential bottleneck*; everything after the broadcast executes with full parallelism.

---

## 4.3 Bitonic Sort — The Complete 8-Element Network (3 Stages of Merges)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Limitation Acknowledgment:** Mermaid cannot faithfully render a true butterfly network. We therefore provide a **two-level block diagram**: (a) the top-level stage structure, and (b) the *Stage 2* and *Stage 3* detailed butterfly unfoldings in plain-text schematic form.

### (a) Top-Level Stage Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0["Input 8 elements unsorted"] --> S1["Stage 1 Four 2 sorts produce bitonic pairs"]
    S1 --> S2["Stage 2 Two 4 bitonic merges"]
    S2 --> S3["Stage 3 One 8 bitonic merge"]
    S3 --> S4["Final Sorted 8 elements"]

    classDef stage fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    class S0,S4 fill:#7f7f7f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    class S1,S2,S3 stage
```

### (b) Stage 1 — Four Parallel 2-Sorts (Bitonic Pairs)

```
Input indices : 0   1   2   3   4   5   6   7
                |   X   |   X   |   X   |   X          ← comparators (X = swap-down direction)
                v   ^   v   ^   v   ^   v   ^          ← result
            4 bitonic sequences of length 2  (a 0 , a 1 , a 2 , a 3 )
```

### (c) Stage 2 — Two Parallel 4-Bitonic-Merges

```
Block A: indices 0..3                  Block B: indices 4..7
                                       (cyclic shift of bitonic = bitonic)
   Pass 1: cmp(0,2) down, cmp(1,3) down
   Pass 2: cmp(0,1) down within left half, cmp(2,3) down within right half
   =>  Block A sorted   <=>   Block B sorted
```

### (d) Stage 3 — Single 8-Bitonic-Merge (The Critical Stage)

```
Pass 1 of Stage 3:  cmp(0,4), cmp(1,5), cmp(2,6), cmp(3,7)   [all "down" — smaller goes low]
Pass 2 of Stage 3:  cmp(0,2) down, cmp(1,3) down  |  cmp(4,6) down, cmp(5,7) down
Pass 3 of Stage 3:  cmp(0,1) down  |  cmp(2,3) down  |  cmp(4,5) down  |  cmp(6,7) down

Total comparators in Stage 3: 4 + 4 + 4 = 12
Total across the network: 4 (Stage 1) + 8 (Stage 2) + 12 (Stage 3) = 24 comparators
Total stages: 1 + 2 + 3 = 6 stages   ✓ matches  k(k+1)/2 = 3·4/2
```

### (e) Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Bitonic Network

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph stage1["Stage 1 Build Bitonic Pairs depth 1"]
        s1c1["CMP 0 1 down"]
        s1c2["CMP 2 3 down"]
        s1c3["CMP 4 5 down"]
        s1c4["CMP 6 7 down"]
    end
    subgraph stage2["Stage 2 Build Bitonic Quads depth 2"]
        s2c1["CMP 0 2 down"]
        s2c2["CMP 1 3 down"]
        s2c3["CMP 4 6 up"]
        s2c4["CMP 5 7 up"]
    end
    subgraph stage3["Stage 3 Final Bitonic Merge depth 3"]
        s3c1["CMP 0 4 down"]
        s3c2["CMP 1 5 down"]
        s3c3["CMP 2 6 down"]
        s3c4["CMP 3 7 down"]
    end
    stage1 --> stage2 --> stage3
```

> *Reading aid:* Each `CMP i j dir` node represents one compare-and-swap unit. The direction (`down`/`up`) is fixed *before* runtime; the network is data-oblivious. This is precisely why Bitonic Sort maps cleanly to GPU warp-synchronous execution.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a *bitonic sequence*. Give one example of a 4-element bitonic sequence.  `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A sequence $a_0, a_1, \dots, a_{n-1}$ is called **bitonic** if it is the cyclic rotation of a sequence that first strictly increases and then strictly decreases. Formally, $\exists\, i$ such that

$$a_0 \leq a_1 \leq \cdots \leq a_i \geq a_{i+1} \geq \cdots \geq a_{n-1}$$

**Example:** $3,\ 7,\ 9,\ 5$ — increases from 3 → 7 → 9, then decreases to 5. (3 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often say "bitonic means monotonically increasing then decreasing *exactly once*." This is wrong — *cyclic rotations* of such a sequence are also bitonic. Example: $5,\ 3,\ 7,\ 9$ is also bitonic.

---

### Q2. State the *Zero-One Principle* for comparison networks. Why is it useful in proving bitonic sort correct?  `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Zero-One Principle:** A comparison network with $n$ inputs correctly sorts *all* possible inputs if and only if it correctly sorts every input consisting only of 0s and 1s.

**Usefulness:** There are $2^n$ binary inputs — much fewer than $n!$ permutations — so we need to check only $2^n$ cases. For bitonic merge, the proof reduces to a *counting argument* on 0s and 1s, since after one compare-and-swap stage, all 0s in the upper half are swapped down to the lower half. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### ❑ Question A — Parallel Sorting — Merge Sort & Quicksort  `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain **Parallel Merge Sort** on a CREW PRAM. Derive its parallel time complexity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Parallel Quicksort** with randomized pivot. Compare its average-case and worst-case parallel time complexity, and discuss why random pivot selection helps. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1 — Divide phase:** Recursively split array of $n$ elements into two halves of $n/2$. Recursion bottoms at $n=1$.  
**Step 2 — Parallel merge phase:** Given two sorted arrays $A$ (size $m$) and $B$ (size $n$) with $m+n=N$, the Borodin–Hopcroft algorithm:
- Assigns $m$ processors, one per element of $A$.
- Each processor binary-searches its element into $B$ in $O(\log n)$ time → finds the rank.
- Each processor computes the final merged position: $\text{pos}(A_i) = i + \text{rank}(A_i, B)$.
- All $m$ processors write to disjoint output locations in $O(1)$ time.

**Step 3 — Recurrence:** $T(n) = T(n/2) + O(\log n)$.

**Step 4 — Solve by expansion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= T(n/2) + \log n \\
&= T(n/4) + \log(n/2) + \log n \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{\log_2 n} \log_2(2^i) \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{k} i = \frac{k(k+1)}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

With $k = \log_2 n$:

$$\boxed{T(n) = \frac{\log_2 n \cdot (\log_2 n + 1)}{2} = O(\log^2 n) \text{ with } O(n) \text{ processors.}}$$

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Pivot:** A single processor selects a pivot $x$ uniformly at random from the $n$ elements.

**Step 2 — Broadcast:** $x$ is broadcast to all $p$ processors using a tree-broadcast in $O(\log p)$ time.

**Step 3 — Partition (parallel prefix scan):** Each processor examines its local $n/p$ elements, marks them with $+1$ (less) or $-1$ (greater), and computes a *prefix sum* to determine the output position. This takes $O(n/p + \log p)$ time.

**Step 4 — Recurse:** The two sub-problems have *expected* size $n/2$ each. With $n$ processors, parallel partition cost is $O(1)$ per level.

**Average-case parallel time:**

$$\boxed{T_{\text{avg}}(n) = \sum_{d=0}^{\log_2 n} O(1) = O(\log n)}$$

**Worst-case parallel time:** If the pivot is always the minimum (probability $\sim 1/n!$):

$$\boxed{T_{\text{worst}}(n) = T(n-1) + O(1) = O(n)}$$

**Why random pivot helps:** With a deterministic pivot (e.g., the first element), a pre-sorted input causes the worst case. Random selection makes the worst case have probability $1/n!$, effectively negligible. Randomized algorithms are often used in practice (e.g., Java's `Arrays.parallelSort`).

---

### ❑ Question B — Bitonic Sort  `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Describe the **Bitonic Merge Sort** algorithm for $n = 8$ elements. Draw the complete comparator network. Calculate the number of comparator stages and the total number of comparators used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Prove the correctness of the bitonic merge operation using the **Zero-One Principle**. Show that the number of comparator stages for $n = 2^k$ elements is $\frac{k(k+1)}{2}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1 — Algorithm Sketch:** Bitonic sort recursively builds a bitonic sequence and merges it. For $n=8 = 2^3$:

1. Sort pairs → 4 bitonic sequences of length 2.
2. Merge pairs of bitonic sequences → 2 bitonic sequences of length 4.
3. Merge the two bitonic sequences → 1 sorted sequence of length 8.

**Step 2 — Network (reproduce from §4.3 above):** Three stages, with 4 + 8 + 12 = 24 comparators.

**Step 3 — Computation:**

$$\text{Stages} = \frac{\log_2 8 \cdot (\log_2 8 + 1)}{2} = \frac{3 \cdot 4}{2} = 6 \text{ stages}$$

Wait — this is the **total** across all three sub-stages. The KTU question may ask for *per-stage* or *total*. Compute both:

- Stage 1 (2-sort): 1 stage.
- Stage 2 (4-merge): 2 stages ($4 \to 2 \to 1$).
- Stage 3 (8-merge): 3 stages ($8 \to 4 \to 2 \to 1$).
- **Total stages: $1 + 2 + 3 = 6$** ✓ matches formula.

$$\text{Total comparators} = \frac{n \log_2 n \cdot (\log_2 n + 1)}{4} = \frac{8 \cdot 3 \cdot 4}{4} = 24$$

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Zero-One Principle Statement:** A comparison network correctly sorts *all* $n!$ permutations if and only if it correctly sorts every sequence of 0s and 1s of length $n$.

**Step 2 — Apply to a Bitonic 0-1 Sequence:** A bitonic 0-1 sequence of length $n$ has the form: $0^{a}\, 1^{b}\, 0^{c}$ (or cyclic rotation), where $a + b + c = n$.

**Step 3 — One Stage of Bitonic Merge:** Compare-and-swap $(i, i + n/2)$ with the "smaller goes down" direction. The smaller half is the *lower* half.

- The lower half gets only 0s and the upper half gets only 1s: this is because every 0 in the upper half is matched with a 1 in the lower half and is swapped down.

**Step 4 — Induction:** Both halves are now *bitonic* (each is monotonic), so by induction we apply the merge recursively to each half. The recursion bottoms out at length 2.

**Step 5 — Number of Stages:** Proved by induction in §3.3:

$$\boxed{\text{Stages}(n) = \frac{k(k+1)}{2} \text{ where } k = \log_2 n}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 
> 1. **Wrong stage count for $n=8$:** Some students report 3 stages (the *number of merges*), but the *number of comparator stages* is **6** because each merge has internal sub-stages. Read the question carefully: *stages* ≠ *merge levels*.
> 2. **Wrong comparator count:** The formula $\frac{n \log_2 n \cdot (\log_2 n + 1)}{4}$ gives **24 for $n=8$**, not 12. Off-by-a-factor-of-2 errors are common — verify by drawing the network.
> 3. **Missing the cyclic-rotation clause** in the bitonic definition.
> 4. **Forgetting that Bitonic Sort is *not work-optimal*** in its standard form (it's super-linear work).
> 5. **Confusing "down" and "up" comparators** in the network diagram — direction is determined by which element should be smaller in the *output*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This Before the Exam)**

- [ ] A sequence is **bitonic** if it is a cyclic rotation of an "up-then-down" sequence.
- [ ] **Zero-One Principle** reduces $n!$ test cases to $2^n$ binary test cases.
- [ ] Parallel Merge Sort: $T(n) = O(\log^2 n)$ with $n$ processors, uses **CREW PRAM** Borodin–Hopcroft merge.
- [ ] Work-optimal Parallel Merge Sort: $O(\log n)$ with $n/\log n$ processors.
- [ ] Parallel Quicksort: **average $O(\log n)$**, **worst $O(n)$**; relies on random pivot to avoid worst case.
- [ ] Bitonic Sort: **deterministic**, **data-oblivious**, uses a **fixed butterfly network**.
- [ ] Number of bitonic-sort stages: $\dfrac{k(k+1)}{2}$ where $k = \log_2 n$.
- [ ] Number of bitonic-sort comparators: $\dfrac{n \log_2 n \cdot (\log_2 n + 1)}{4}$.
- [ ] For $n=8$: **6 stages, 24 comparators**.
- [ ] **Bitonic Sort is *not* work-optimal** in its standard form.
- [ ] Merge Sort is the *only* stable algorithm of the three.
- [ ] Quicksort is the *only* in-place algorithm of the three.
- [ ] Bitonic Sort is the *only* data-independent / regular-parallelism algorithm of the three.
- [ ] Bitonic Sort maps naturally to **GPU and FPGA** because of its regular butterfly topology.
- [ ] Speedup $S_p = T_1 / T_p$; Efficiency $E_p = S_p / p$.
- [ ] Speedup is bounded above by $p$ (Amdahl's Law / Gustafson's insight).
- [ ] Always state *both* the **parallel time** and the **number of processors** when reporting complexity.
- [ ] For external-memory / database sorts, prefer **Parallel Merge Sort** (used by Spark, MapReduce shuffles).
- [ ] The phrase "**PRAM model**" alone is incomplete — always specify **EREW / CREW / CRCW / ERCW**.
- [ ] **Don't** use the phrase "bitonic sequence" when you mean "monotonic sequence" — they are different.
- [ ] **Memorize** the $n=8$ diagram — it appears as a guaranteed 5-to-7-mark sub-question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parallel Algorithms for Minimum Spanning Tree and Shortest Path
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST759 - PARALLEL ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Parallel Algorithms for Minimum Spanning Tree and Shortest Path

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Minimum Spanning Tree (MST)

### Formal Definition
A **Minimum Spanning Tree** of a connected, undirected, weighted graph $G = (V, E)$ with $n$ vertices and $m$ edges is a tree that:
- Contains **all $n$ vertices** of the graph.
- Uses exactly $(n-1)$ edges.
- Has **no cycles** (acyclic).
- Has the **minimum possible total edge weight** among all such spanning trees.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECST759 Module 3):** *A spanning tree of a connected graph is a sub-graph which contains all the vertices and is a tree. A minimum spanning tree is a spanning tree of a weighted graph having the minimum total weight among all possible spanning trees.*

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Cable Network Problem:** Imagine a telecom company must connect **5 cities** with underground fiber-optic cables. Every pair of cities has a different installation cost. The company has two constraints: **(a)** every city must be connected (no isolated city), and **(b)** total cable cost must be minimum. The MST gives the *cheapest network* that still keeps every city reachable. The graph here represents cities as **nodes** and cable costs as **weighted edges**.

**Mathematical Property (Cut Property):** For any cut $(S, V \setminus S)$ of the graph, the **minimum weight edge** crossing the cut belongs to *some* MST of $G$.

**Mathematical Property (Cycle Property):** For any cycle $C$ in $G$, the **maximum weight edge** in $C$ does *not* belong to *any* MST of $G$ (provided edge weights are unique; generalized otherwise).

---

## 1.2 Shortest Path Problem

### Formal Definition
Given a weighted graph $G = (V, E)$ with a weight function $w : E \to \mathbb{R}$ and a designated **source vertex** $s$, the **Single-Source Shortest Path (SSSP)** problem asks for the path of minimum total weight from $s$ to every other vertex $v \in V$.

The **All-Pairs Shortest Path (APSP)** problem asks for the shortest path between **every pair** of vertices.

The shortest path distance is recursively defined by the **Bellman Optimality Equation**:

$$dist(s, v) = \min_{(u, v) \in E} \left\{ dist(s, u) + w(u, v) \right\}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight — Negative Weights:** Dijkstra's algorithm **fails** for graphs with **negative edge weights**. Use the **Bellman-Ford** algorithm instead. For All-Pairs, use **Floyd-Warshall** when negative edges are allowed (but no negative cycles).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Google Maps:** When you ask Google Maps for the fastest route from your home to your office, the road network is modeled as a graph: intersections are **vertices**, road segments are **edges**, and travel time is the **edge weight**. The shortest path algorithm finds the minimum-time path. In a parallel setting, we could explore **multiple alternative routes simultaneously** to converge faster.

---

## 1.3 Why Parallelize MST and Shortest Path?

Standard sequential algorithms for these problems have the following complexities on a graph with $n$ vertices and $m$ edges:

| Problem | Best Sequential Algorithm | Sequential Complexity |
|---|---|---|
| MST (dense) | Prim's / Borůvka's | $O(n^2)$ |
| MST (sparse) | Kruskal's w/ Union-Find | $O(m \log n)$ |
| SSSP (non-negative) | Dijkstra + Fibonacci heap | $O(m + n \log n)$ |
| SSSP (general) | Bellman-Ford | $O(nm)$ |
| APSP | Floyd-Warshall | $O(n^3)$ |

With **$p$ processors**, our **theoretical goal** is to achieve a **speedup of $p$** and ideally reach a **polynomial** or near-linear parallel time complexity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Parallel Metrics Used in this Module:**
> - **Work** $T_1$ = sequential time complexity.
> - **Span** $T_\infty$ = parallel time on infinite processors.
> - **Parallel Time** $T_p$ = time on $p$ processors.
> - **Speedup** $S_p = T_1 / T_p$.
> - **Efficiency** $E_p = S_p / p$.
> - **Cost** $C_p = p \cdot T_p$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sequential Prim's Algorithm (Foundation for Parallel Prim's)

**Logic Steps (Sequential Prim's):**
1. Initialize a tree $T$ containing only the source vertex $s$.
2. Maintain a **min-priority queue** $Q$ keyed on edge weight, holding all edges crossing the cut between $T$ and $V \setminus T$.
3. **Repeat $(n-1)$ times:**
   - Extract the minimum-weight edge $(u, v)$ from $Q$.
   - If $v$ is already in $T$, discard (creates cycle).
   - Else, add edge $(u, v)$ and vertex $v$ to $T$.
   - Update $Q$ with all edges from $v$ to vertices outside $T$.

**Time Complexity:** $O(m \log n)$ with binary heap, $O(m + n \log n)$ with Fibonacci heap.

---

## 2.2 Parallel Prim's Algorithm

### Core Idea
In sequential Prim's, only **one** vertex is added to $T$ per iteration. In parallel Prim's, we relax the constraint and allow **multiple vertices** to be added in parallel, as long as no cycles are formed.

**Why this works (Cut Property):** Adding the minimum-weight edge crossing *any* cut is safe. If we use $p$ processors, we can process $p$ independent "min-edge extractions" in parallel.

### Algorithmic Steps
1. Partition the vertex set $V$ into $p$ groups, each handled by one processor.
2. Each processor maintains a **local minimum candidate** $d[v]$ = min-weight edge from $T$ to $v$.
3. Each processor checks all its assigned edges for relaxed weights.
4. Perform a **global reduction (min)** across all processors to find the true minimum.
5. Add the winning edge to $T$ in parallel; broadcast the update.

### Complexity
On $p$ processors with a graph having $n$ vertices and $m$ edges:

$$T_p^{\text{Prim}} = O\left(\frac{n^2}{p}\right) + O(n \log p)$$

The first term is the work distributed; the second is the reduction overhead.

---

## 2.3 Sequential Kruskal's Algorithm

**Logic Steps:**
1. Sort all $m$ edges by weight: $O(m \log m)$.
2. Initialize Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union) data structure with $n$ singleton sets.
3. Process edges in **ascending weight order**:
   - If `FIND(u) ≠ FIND(v)`, add edge $(u, v)$ to MST and `UNION(u, v)`.
   - Else, discard (would form a cycle).
4. Stop when $n-1$ edges are added.

**Time Complexity:** $O(m \log m)$ dominated by sort, or $O(m \log n)$ with optimal DSU.

---

## 2.4 Parallel Kruskal's Algorithm

### Core Idea
Kruskal's is **embarrassingly parallel** at the edge level — sorting and the union-find can both be parallelized.

**Steps:**
1. **Parallel Sort** all $m$ edges by weight using parallel sorting: $O\left(\frac{m \log m}{p}\right)$ work, $O(\log^2 m)$ span.
2. **Distribute** sorted edges across $p$ processors (each gets $m/p$ edges).
3. Each processor locally applies Kruskal's using **local DSU**.
4. Periodically, perform **global DSU merge** when edges from different processors might connect components (use a coordinator or parallel `UNION` with pointer jumping).

### Complexity
$$T_p^{\text{Kruskal}} = O\left(\frac{m \log n}{p}\right) + O(n \log p)$$

**Limitation:** DSU operations across processors require synchronization, limiting practical speedup.

---

## 2.5 Parallel Borůvka's Algorithm (Naturally Parallel!)

Borůvka's algorithm is **inherently parallel** and is the basis for many modern parallel MST implementations.

**Steps:**
1. For each vertex $v$, find its **minimum-weight incident edge**. This is fully parallel: $O(m/p)$ per phase.
2. Add all selected minimum edges to $F$. Edges that share a vertex create components.
3. **Contract** each connected component into a single **supernode**.
4. Remove self-loops; keep only the minimum-weight edge between any two supernodes.
5. Repeat until only 1 supernode remains.

### Complexity (Brent's Scheduling)
With $p$ processors:

$$T_p^{\text{Borůvka}} = O\left(\frac{m \log n}{p}\right) + O(\log^2 n)$$

The first term is the work; the second is the span (parallel depth).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Borůvka is parallel-friendly:** At each phase, **every vertex** independently picks its minimum edge with **no inter-processor communication** for the selection step. Only the contraction step requires a global reduction. This makes it ideal for distributed-memory and GPU implementations.

---

## 2.6 Sequential Dijkstra's Algorithm

**Logic Steps:**
1. Initialize `dist[s] = 0`; `dist[v] = ∞` for all other $v$.
2. Insert all vertices into a min-priority queue $Q$ keyed on `dist`.
3. While $Q$ is not empty:
   - Extract vertex $u$ with minimum `dist[u]`.
   - **Relax** all outgoing edges $(u, v)$: if `dist[u] + w(u,v) < dist[v]`, update `dist[v]`.

**Time Complexity:** $O(m + n \log n)$ with Fibonacci heap.

---

## 2.7 Parallel Dijkstra's Algorithm

### Key Challenge
Standard Dijkstra has an **inherent sequential bottleneck** because vertices are extracted in non-decreasing distance order, and this order **must be globally consistent**.

### Approaches
**Approach A — Vertex Partition (Limited Parallelism):**
1. Partition $V$ into $p$ subsets, one per processor.
2. Each processor computes candidate distances for its local vertices.
3. Perform a **global min-reduction** to extract the next vertex.
4. After extraction, all processors relax edges from $u$ in parallel.
5. Speedup is limited to ~$p$ for sparse graphs; near-linear in dense.

**Approach B — Δ-Stepping (More Practical):**
1. Bucket vertices by `dist` value in buckets of width $\Delta$.
2. Process **all vertices in a bucket in parallel**.
3. After processing, re-bucket relaxed neighbors.
4. Achieves near-linear speedup in practice; parallel time $O((n \log n + m)/p)$ with good constants.

### Complexity
$$T_p^{\text{Dijkstra}} = O\left(\frac{n \log n + m}{p}\right) + O(n \cdot \log p)$$

---

## 2.8 Parallel Floyd-Warshall Algorithm (All-Pairs Shortest Path)

Floyd-Warshall is **highly amenable to parallelization** because it uses a triple-nested loop with **no data dependencies** between different $(i, j)$ pairs in the innermost loop.

### Standard Sequential Form
```
for k = 1 to n:
    for i = 1 to n:
        for j = 1 to n:
            dist[i][j] = min(dist[i][j], dist[i][k] + dist[k][j])
```

### Parallelization Strategy
For each iteration of $k$ (sequential), the inner two loops over $i$ and $j$ can be **fully parallelized** across $p = n^2 / c$ processors (or chunked over $p$ processors).

**Complexity:**
$$T_p^{\text{FW}} = O\left(\frac{n^3}{p}\right) + O(n \log p)$$

For $p = n^2$ processors, this becomes $O(n \log n)$ — a **linear speedup** is achievable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Question Frequency:** Floyd-Warshall parallelization is one of the **most frequently asked** questions in KTU Module 3 because of its clean $O(n^3) \to O(n^3/p)$ speedup formula and the elegant dependency structure.

---

## 2.9 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Algorithm | Sequential $T_1$ | Parallel $T_p$ | Parallelism | Best Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Prim's | $O(n^2)$ | $O\left(\frac{n^2}{p}\right) + O(n \log p)$ | Limited by reduction | Dense graphs |
| Kruskal's | $O(m \log n)$ | $O\left(\frac{m \log n}{p}\right) + O(n \log p)$ | Sort + DSU | Sparse graphs |
| Borůvka's | $O(m \log n)$ | $O\left(\frac{m \log n}{p}\right) + O(\log^2 n)$ | High — natural | Distributed/GPU |
| Dijkstra's | $O(m + n \log n)$ | $O\left(\frac{m + n \log n}{p}\right) + O(n \log p)$ | Limited | SSSP, non-negative |
| Δ-Stepping | $O(m + n \log n)$ | $O\left(\frac{m + n \log n}{p}\right)$ | High | SSSP, practical |
| Floyd-Warshall | $O(n^3)$ | $O\left(\frac{n^3}{p}\right) + O(n \log p)$ | Very High ($n^2$ tasks) | APSP |
| Bellman-Ford | $O(nm)$ | $O\left(\frac{nm}{p}\right)$ | High (edge relax) | Negative weights |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **MST:** Network design (LAN cabling, pipeline layout), VLSI chip routing, image segmentation (Kruskal's), cluster analysis.
> - **Shortest Path:** GPS navigation (Dijkstra), Internet routing protocols (Bellman-Ford in RIP), airline scheduling, robotic path planning.
> - **All-Pairs:** Urban traffic matrices, social network analysis, computational biology (sequence alignment).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Parallel Floyd-Warshall Complexity

### Setup
We have $n^3$ total work units in sequential Floyd-Warshall. We distribute this across $p$ processors.

### Step 1: Total Work
The triple loop executes:

$$T_1 = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} 1 = n \cdot n \cdot n = n^3$$

### Step 2: Work Distribution
With $p$ processors sharing the work, each processor handles $n^3 / p$ operations. Therefore, work contribution is:

$$T_{\text{work}} = \frac{n^3}{p}$$

### Step 3: Span (Critical Path)
The outer loop over $k$ is **strictly sequential** (each $k$ depends on the previous $k$). However, the inner loops over $i$ and $j$ are independent.

For the $k$-th iteration, we can parallelize the $i$ and $j$ loops. With $p$ processors, this requires a tree reduction to find the minimum across $n^2$ values, taking:

$$T_{\text{span per iter}} = O(\log p) + O(1) \text{ (for the min reduction)}$$

Over $n$ iterations of $k$:

$$T_{\text{span}} = O(n \cdot \log p) + O(n)$$

### Step 4: Final Brent's Scheduling Bound
By Brent's Theorem, the parallel time is bounded by:

$$T_p \leq \frac{T_1}{p} + T_\infty = \frac{n^3}{p} + n \log p$$

Thus:

$$\boxed{T_p^{\text{FW}} = O\left(\frac{n^3}{p}\right) + O(n \log p)}$$

### Step 5: Speedup Calculation
Speedup is the ratio of sequential to parallel time:

$$S_p = \frac{T_1}{T_p} = \frac{n^3}{\frac{n^3}{p} + n \log p}$$

For $p \leq n^2$ (the meaningful range), the $n \log p$ term is **dominated** by $n^3/p$, giving:

$$S_p \approx p \quad \text{(linear / near-linear speedup)}$$

For $p = n^2$ specifically:

$$S_{n^2} = \frac{n^3}{n + n \log(n^2)} = \frac{n^3}{n + 2n \log n} = \frac{n^2}{1 + 2 \log n} \approx \frac{n^2}{2 \log n}$$

So even with $n^2$ processors, near-linear speedup in $n^2$ is preserved — this is one of the rare **polynomial-parallel** algorithms.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Parallel Borůvka Complexity

### Setup
At each phase, every vertex $v$ finds its minimum-weight incident edge in $O(\text{degree}(v))$ time. Total work per phase: $O(m)$.

### Step 1: Number of Phases
Each phase **at least doubles the size** of the contracted components (Borůvka's key invariant). So the number of phases is bounded by:

$$\text{Phases} \leq \log_2 n$$

### Step 2: Total Sequential Work
$$T_1 = O(m \cdot \log_2 n) = O(m \log n)$$

### Step 3: Parallel Per-Phase Work
With $p$ processors, finding min-incident-edge for all $n$ vertices is partitioned: each processor handles $n/p$ vertices. Edges must be distributed accordingly.

$$T_{\text{work per phase}} = \frac{m}{p} + \text{(distribution overhead)}$$

### Step 4: Span Per Phase
Component contraction requires a **connected-components** style parallel algorithm, e.g., using pointer jumping or tree-based union-find with parallel `UNION`:

$$T_{\text{span per phase}} = O(\log n)$$

This accounts for the parallel DSU merging.

### Step 5: Total Parallel Time
With $\log_2 n$ phases, each taking $O(\log n)$ span:

$$T_\infty = O(\log n \cdot \log n) = O(\log^2 n)$$

By Brent's Theorem:

$$T_p = \frac{T_1}{p} + T_\infty = \frac{m \log n}{p} + O(\log^2 n)$$

### Step 6: Speedup with $p = m / \log n$ Processors
With $p = m/\log n$:

$$S_p = \frac{m \log n}{\frac{m \log n \cdot \log n}{m} + \log^2 n} = \frac{m \log n}{(\log n)^2 + (\log n)^2} = \frac{m}{2 \log n}$$

Linear speedup in $m$ — Borůvka is **work-efficient** and **highly parallel**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Parallel Dijkstra Span (Vertex-Partition Approach)

### Setup
With $p$ processors, partition $V$ into $p$ sets $V_1, V_2, \ldots, V_p$ each of size $n/p$.

### Step 1: Per-Iteration Work
In sequential Dijkstra, each iteration extracts one minimum and relaxes its edges. Total work: $O(m + n \log n)$.

### Step 2: Per-Iteration Parallel Span
The bottleneck is the **min-extraction** which must be a global reduction. With $p$ processors, finding the global min from $p$ values:

$$T_{\text{min-extract}} = O(\log p)$$

Edge relaxation for the extracted vertex $u$ touches `out-degree(u)` neighbors. With all $p$ processors cooperating to check all edges:

$$T_{\text{relax}} = O\left(\frac{m}{p}\right)$$

### Step 3: Total Iterations
There are $n$ extractions (one per vertex). Each takes $O(\log p)$ span for min + parallel relaxation.

### Step 4: Final Bound

$$T_p = n \cdot O(\log p) + O\left(\frac{m + n \log n}{p}\right) = O\left(\frac{m + n \log n}{p} + n \log p\right)$$

### Step 5: Efficiency
For $p = \sqrt{m}$:

$$E_p = \frac{S_p}{p} = \frac{T_1}{p \cdot T_p} = \frac{m + n \log n}{p \cdot \left(\frac{m + n \log n}{p} + n \log p\right)}$$

For large sparse graphs where $m \gg n \log n$, this is **good** but not perfect. The bottleneck is the $\log p$ per-iteration overhead.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Δ-Stepping Wins:** It amortizes the $\log p$ overhead by processing **entire buckets** in parallel, achieving practical speedups within a small constant factor of $p$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Prim's (Parallel) | Kruskal's (Parallel) | Borůvka's (Parallel) | Dijkstra (Parallel) | Floyd-Warshall (Parallel) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Graph Type | Undirected | Undirected | Undirected | Directed/undir., non-neg. | Directed, neg. allowed |
| Parallelism Class | Low–Medium | Medium | **Very High** | Low–Medium | **Very High** |
| Memory Access | Contention-prone | Scatter/gather | Contention-tolerant | Heap contention | Regular, cache-friendly |
| Load Balancing | Hard (imbalanced cut) | Easy (sort) | **Natural (per-vertex)** | Hard (frontier) | **Trivial (block-wise)** |
| DSU Required | No | **Yes** | **Yes** | No | No |
| Communication Cost | High (reductions) | Medium | Low–Medium | High (heap) | Low |
| Best Architecture | Shared memory | Shared/distributed | **GPU/Distributed** | Shared memory | **Any (incl. GPU)** |
| Work-Efficient? | Yes | Yes | **Yes** | Yes | Yes |

The following block diagram illustrates the data flow of a parallel Borůvka implementation on $p$ processors.
