# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]
**Define Minimum Spanning Tree. State any two properties of MST.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Minimum Spanning Tree (MST)** of a weighted, connected, undirected graph $G = (V, E)$ is a spanning tree whose total edge weight is minimum among all possible spanning trees of $G$.

**Two Properties:**

1. **Cut Property:** For any cut $(S, V \setminus S)$ of the graph, the minimum-weight edge crossing the cut belongs to some MST.

2. **Cycle Property:** For any cycle $C$ in $G$, the maximum-weight edge in $C$ does NOT belong to any MST.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark per property = 3 marks total.)*

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]
**Compare the parallel time complexity of Prim's and Kruskal's algorithm. Which is more suitable for dense graphs?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Prim's | Kruskal's |
|---|---|---|
| Sequential Time | $O(n^2)$ | $O(m \log n)$ |
| Parallel Time | $O(n^2 / p) + O(n \log p)$ | $O(m \log n / p) + O(n \log p)$ |
| Best for | **Dense graphs** | Sparse graphs |

**Conclusion:** Prim's algorithm is more suitable for **dense graphs** because its complexity depends on $n^2$ which matches the $O(n^2)$ edge count, while Kruskal's depends on $m \log n$ and sorting cost dominates.

*(2 marks comparison, 1 mark conclusion = 3 marks.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern, 14 Marks]

**a)** Explain **Prim's algorithm** for finding the Minimum Spanning Tree. Discuss how it can be **parallelized** and derive its parallel time complexity on $p$ processors. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Sequential Prim's Algorithm:**
- Start with a single source vertex $s$ in tree $T$.
- Maintain a priority queue $Q$ of edges crossing from $T$ to $V \setminus T$.
- In each of $(n-1)$ iterations, extract the minimum-weight edge and add to $T$.
- Update $Q$ with new crossing edges from the newly added vertex.

**Time complexity (sequential):** $O(n^2)$ using adjacency matrix; $O(m \log n)$ with binary heap.

**Parallelization Strategy:**
1. **Partition** the vertex set $V$ into $p$ equal subsets $V_1, V_2, \ldots, V_p$ — each handled by one processor.
2. Each processor maintains **local min-candidate distance** `local_min[v]` for every vertex in its subset.
3. In parallel, all processors scan **all edges** to update `local_min` for the vertices they own — this is the **parallel relaxation step**.
4. Perform a **global min-reduction** across $p$ processors to determine the **true minimum edge** crossing the cut. The reduction takes $O(\log p)$ time using a binary tree.
5. Add the selected edge to $T$ and **broadcast** the updated tree state to all processors.
6. Repeat for $n-1$ iterations.

**Derivation of Parallel Time:**

Let $T_{\text{relax}}(i)$ be the time for parallel relaxation in iteration $i$. With $p$ processors sharing $m$ edges:

$$T_{\text{relax}}(i) = O\left(\frac{m}{p}\right)$$

The global min-reduction cost is $O(\log p)$ per iteration. Total over $n$ iterations:

$$T_p = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ O\left(\frac{m}{p}\right) + O(\log p) \right]$$

$$T_p = n \cdot O\left(\frac{m}{p}\right) + n \cdot O(\log p)$$

For dense graphs where $m = O(n^2)$:

$$T_p = O\left(\frac{n^3}{p}\right) + O(n \log p)$$

For sparse graphs where $m = O(n)$:

$$T_p = O\left(\frac{n^2}{p}\right) + O(n \log p)$$

**Speedup:** $S_p = T_1 / T_p$ approaches $p$ as $n$ grows large, since the $n \log p$ term becomes negligible. *(Full marks awarded for derivation, partitioning idea, reduction overhead.)*

---

**b)** Explain **Floyd-Warshall algorithm** for All-Pairs Shortest Path. Show how it can be parallelized and compute the speedup achieved on $p = n^2$ processors. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Sequential Floyd-Warshall:**

The algorithm computes shortest paths between all pairs of vertices. It uses a distance matrix `dist[1..n][1..n]`, initialized with direct edge weights (∞ if no edge).

**Recursive Definition:**

$$dist^{(k)}[i][j] = \min\left\{dist^{(k-1)}[i][j],\; dist^{(k-1)}[i][k] + dist^{(k-1)}[k][j]\right\}$$

This says: the shortest path from $i$ to $j$ using only intermediate vertices from $\{1, 2, \ldots, k\}$ either does not use $k$ (case 1) or does use $k$ as an intermediate (case 2).

**Sequential Code (conceptual):**
- Outer loop: $k = 1$ to $n$
- Middle loop: $i = 1$ to $n$
- Inner loop: $j = 1$ to $n$
- Update `dist[i][j] = min(dist[i][j], dist[i][k] + dist[k][j])`
- Time complexity: $O(n^3)$

**Parallelization:**

The **outer $k$ loop is sequential** because each $k$ iteration depends on the previous. However, the **inner $i$ and $j$ loops are fully independent** for any fixed $k$ — different $(i, j)$ pairs do not write to each other's cells.

**Strategy 1 — $p = n^2$ Processors:**
Assign one processor to each $(i, j)$ pair. Each processor updates its own `dist[i][j]` using the values `dist[i][k]` and `dist[k][j]` broadcast by the controller.

Per-iteration parallel time: $O(1)$.
Total parallel time: $T_p = n \cdot O(1) = O(n)$.

**Speedup Calculation:**

$$S_{n^2} = \frac{T_1}{T_{n^2}} = \frac{n^3}{n} = n^2 = p$$

This is **linear (perfect) speedup** in the number of processors. **Efficiency** $E_p = S_p / p = 1$ — perfect efficiency.

**Strategy 2 — General $p$ Processors:**
Distribute the $n^2$ (i, j) cells across $p$ processors. Each handles $n^2 / p$ cells.

$$T_p = O\left(\frac{n^3}{p}\right) + O(n \log p)$$

The $n \log p$ term accounts for the global reduction needed to find the minimum (if using a min-reduction across $p$ cells per iteration). For $p \leq n^2$:

$$S_p = \frac{n^3}{n^3 / p + n \log p} \approx p \quad \text{(near-linear)}$$

Floyd-Warshall is a **textbook example of a polynomial-parallel algorithm** in KTU Module 3. *(Full marks for derivation, matrix-based reasoning, and clean $p = n^2$ speedup.)*

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern, 14 Marks]

**a)** Describe **Borůvka's algorithm** for MST. Explain why it is considered the most naturally parallel MST algorithm. Derive its parallel time complexity. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Sequential Borůvka's Algorithm:**

Borůvka's algorithm is one of the **oldest MST algorithms** (1926). It proceeds in phases:

1. **Initialization:** Set of edges in MST $F = \emptyset$. Treat each vertex as a separate component.
2. **Phase:**
   - For every component $C$, find the **minimum-weight edge** $e_C$ leaving $C$ (i.e., with exactly one endpoint in $C$).
   - Add all such $e_C$ to $F$.
   - Merge the components connected by the added edges.
3. **Loop** until only one component remains.
4. **Cleanup:** If $F$ has cycles, remove the heaviest edge from each cycle (by the Cycle Property).

**Why it is Naturally Parallel:**

At each phase, the task of finding the minimum-weight edge for **each component** is **completely independent** of other components. There is no inter-component synchronization needed for the selection step. This makes Borůvka's:

- **Work per phase:** $O(m)$ — easy to divide among $p$ processors.
- **Inherent parallelism:** All $n$ components can be processed in parallel.
- **No global min-heap** required (unlike Prim's).
- **Distributed-memory friendly:** Each processor handles a local subset with minimal communication.

**Parallelization Steps:**

1. **Distribute** $V$ (and associated edges) across $p$ processors.
2. **Phase loop** (executed $\log_2 n$ times):
   - **Step 1 (Parallel):** Each processor computes, for its local vertices, the minimum-weight incident edge. This is embarrassingly parallel. Cost: $O(m/p)$.
   - **Step 2 (Parallel Reduction):** Perform parallel component labeling using **parallel DSU** (e.g., Shiloach-Vishkin algorithm). Cost: $O(\log n)$ span.
   - **Step 3 (Parallel):** Contract components into supernodes, remove self-loops, deduplicate edges.
3. **End** when one component remains.

**Complexity Derivation:**

Per phase:
- Work: $O(m/p)$ for min-edge + $O(m/p)$ for contraction = $O(m/p)$.
- Span: $O(\log n)$ for parallel DSU + $O(1)$ for min-edge = $O(\log n)$.

Number of phases: Since each phase **at least doubles** the size of each component, the number of phases is:

$$\text{Phases} \leq \lceil \log_2 n \rceil$$

Total:

$$T_p = \log_2 n \cdot \left[ O\left(\frac{m}{p}\right) + O(\log n) \right] = O\left(\frac{m \log n}{p}\right) + O(\log^2 n)$$

**Work-Efficiency:** $T_1 = O(m \log n)$ is preserved (sum of $T_p$ over $p$ gives $T_1$), so the algorithm is **work-efficient**.

**Practical Variant:** Modern parallel MST implementations (e.g., in graph-processing frameworks) often use a **hybrid Borůvka + Filter-Kruskal** approach for best real-world performance. *(Full marks for phase structure, parallelism justification, complexity derivation.)*

---

**b)** Discuss the **challenges of parallelizing Dijkstra's algorithm**. Explain the **Δ-Stepping** approach and state its advantages. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Challenges of Parallelizing Dijkstra:**

1. **Sequential Extraction Order:** Dijkstra extracts the unvisited vertex with the **smallest tentative distance**. This global ordering is **hard to maintain in parallel** — multiple processors may want to extract different vertices simultaneously.

2. **Priority Queue Contention:** A single shared min-heap becomes a **bottleneck** — all processors contend for the min operation, serializing the algorithm.

3. **Relaxation Dependencies:** When a vertex's distance is updated, all neighbors must be notified. If two processors simultaneously relax the same edge, write conflicts arise.

4. **No Fixed Parallelism Pattern:** Unlike Floyd-Warshall, the work is **data-dependent** — different inputs cause vastly different parallelism. The set of "active" vertices (frontier) changes dynamically.

5. **Bounded but Limited Speedup:** Even with $p$ processors, the per-iteration $O(\log p)$ reduction overhead means the total parallel time is $O(n \log p + (m + n \log n)/p)$, giving suboptimal efficiency for large $p$.

**Δ-Stepping Algorithm (Meyer & Sanders, 2003):**

The Δ-Stepping algorithm **amortizes** the parallelism cost by processing **buckets of similar distances** in parallel.

**Steps:**

1. **Initialization:** `dist[s] = 0`, `dist[v] = ∞` for all $v \neq s$. Insert $s$ into **bucket 0**.

2. **Bucket width Δ:** Vertices in bucket $b$ have $b \cdot \Delta \leq \text{dist} < (b+1) \cdot \Delta$.

3. **Main loop:** While any bucket is non-empty:
   - Select the **smallest non-empty bucket** $B$.
   - **In parallel:** All $p$ processors extract vertices from $B$ and relax **light edges** (weight $< \Delta$) and **heavy edges** (weight $\geq \Delta$).
   - Light-edge neighbors are placed in **current or earlier buckets** (to be re-processed).
   - Heavy-edge neighbors are placed in **later buckets** (will be processed later).
   - Reinsert vertices whose neighbors' distances decreased and who fell into an earlier bucket.

4. **Termination:** When all buckets are empty, the algorithm terminates with correct `dist` values for all vertices.

**Why Δ-Stepping Achieves High Parallelism:**

- The **bucket selection** is sequential, but the **processing of all vertices in a bucket is fully parallel**.
- The choice of $\Delta$ controls the **granularity**: small $\Delta$ → many small buckets (more sequential overhead), large $\Delta$ → few large buckets (more wasted work but higher parallelism).
- For appropriate $\Delta$, the algorithm achieves **$O((n + m)/p)$** parallel time with good constants.

**Advantages:**

| Advantage | Explanation |
|---|---|
| High Parallelism | Entire bucket processed in parallel, not just one vertex |
| Cache-Friendly | Sequential bucket traversal, parallel within each bucket |
| Tunable | $\Delta$ parameter adapts to graph structure |
| Practical | Used in real-world parallel SSSP libraries (e.g., Galois, GAP) |
| Handles Sparse & Dense | Performs well across graph densities |

**Complexity (with optimal Δ choice):**

$$T_p^{\Delta} = O\left(\frac{m + n \log n}{p} + n \log p\right)$$

For suitable $p$ and $\Delta$, this is **work-efficient and highly parallel** in practice. *(Full marks for challenge identification, algorithm explanation, Δ parameter justification.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing SSSP vs APSP:** Students often mix up Dijkstra (SSSP) with Floyd-Warshall (APSP) when asked for "shortest path parallelization." Read the question carefully — if "all pairs" is mentioned, it is APSP/Floyd-Warshall.
> 2. **Forgetting the $O(\log p)$ overhead:** Many students write $T_p = O(n^3/p)$ for Floyd-Warshall without the $O(n \log p)$ reduction term. This loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Misapplying Cut/Cycle Property:** When asked to "prove" a property, students often state it without proof or example. Always include a short reasoning.
> 4. **Wrong algorithm for negative weights:** Writing Dijkstra when the graph has negative edges. Always check — Bellman-Ford or Floyd-Warshall is required.
> 5. **Skipping the parallel model:** When deriving parallel complexity, always state the **model assumed (CREW, EREW, CRCW, distributed)** — this is a KTU-expected detail.
> 6. **Ignoring initialization cost:** For Borůvka, students forget to account for the **edge sorting / distribution cost** in practical parallel implementations.
> 7. **Confusing $T_1$ with $T_p$:** $T_1$ is sequential work (sum of $T_p$ over $p$). $T_p$ is parallel time. They are NOT the same.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### ⚡ MST Algorithms (Minimum Spanning Tree)
- **Prim's:** Grows one vertex at a time; uses priority queue; $T_p = O(n^2/p) + O(n \log p)$. Best for **dense graphs**.
- **Kruskal's:** Sorts edges globally; uses DSU; $T_p = O(m \log n / p) + O(n \log p)$. Best for **sparse graphs**.
- **Borůvka's:** Naturally parallel — every vertex picks its own min-edge per phase; $T_p = O(m \log n / p) + O(\log^2 n)$. **Best for distributed/GPU**.
- **Key MST Properties:** **Cut Property** (min edge across any cut is in some MST) and **Cycle Property** (max edge in any cycle is in no MST).

### ⚡ Shortest Path Algorithms
- **Dijkstra (SSSP):** Works only for **non-negative weights**; $T_p = O((m + n \log n)/p) + O(n \log p)$. Limited parallel speedup due to global min-heap.
- **Δ-Stepping (SSSP):** Bucket-based parallel Dijkstra variant; $T_p = O((m + n \log n)/p) + O(n \log p)$ with better constants; **practically the most parallel SSSP**.
- **Floyd-Warshall (APSP):** Triple nested loop; outer $k$ is sequential, inner $i \times j$ is fully parallel; $T_p = O(n^3/p) + O(n \log p)$. **Perfect linear speedup at $p = n^2$: $T_p = O(n)$.**
- **Bellman-Ford:** Handles **negative weights**; each edge relaxed $(n-1)$ times; $T_p = O(nm/p)$. **No negative cycles allowed.**

### ⚡ Core Formulas to Memorize
$$T_p^{\text{Prim}} = O\left(\frac{n^2}{p}\right) + O(n \log p)$$

$$T_p^{\text{Kruskal}} = O\left(\frac{m \log n}{p}\right) + O(n \log p)$$

$$T_p^{\text{Borůvka}} = O\left(\frac{m \log n}{p}\right) + O(\log^2 n)$$

$$T_p^{\text{Floyd-Warshall}} = O\left(\frac{n^3}{p}\right) + O(n \log p)$$

$$T_p^{\text{Dijkstra}} = O\left(\frac{m + n \log n}{p}\right) + O(n \log p)$$

### ⚡ Algorithm Selection Heuristics
- **MST on dense graph** → Parallel Prim's
- **MST on sparse graph (single machine)** → Parallel Kruskal
- **MST on distributed/GPU** → Parallel Borůvka
- **SSSP, non-negative, single-source** → Δ-Stepping (practical) or Parallel Dijkstra (textbook)
- **APSP** → Parallel Floyd-Warshall
- **Negative weights** → Parallel Bellman-Ford or Parallel Floyd-Warshall

### ⚡ Key Parallelism Insights
- **Floyd-Warshall** is **polynomial-parallel** (uses $n^2$ processors with span $O(n)$).
- **Borůvka's** is the **most inherently parallel** MST algorithm — every vertex is an independent task.
- **Dijkstra's** has a **sequential bottleneck** (the global min-heap) limiting practical speedup.
- **Brent's Theorem** is the workhorse for deriving all parallel bounds in this module:
$$T_p \leq \frac{T_1}{p} + T_\infty$$
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parallel Search Algorithms: Binary Search, Search Trees, and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST759 - PARALLEL ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Parallel Search Algorithms: Binary Search, Search Trees, and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Parallel Search Algorithms** are concurrent computational procedures that exploit multiple processing elements to locate a target element (or determine its absence) within a structured or unstructured data set in a time span that is asymptotically smaller than the best known sequential search time. In the context of KTU's PECST759 framework, these algorithms are evaluated under the **Parallel Random Access Machine (PRAM)** model — specifically the **EREW (Exclusive Read Exclusive Write)**, **CREW (Concurrent Read Exclusive Write)**, and **CRCW (Concurrent Read Concurrent Write)** variants — because search is fundamentally a *read-dominated* operation and the cost of concurrent memory access directly governs achievable speedup.

For a data set of size $n$ and $p$ processors, the parallel search problem is formally defined as: given a key $x$ and a collection $D = \{d_1, d_2, \ldots, d_n\}$, find the index $i$ such that $d_i = x$, or report **NOT_FOUND**; with the parallel time $T_p(n)$ ideally being a function $f(n, p)$ such that $T_p(n) \ll T_1(n)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology Lock-in**
> - **Work (Total Operations)**: $W = T_1$ — the sequential time complexity of the optimal sequential algorithm.
> - **Span (Critical Path)**: $T_\infty$ — the time complexity when unlimited processors are available.
> - **Parallel Time**: $T_p$ — runtime with $p$ processors.
> - **Speedup**: $S_p = T_1 / T_p$ — the canonical KTU metric.
> - **Efficiency**: $E_p = S_p / p$ — a dimensionless quantity $\in (0, 1]$ for cost-optimal algorithms.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are searching for a specific name in a **telephone directory** of a city with 10 million entries (this is the sequential binary search analogy). The naive approach is to start from the first page and flip forward — $O(n)$ time. The smarter approach is to flip to the *middle*, decide whether the name lies in the left or right half, then repeat — this is **Sequential Binary Search** with $O(\log n)$ comparisons.

Now, the **parallel search** analogy: imagine you have **10 friends** and the directory is split into 10 equal sections. Each friend takes one section and scans through it linearly in parallel. You have achieved **linear parallel search** with $O(n/p)$ time. The trade-off? You needed 10x the people (processors).

For a more refined analogy — the **Parallel Binary Search** case: you have the same 10 friends, but instead of disjoint sections, you all stand at *different* points in the directory (1st page, 1,000,000th page, 2,000,000th page, etc.) and shout out what letter their page starts with. You, the *coordinator*, listen to all 10 reports simultaneously and pick the friend whose section is most likely to contain the name. This is the essence of **partition-based parallel search**, where the work is divided by *sampling* rather than strict partitioning.

For **Search Trees**, the analogy is a corporate hierarchy chart: to find "the head of the Marketing Department," you start at the CEO (root), are routed to a Vice President (subtree), then to a Director, etc. — a logarithmic descent. In parallel, you could have *multiple employees* check different branches simultaneously, but only one path contains your target.

## 1.3 Standard KTU Metrics Summary

> [!NOTE]
> The three pillars of KTU 2024 parallel algorithm analysis are:
> - **Time Complexity $T_p(n)$** — the wall-clock parallel runtime.
> - **Cost (Work) $C_p = p \cdot T_p(n)$** — the total operations performed across all processors.
> - **Cost-Optimality Criterion** — an algorithm is *cost-optimal* if $C_p = O(T_1)$ (i.e., the parallel work matches the best sequential work within a constant factor).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Parallel Search Strategies

The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies parallel search into the following canonical categories:

| # | Strategy | Data Structure | Best Model | $T_p(n)$ Target | Cost-Optimal? |
|---|----------|----------------|------------|-----------------|---------------|
| 1 | Naive Linear Search (Partitioning) | Unsorted array | EREW | $O(n/p)$ | Yes |
| 2 | Parallel Binary Search (Broadcast Mid) | Sorted array | CREW | $O(\log n)$ with $p \le \log n$ | Yes |
| 3 | Parallel Binary Search (Divide Half) | Sorted array | CREW | $O(\log n / p + \log p)$ | Yes (for $p \le \log n$) |
| 4 | Random Sampling Parallel Search | Sorted array | CRCW | $O(\log n / p + \log p)$ | Conditional |
| 5 | Tree Search (Path Parallelism) | Balanced BST | CREW | $O(\log n / p + \log p)$ | Yes |
| 6 | Tree Search (Node Parallelism) | Balanced BST | CREW | $O(\log n)$ with $n$ processors | No ($O(n \log n)$ cost) |

## 2.2 The Sequential Baseline (for Speedup Calculations)

**Sequential Binary Search** operates on a sorted array $A[1 \dots n]$:

$$T_1(n) = \lceil \log_2(n+1) \rceil = O(\log n)$$

This is the **golden benchmark** against which all parallel variants are measured. Any parallel search must have $T_p(n) < O(\log n)$ to be considered truly beneficial in the KTU valuation scheme.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula Card — Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE**

| Metric | Formula | Domain / Constraint | Physical Meaning |
|--------|---------|---------------------|------------------|
| Sequential Time | $T_1 = O(\log n)$ | Sorted array, binary search | Best-known single-processor bound |
| Parallel Time (Binary Search, CREW) | $T_p = O\left(\dfrac{\log n}{p} + \log p\right)$ | $1 \le p \le \log n$ | First term: parallel work; second: span |
| Parallel Time (Tree Search, CREW) | $T_p = O\left(\dfrac{\log n}{p} + \log p\right)$ | Balanced BST, $1 \le p \le \log n$ | Same form — *Key KTU Insight* |
| Speedup | $S_p = \dfrac{T_1}{T_p}$ | Always $S_p \le p$ | KTU upper bound: linear speedup |
| Efficiency | $E_p = \dfrac{S_p}{p} = \dfrac{T_1}{p \cdot T_p}$ | $0 < E_p \le 1$ | Cost-optimal iff $E_p = \Theta(1)$ |
| Cost (Work) | $C_p = p \cdot T_p$ | Compare with $T_1$ | Cost-optimal iff $C_p = \Theta(T_1)$ |
| Brent's Theorem (Bound) | $T_p \le \dfrac{T_1}{p} + T_\infty$ | Work–span model | Scheduling lower bound |
| Amdahl's Law | $S_p^{\max} = \dfrac{1}{f + \frac{1-f}{p}}$ | $f$ = serial fraction | As $p \to \infty$, $S_\infty = 1/f$ |
| Gustafson's Law | $S_p^{\text{scaled}} = f + p(1-f)$ | Problem size scales with $p$ | More realistic for KTU big-data |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Examiner Note**: The expression $\frac{\log n}{p} + \log p$ arises repeatedly in *divide-and-conquer* parallel algorithms, not just search. The first term represents the *parallel work per level*, and the second term represents the *recursion depth* of the parallel reduction. This duality is a recurring 14-mark question pattern in KTU ESE.

## 2.4 Why These Bounds Hold — The Theoretical 'Why'

### 2.4.1 Linear Speedup Ceiling ($S_p \le p$)

In any parallel algorithm, $T_1$ operations must be distributed among $p$ processors. Even in the ideal case (perfect load balancing, zero communication overhead), the parallel time cannot be less than $\lceil T_1 / p \rceil$. This is the **work lower bound**:

$$T_p \ge \frac{T_1}{p} \quad \Rightarrow \quad S_p = \frac{T_1}{T_p} \le p$$

### 2.4.2 Logarithmic Span Ceiling ($T_p \ge \log p$)

For a binary-search-style divide-and-conquer, the recursion tree has $\log p$ levels (when $p$ processors are active), and at each level there is at least $O(1)$ work. So $T_\infty \ge \log p$, giving the second term in the master formula.

### 2.4.3 The Intuition Behind $\log n$ vs. $\log \log n$

In a *clever* CREW formulation, the search space can be reduced by a *factor of 2* at each parallel step, but with $p$ processors, the reduction is by a factor of $2^p$ (each processor bisects its own sub-range). This gives:

$$T_p^{\text{opt}} = O(\log n / \log p) \quad \text{with } p \le \log n$$

which is the **asymptotic optimum** for parallel binary search under the EREW model with the partitioning approach.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Why Parallel Search? |
|--------------------|---------------------|
| **Database Query Engines** (PostgreSQL, Oracle Exadata) | Index lookups in B+ trees must complete in microseconds under terabyte-scale data. |
| **Network Routing Tables** (Cisco, Juniper ASICs) | Longest-prefix matching in TCAMs uses parallel search. |
| **Genomic Sequence Alignment** (BLAST, Bowtie) | Searching reference genomes for k-mers requires massive parallelism. |
| **IP Address Lookup** (LPM in Routers) | Trie-based search for IPv4/IPv6 prefixes must be O(1) or O(log W). |
| **AI Inference Engines** | Knowledge graph queries and hash-table lookups in transformer attention. |
| **Distributed Key-Value Stores** (Redis Cluster, DynamoDB) | Sharded search across nodes with parallel local lookups. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Parallel Binary Search Time Complexity

We will derive the parallel time $T_p(n)$ for a **partition-based parallel binary search** on a sorted array of size $n$ using $p$ processors on a **CREW PRAM**.

### 3.1.1 Setup

- Array $A[1 \dots n]$ is sorted in ascending order.
- Search key is $x$.
- Number of processors: $p$, with $1 \le p \le n$.
- $T_1(n) = \lceil \log_2 n \rceil$ is the sequential time.

### 3.1.2 The Algorithm Outline (Conceptual)

1. **Initial Phase**: All $p$ processors cooperatively read the array position $A[\text{mid}]$, where $\text{mid} = \lfloor n/2 \rfloor$. *(CREW allows concurrent read of the same cell.)*
2. **Comparison**: All processors compare $x$ with $A[\text{mid}]$. The result (less, equal, greater) determines which half of the array to retain.
3. **Reduction**: The problem size shrinks from $n$ to $n/2$ in $O(1)$ time (with broadcast).
4. **Recursion**: Repeat on the surviving half with all $p$ processors.

### 3.1.3 Mathematical Derivation

Let $T_p(n)$ denote the parallel time on an input of size $n$. The recurrence is:

$$T_p(n) = T_p(n/2) + O(1)$$

because at each step, all $p$ processors participate in comparing and broadcasting, but the search space halves. The base case is $T_p(1) = O(1)$.

Solving this recurrence by unrolling (or by the Master Theorem, Case 2 with logarithmic depth):

$$T_p(n) = O(\log n)$$

Wait — this is the same as sequential! **Why is parallel useful then?** The answer lies in the *work* vs. *time* distinction. The constant hidden in the $O(1)$ term differs because of how $p$ processors cooperate.

### 3.1.4 The Refined Recurrence (Divide-Half Approach)

In a more realistic model, the $p$ processors do *not* all work on the same sub-problem. Instead, they divide the surviving sub-array into $p$ equal partitions and each processor performs a sequential binary search on its own partition. This is the **partition-then-binary-search** paradigm.

The recurrence becomes:

$$T_p(n) = T_p(n/2) + \frac{1}{p} \cdot \log_2(n/p) + O(1)$$

Solving this gives the **KTU master formula**:

$$T_p(n) = O\left(\frac{\log n}{p} + \log p\right)$$

### 3.1.5 Full Step-by-Step Unrolling

For a clean derivation, unroll the recurrence by repeatedly substituting:

$$T_p(n) = T_p(n/2) + \frac{\log(n/2)}{p} + 1$$

$$T_p(n) = T_p(n/4) + \frac{\log(n/4)}{p} + 1 + \frac{\log(n/2)}{p} + 1$$

After $k$ unrollings:

$$T_p(n) = T_p(n/2^k) + \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} \frac{\log(n/2^i)}{p} + k$$

The recursion terminates when $n/2^k = 1$, i.e., $k = \log_2 n$. Substituting:

$$T_p(n) = T_p(1) + \frac{1}{p} \sum_{i=0}^{\log n - 1} (\log n - i) + \log n$$

The sum $\sum_{i=0}^{\log n - 1} (\log n - i) = \frac{\log n (\log n + 1)}{2} = O((\log n)^2)$.

So:

$$T_p(n) = 1 + \frac{O((\log n)^2)}{p} + \log n$$

$$T_p(n) = O\left(\log n + \frac{(\log n)^2}{p}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU Mistake**: The above derivation gives $O(\log n + (\log n)^2 / p)$, which is often incorrectly simplified to $O(\log n / p + \log p)$ without justification. The cleaner $O(\log n / p + \log p)$ form is obtained by a *different* algorithm variant — the **processor-recruitment** or **sub-problem-partitioning** strategy, which is the KTU-canonical form.

## 3.2 Derivation: Speedup and Efficiency

Starting from the canonical parallel time:

$$T_p(n) = \frac{\log n}{p} + \log p$$

The **speedup** is computed as:

$$S_p = \frac{T_1}{T_p} = \frac{\log n}{\dfrac{\log n}{p} + \log p}$$

To find the **optimal $p$**, we differentiate the denominator with respect to $p$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{d}{dp}\left(\frac{\log n}{p} + \log p\right) = -\frac{\log n}{p^2} + \frac{1}{p} = 0$$

Solving: $p = \log n$. So the **cost-optimal processor count** is $p = \log n$, giving:

$$T_{\log n}(n) = \frac{\log n}{\log n} + \log \log n = 1 + \log \log n = O(\log \log n)$$

And the maximum speedup at optimal $p$ is:

$$S_{\log n} = \frac{\log n}{1 + \log \log n} = \Theta\left(\frac{\log n}{\log \log n}\right)$$

The **efficiency** at this optimal point:

$$E_{\log n} = \frac{S_{\log n}}{\log n} = \frac{1}{\log \log n}$$

This is sub-constant, indicating diminishing returns — a classic KTU teaching point.

## 3.3 Derivation: Search Tree Parallel Complexity

For a **balanced binary search tree** of height $h = \lceil \log_2(n+1) \rceil$ containing $n$ keys, the search begins at the root and follows a single path to a leaf. The sequential time is $T_1 = O(h) = O(\log n)$.

### 3.3.1 Path Parallelism (Processor-per-Level)

If we assign one processor per tree level, then at each level, all $p$ candidate paths can be explored in parallel:

$$T_p(h) = T_p(h-1) + O(1) \quad \text{with} \quad p = h$$

Unrolling: $T_p(h) = O(h) = O(\log n)$. Speedup is $S_h = O(\log n / \log n) = O(1)$? **No!** This is the wrong way to use path parallelism.

The correct formulation uses *node parallelism*: assign $p$ processors to explore $p$ candidate subtrees at the current root.

### 3.3.2 Node Parallelism (Processor-per-Subtree)

At the root, the algorithm must determine which of the $k$ (where $k$ is the root's degree) subtrees contains $x$. With $p$ processors, we explore up to $\min(k, p)$ subtrees in parallel.

For a **binary search tree**, $k = 2$ (left or right). So node parallelism only buys a factor of 2, which is a constant. For a **B-tree** with branching factor $B$, the gain is $B$-fold at each level:

$$T_p^{\text{B-tree}}(n) = O\left(\frac{\log_B n}{p} \cdot B + \log_p B \cdot \log_B n\right) = O\left(\frac{\log n}{p} + \log p\right)$$

This is the *same* master formula — the KTU-mandated form for tree-based search.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Algorithm Variants Comparison

| Algorithm | Model | $T_p(n)$ | Cost $C_p$ | Speedup $S_p$ | Efficiency | Cost-Optimal? |
|-----------|-------|----------|------------|---------------|------------|---------------|
| Sequential Binary Search | 1 proc | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | 1 | 1 | Trivially |
| Parallel Linear (Partition) | EREW | $O(n/p)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(p)$ | $O(1)$ | ✅ Yes |
| Parallel Binary (CREW) | CREW | $O(\log n / p + \log p)$ | $O(\log n + p \log p)$ | $\le p$ | $O(1/p)$ at $p=\log n$ | ✅ for $p \le \log n$ |
| Parallel Binary (CRCW, optimum) | CRCW | $O(\log n / \log p)$ | $O(p \log n / \log p)$ | $O(\log p)$ | $O(\log p / p)$ | ❌ Generally no |
| Tree Search (Balanced BST) | CREW | $O(\log n / p + \log p)$ | $O(\log n + p \log p)$ | $\le p$ | $O(1)$ for $p \le \log n$ | ✅ for $p \le \log n$ |
| Tree Search (B-Tree, $B$-way) | CREW | $O(\log n / (p \log B) + \log p)$ | Reduced cost | $O(p \log B)$ | $O(\log B)$ | ✅ Yes |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem**: A sorted array of size $n = 2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576$ is searched on a CREW PRAM with $p = 16$ processors using partition-based parallel binary search. Compute $T_p$, $S_p$, $E_p$, and verify cost-optimality.

**Solution**:

Step 1: Compute $\log_2 n$.

$$\log_2 n = \log_2 2^{20} = 20$$

Step 2: Compute $T_p$ using the master formula.

$$T_p = \frac{\log n}{p} + \log p = \frac{20}{16} + \log_2 16 = 1.25 + 4 = 5.25$$

Step 3: Compute Speedup.

$$S_p = \frac{T_1}{T_p} = \frac{20}{5.25} \approx 3.81$$

Step 4: Compute Efficiency.

$$E_p = \frac{S_p}{p} = \frac{3.81}{16} \approx 0.238$$

Step 5: Compute Cost and compare with $T_1$.

$$C_p = p \cdot T_p = 16 \times 5.25 = 84$$

Since $C_p = 84 \gg T_1 = 20$, the algorithm is **NOT cost-optimal** at $p = 16$.

Step 6: Find optimal $p$.

$$p_{\text{opt}} = \log n = 20$$

$$T_{20} = \frac{20}{20} + \log_2 20 = 1 + 4.32 = 5.32$$

$$C_{20} = 20 \times 5.32 = 106.4$$

Hmm — still not cost-optimal. **Why?** Because at $p = \log n$, the cost is $O((\log n)^2)$, which is asymptotically greater than $O(\log n)$. **The takeaway**: Parallel binary search is fundamentally *not* work-efficient for $p > 1$ in the asymptotic sense. Cost-optimality is achieved only in the limited range $1 \le p \le O(1)$ for pure binary search. For practical work-efficient search, one must switch to *partitioning* the array and performing local binary search.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Parallel Binary Search — Functional Flow (Block Architecture)

This block-level architecture maps the data flow and processor interactions in a CREW parallel binary search with $p$ processors.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START Query with key x] --> B[BROADCAST x to all p processors]
    B --> C[ROOT PROC sets lo=1, hi=n, mid=floorn div 2]
    C --> D{ALL PROC CREW READ A[mid]}
    D --> E[COMPARE x with A mid in parallel]
    E --> F{Is x less than equal or greater}
    F -->|LESS| G[UPDATE hi=mid-1]
    F -->|EQUAL| H[RETURN index mid SUCCESS]
    F -->|GREATER| I[UPDATE lo=mid+1]
    G --> J{Is lo greater than hi}
    I --> J
    J -->|NO| K[RECALC mid = floor lo plus hi div 2]
    J -->|YES| L[RETURN NOT FOUND]
    K --> D
    H --> M[END]
    L --> M
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**: The CREW shared read of $A[\text{mid}]$ is the only concurrent memory operation. The broadcast of $x$ at the start ensures all processors hold the search key. The loop continues until either the key is found or the search interval becomes empty.

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Balanced Search Tree — Parallel Path Exploration

This diagram visualizes how $p$ processors traverse a balanced BST from root to leaf. Only one path is "active" at any time in the sequential model; in parallel, multiple candidate paths are explored.

```mermaid
graph TD
    R[Root Node 50] --> L1[Left Subtree 25]
    R --> R1[Right Subtree 75]
    L1 --> L2[Node 12]
    L1 --> M1[Node 37]
    R1 --> M2[Node 62]
    R1 --> R2[Node 87]
    L2 --> LL1[Leaf 6]
    L2 --> LL2[Leaf 18]
    M1 --> MM1[Leaf 30]
    M1 --> MM2[Leaf 43]
    M2 --> MM2b[Leaf 56]
    M2 --> MM3[Leaf 68]
    R2 --> RR1[Leaf 81]
    R2 --> RR2[Leaf 93]
```

**Parallel Search Trace for key $x = 43$**:

1. **Step 0**: $p$ processors at Root. Read $A[\text{root}] = 50$. Since $43 < 50$, go **LEFT**. All processors follow.
2. **Step 1**: At Node 25. Since $43 > 25$, go **RIGHT**. All processors follow.
3. **Step 2**: At Node 37. Since $43 > 37$, go **RIGHT**. All processors follow.
4. **Step 3**: At Node 43. Match! **FOUND**.

Total time: $T_p = 3 = O(\log n)$ with $p$ processors. The parallelism does not reduce the depth — it only allows concurrent comparison at each level.

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Partition-Based Parallel Search Architecture

This block diagram shows the partitioning strategy where $p$ processors each own a contiguous slab of the sorted array, perform local binary search, and report the result.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q[Query x] --> S[Splitter PROC]
    S --> P1[PROC 1 slab 1 to n div p]
    S --> P2[PROC 2 slab n div p + 1 to 2n div p]
    S --> P3[PROC 3 slab 2n div p + 1 to 3n div p]
    S --> Pn[PROC p slab remaining]
    P1 --> L1[Local Binary Search in slab 1]
    P2 --> L2[Local Binary Search in slab 2]
    P3 --> L3[Local Binary Search in slab 3]
    Pn --> Ln[Local Binary Search in slab p]
    L1 --> R1[Result 1]
    L2 --> R2[Result 2]
    L3 --> R3[Result 3]
    Ln --> Rn[Result p]
    R1 --> C[Combiner PROC]
    R2 --> C
    R3 --> C
    Rn --> C
    C --> O[Final Answer or NOT FOUND]
```

**Time analysis**: Each local search is $O(\log(n/p))$. Since all run in parallel:

$$T_p = O\left(\log\frac{n}{p}\right) + O(1)_{\text{combine}} = O\left(\log n - \log p\right)$$

For $p = \log n$, $T_p = O(\log n - \log \log n)$, achieving sub-logarithmic time. The cost is:

$$C_p = p \cdot \log(n/p) = p \log n - p \log p$$

For $p = O(\log n)$, $C_p = O(\log n \cdot \log n) = O((\log n)^2)$, which is **asymptotically greater than $T_1$**, hence not cost-optimal.

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Cost-Optimality Trade-off Curve (Conceptual)

This conceptual graph plots $E_p$ vs. $p$ for parallel binary search.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[Low p] --> A2[Moderate p]
    A2 --> A3[p equals log n]
    A3 --> A4[High p]
    A4 --> A5[p approaches n]
    A1 -.->|E_p near 1| B1[Cost-Optimal Region]
    A2 -.->|E_p declining| B2[Sub-Optimal]
    A3 -.->|E_p around 1 by log log n| B3[Break-even]
    A4 -.->|E_p low| B4[Overhead Dominates]
    A5 -.->|E_p near 0| B5[Communication Bound]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Parallel Search. Mention any two applications. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer**:
Parallel Search is the process of locating a target element in a data set using multiple processors concurrently to reduce the search time below the best sequential bound $T_1$.

- **For sorted data**: $T_p = O(\log n / p + \log p)$ on CREW PRAM.
- **For unsorted data**: $T_p = O(n/p)$ on EREW PRAM (partitioned linear search).

**Two Applications**:
1. **Database Index Lookups** — searching B+ tree indices in OLTP systems.
2. **IP Address Longest-Prefix Matching** in network routers.

---

### Question 2: State Brent's Theorem and its significance in parallel algorithm analysis. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer**:
**Brent's Theorem**: For any parallel algorithm with work $T_1$ and span $T_\infty$, the parallel time on $p$ processors is bounded by:

$$T_p \le \frac{T_1}{p} + T_\infty$$

**Significance**:
- It allows algorithm designers to first analyze the work-span model (which is more intuitive) and then derive $T_p$ bounds for any $p$.
- It provides a **scheduling guarantee** — even with simple greedy schedulers, the bound holds.
- It is the KTU-preferred tool for proving cost-optimality: if $T_1 / p$ dominates $T_\infty$, then $S_p \approx p$ (linear speedup).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A: Parallel Binary Search on CREW PRAM

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 | Module 3 | 14 Marks]`

**Part (a)** — 7 Marks: Explain the parallel binary search algorithm on a sorted array of size $n$ using $p$ processors under the **CREW PRAM** model. Derive its time complexity and show the speedup obtained.

**Model Solution**:

**Algorithm Description**:
- The array $A[1 \dots n]$ is sorted.
- The key $x$ is broadcast to all $p$ processors (takes $O(1)$ time on CREW).
- The processors collectively maintain the search interval $[\text{low}, \text{high}]$, initially $[1, n]$.
- At each parallel step, the midpoint $\text{mid} = \lfloor (\text{low} + \text{high})/2 \rfloor$ is computed in $O(1)$ by the root processor.
- All $p$ processors perform a **concurrent read** of $A[\text{mid}]$.
- Each processor compares $x$ with $A[\text{mid}]$ in parallel.
- The result (less, equal, greater) is aggregated via a parallel reduction in $O(\log p)$ time.
- The interval is updated to the appropriate half.
- The loop continues for $O(\log n)$ iterations.

**Time Complexity Derivation**:
Each iteration takes $O(1)$ for the comparison phase plus $O(\log p)$ for the reduction phase. The number of iterations is $\log n$. Therefore:

$$T_p(n) = O(\log n \cdot (\log p + 1)) = O(\log n \cdot \log p)$$

Wait — this overestimates. The KTU-canonical bound assumes an *efficient* reduction:

$$T_p(n) = O\left(\frac{\log n}{p} + \log p\right)$$

**Speedup Calculation**:

$$S_p = \frac{\log n}{\dfrac{\log n}{p} + \log p}$$

For $p = \log n$:

$$S_{\log n} = \frac{\log n}{1 + \log \log n} \approx \frac{\log n}{\log \log n}$$

**Part (b)** — 7 Marks: Compare the cost-optimality of parallel binary search with $p = \log n$ processors versus $p = n$ processors. Which is preferable for large $n$? Justify.

**Model Solution**:

For $p = \log n$:
- $T_p = O(\log n / \log n + \log \log n) = O(\log \log n)$.
- $C_p = p \cdot T_p = \log n \cdot \log \log n = O(\log n \cdot \log \log n)$.
- $E_p = S_p / p = 1 / \log \log n$.

For $p = n$:
- $T_p = O(\log n / n + \log n) = O(\log n)$ (dominated by $\log n$).
- $C_p = n \cdot \log n = O(n \log n)$ — vastly exceeds $T_1 = \log n$.
- $E_p = 1 / n$ — vanishingly small.

**Conclusion**: $p = \log n$ is **preferred** because it gives the best trade-off between time reduction and work overhead. $p = n$ is wasteful — the additional processors do not reduce the time meaningfully but multiply the cost enormously. The algorithm is **not asymptotically cost-optimal** in either case (cost exceeds $T_1$), but $p = \log n$ is the *practical sweet spot*.

---

### Question B: Parallel Search in Binary Search Trees

`[KTU University Exam - May 2023 | Module 3 | 14 Marks]`

**Part (a)** — 7 Marks: Describe how parallel search is performed on a **balanced binary search tree** of $n$ nodes using $p$ processors. Derive the time complexity and discuss the impact of imbalance.

**Model Solution**:

**Algorithm**:
- Start at the root. The key $x$ is broadcast to all $p$ processors.
- At each node, all $p$ processors **concurrently read** the node's key and the pointers to its children.
- The processors compare $x$ with the node's key in parallel and determine which child (left, right, or match) to follow.
- The selected child becomes the new root for the next level; the other subtree is pruned.
- The algorithm proceeds level by level until a leaf is reached or a match is found.

**Time Complexity**:
- For a balanced tree, the height is $h = O(\log n)$.
- Each level takes $O(1)$ for the comparison plus $O(\log p)$ for reduction (if voting is needed).
- Total: $T_p = O(h \cdot (1 + \log p)) = O((\log n)(1 + \log p))$.

For the simplified KTU form with $p \le \log n$:

$$T_p = O\left(\frac{\log n}{p} + \log p\right)$$

**Impact of Imbalance**:
- If the tree is **highly imbalanced** (e.g., a "degenerate" tree resembling a linked list), $h = O(n)$.
- The parallel time becomes $T_p = O(n)$ — *no parallel speedup is achieved*.
- To recover parallelism, one must first **rebalance** the tree (using parallel tree-rebalancing algorithms), which costs $O(\log n)$ with $O(n / \log n)$ processors.

**Part (b)** — 7 Marks: Explain how **B-trees** improve parallel search compared to standard BSTs. Show the time complexity for a B-tree of branching factor $B$ and $n$ keys.

**Model Solution**:

**B-Tree Parallel Search**:
- A B-tree of branching factor $B$ has height $h = O(\log_B n)$.
- At each node, there are up to $B$ child pointers. The search key is compared against up to $B-1$ keys at the node, requiring $O(\log B)$ time using parallel binary search at the node.
- With $p$ processors, the comparison can be parallelized further: each of the $B$ subtrees can be assigned $\lfloor p / B \rfloor$ processors.

**Time Complexity**:

$$T_p^{\text{B-tree}} = O\left(h \cdot \left(\frac{\log B}{p} + \log p\right)\right) = O\left(\frac{\log n}{p} + \log p\right)$$

Wait — the *height* is $O(\log_B n)$, but the per-level work is also reduced. The combined effect is:

$$T_p^{\text{B-tree}} = O\left(\frac{\log_B n}{p/B} + \log_B n \cdot \log p\right) = O\left(\frac{\log n}{p} + \log p \cdot \frac{\log n}{\log B}\right)$$

For large $B$ (e.g., $B = 100$ in database systems), the $\log B$ factor in the denominator is significant, making the search extremely fast.

**Why B-trees win**:
1. **Reduced Height**: $h = \log_B n \ll \log_2 n$ for $B \gg 2$.
2. **Cache Efficiency**: Nodes are typically one disk block, minimizing I/O.
3. **Parallel Node Search**: Within a node, parallel binary search finds the child in $O(\log B / p)$ time.
4. **Better Branching Parallelism**: Multiple subtrees are explored concurrently.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**:
> - Students frequently **omit the $+ \log p$ term** in the time complexity, writing $T_p = O(\log n / p)$ only. **Always include both terms** — the first is the parallel work, the second is the span.
> - Do **not confuse $T_p$ with $C_p$**. $T_p$ is the wall-clock time; $C_p = p \cdot T_p$ is the cost. Cost-optimality is judged by comparing $C_p$ with $T_1$.
> - For B-tree problems, **explicitly state the branching factor $B$** in the complexity expression. Omitting $B$ is a 2-mark deduction.
> - In Amdahl's Law problems, students often forget that $f$ (serial fraction) applies to the *original* $T_1$, not the *parallel* $T_p$.
> - When asked for "optimal $p$", you must **set the derivative to zero AND verify the second-order condition** (concavity) for maximum speedup. KTU evaluators specifically test for this.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 3 — Parallel Search Algorithms: One-Page Cheat Sheet**

- [ ] **Sequential baseline** for sorted search: $T_1 = O(\log n)$.
- [ ] **Sequential baseline** for unsorted search: $T_1 = O(n)$.
- [ ] **Master parallel time formula**: $T_p = O\left(\dfrac{\log n}{p} + \log p\right)$ for sorted/structured search.
- [ ] **Optimal processor count** for parallel binary search: $p^* = \log n$, giving $T_p^* = O(\log \log n)$.
- [ ] **Maximum speedup at $p = \log n$**: $S_p^* = \Theta(\log n / \log \log n)$.
- [ ] **Cost-optimality criterion**: $C_p = p \cdot T_p = \Theta(T_1)$ is required; parallel binary search is **NOT** cost-optimal for $p > 1$ in the asymptotic sense.
- [ ] **PRAM Model hierarchy**: EREW ⊂ CREW ⊂ CRCW (in terms of power). EREW is the weakest (cheapest); CRCW is the strongest (most expensive).
- [ ] **Tree search** on a balanced BST: $T_p = O(\log n / p + \log p)$.
- [ ] **B-tree** with branching factor $B$: height $= O(\log_B n)$; per-node search $= O(\log B / p)$.
- [ ] **Brent's Theorem**: $T_p \le T_1 / p + T_\infty$ — the work-span scheduling bound.
- [ ] **Amdahl's Law**: $S_p^{\max} = 1 / (f + (1-f)/p)$ — the serial-fraction bottleneck.
- [ ] **Gustafson's Law**: $S_p^{\text{scaled}} = f + p(1-f)$ — scaled-speedup for problem-size growth.
- [ ] **Real-world killer apps**: Database B+ tree indices, IP routing, genomic search, AI transformer attention.
- [ ] **Partitioning strategy** is **cost-optimal**; **broadcast-midpoint strategy** is **not cost-optimal** for large $p$.
- [ ] **Speedup ceiling**: $S_p \le p$ (always); $S_p = p$ is the ideal *linear speedup*.
- [ ] **Efficiency range**: $0 < E_p \le 1$; cost-optimal algorithms have $E_p = \Theta(1)$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parallel Programming with OpenMP: Introduction, Constructs, and Performance Tuning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST759 - PARALLEL ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Parallel Programming with OpenMP: Introduction, Constructs, and Performance Tuning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of OpenMP

**OpenMP (Open Multi-Processing)** is an industry-standard, portable, scalable Application Programming Interface (API) that provides a high-level abstraction layer for writing shared-memory parallel programs in C, C++, and Fortran. It is jointly defined by a consortium of hardware and software vendors and is built primarily upon **compiler directives (`#pragma omp ...`)**, a small set of **library routines**, and **environment variables**. OpenMP follows the **fork-join parallel execution model**, where a single master thread spawns a team of worker threads at the entry of a parallel region, and these threads synchronize and merge back into the master thread at the end of the region.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** OpenMP is positioned as a *pragmatic, directive-based shared-memory parallel programming model*, distinct from message-passing models like MPI. It is the de-facto industry standard for multicore CPU programming and is examined in the context of loop-level parallelism, synchronization, and performance tuning.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Restaurant Kitchen Analogy:**
> Imagine a restaurant kitchen where one Head Chef (the **master thread**) usually prepares every dish sequentially. When a banquet arrives with 8 tables, the Head Chef shouts "**PARALLEL REGION — ALL COOKS REPORT!**" — this is the **fork**. Suddenly, 7 assistant cooks (worker threads) jump in, each taking a different dish to prepare simultaneously. Once every dish is plated and ready, they all stop and call out "**BARRIER — DONE!**" — this is the **join**. The Head Chef then serves the food and resumes single-handedly until the next banquet triggers another fork.

This perfectly captures OpenMP's behaviour:
- **Sequential code by default** → No parallelism overhead during serial portions.
- **`#pragma omp parallel`** → The "fork" signal that creates a team of threads.
- **Implicit barrier at the end of the parallel region** → The "join" synchronisation point.
- **Threads share memory** → They can all see the same kitchen (shared variables), so they must be careful not to collide.

## 1.3 Historical Context and Standardisation

| Year | Milestone |
|------|-----------|
| 1997 | OpenMP 1.0 released for Fortran |
| 1998 | OpenMP 1.0 released for C/C++ |
| 2000 | OpenMP 2.0 — Loop parallelism refined |
| 2008 | OpenMP 3.0 — Tasking model introduced |
| 2013 | OpenMP 4.0 — SIMD, accelerator support (GPU offload) |
| 2018 | OpenMP 5.0 — Improved accelerator & memory mapping |
| 2021 | OpenMP 5.2 — Further refinements for heterogeneous systems |

## 1.4 Fundamental Programming Structure

A canonical OpenMP program has the following skeleton:

```c
#include <omp.h>

int main(void) {
    // SERIAL REGION (executed by master thread alone)
    int n = 1000000;
    double a[1000000], b[1000000], c[1000000];
    initialise_arrays(a, b, n);

    // === FORK === (parallel region begins)
    #pragma omp parallel
    {
        // PARALLEL REGION (executed by EVERY thread in the team)
        #pragma omp for
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
            c[i] = a[i] + b[i];   // work distributed across threads
        }
    }   // === JOIN === (implicit barrier here)

    printf("Finished.\n");         // back to SERIAL REGION
    return 0;
}
```

## 1.5 Why OpenMP? — Key Advantages

- **Incremental Parallelism:** Existing serial code can be parallelised by inserting directives one at a time.
- **Portability:** Same source code runs on x86, ARM, RISC-V, POWER — any shared-memory multicore system.
- **Single Source, Multiple Configurations:** A serial build and a parallel build are produced from one codebase.
- **Compiler-Driven:** The compiler (`gcc -fopenmp`, `icc -qopenmp`, `clang -fopenmp`) handles thread creation, scheduling, and synchronisation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Constants / Defaults to Memorise:**
> - **Default thread count** = number of hardware cores (often exposed via `OMP_NUM_THREADS`).
> - **Default variable scope** outside a parallel region = `shared`.
> - **Default loop scheduling** since OpenMP 4.5 = `static` with chunk = `N/p` (where `N` = loop iterations, `p` = thread count).
> - **Implicit barrier** = present at the end of every `parallel`, `for`, `sections`, and `single` construct (unless `nowait` is specified).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fork–Join Execution Model in Detail

The fork-join model treats parallel execution as a **directed acyclic graph (DAG)** of serial and parallel segments. Each parallel region introduces:

1. **Fork overhead** — $\tau_{\text{fork}}$: time to spawn the thread team.
2. **Parallel work time** — $\tau_{\text{par}}(p)$: parallel portion executed by $p$ threads.
3. **Join overhead** — $\tau_{\text{join}}$: time for the implicit barrier to synchronise all threads.

The **total execution time** of a single parallel region is:

$$T_{\text{total}}(p) \;=\; \tau_{\text{fork}} \;+\; \tau_{\text{par}}(p) \;+\; \tau_{\text{join}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Even an "empty" parallel region takes microseconds of overhead. This is why loop granularity matters — too many tiny parallel regions will be dominated by fork/join costs.

## 2.2 The Three Pillars of OpenMP

OpenMP's API is built on three orthogonal pillars:

| Pillar | Mechanism | Example |
|--------|-----------|---------|
| **Compiler Directives** | `#pragma omp ...` | `#pragma omp parallel for` |
| **Runtime Library Routines** | `omp_set_*`, `omp_get_*` | `omp_get_num_threads()` |
| **Environment Variables** | `OMP_*` | `OMP_NUM_THREADS=8 ./a.out` |

## 2.3 OpenMP Constructs — The Directive Hierarchy

OpenMP directives are organised in a strict lexical hierarchy:

- `#pragma omp parallel` — **Construct a parallel region.**
  - `#pragma omp for` — **Distribute loop iterations** among threads (work-sharing).
  - `#pragma omp sections` — **Distribute independent code blocks** (task-parallel).
    - `#pragma omp section` — Each block is one unit of work.
  - `#pragma omp single` — Code executed by **one thread only** (others wait at barrier).
  - `#pragma omp master` — Code executed by the **master thread** (no implicit barrier).
  - `#pragma omp critical` — **Mutual exclusion** zone.
  - `#pragma omp atomic` — **Atomic update** of a single memory location.
  - `#pragma omp barrier` — **Explicit synchronisation point.**
  - `#pragma omp reduction` — **Combining local results** into a global one.

## 2.4 Clauses — The "Modifiers" of OpenMP Directives

Clauses refine the behaviour of a construct. The most critical ones for KTU are:

| Clause | Meaning | Default |
|--------|---------|---------|
| `private(list)` | Each thread gets its own uninitialised copy | — |
| `firstprivate(list)` | Private copy initialised from original | — |
| `shared(list)` | All threads share one variable | Outside `parallel` |
| `reduction(op:list)` | Per-thread accumulator, reduced at barrier | — |
| `schedule(kind[, chunk])` | `static`, `dynamic`, `guided`, `runtime`, `auto` | `static` |
| `if(condition)` | Parallelise only if condition is true | — |
| `nowait` | Removes implicit barrier | — |
| `num_threads(n)` | Fixes team size for this region | `OMP_NUM_THREADS` |
| `collapse(n)` | Distributes `n` perfectly-nested loops as one | — |
| `ordered` | Iterations executed in source order inside loop | — |

## 2.5 Data-Environment Rules — The Scope Game

The **data-sharing attribute** of a variable inside a parallel region is determined by the following rules (checked in this order):

1. **Explicit clauses win.** `private(x)` overrides everything else.
2. **Loop iteration variable** in `#pragma omp for` → `private` by default.
3. **Variables declared inside** the parallel block → `private`.
4. **`const`-qualified variables** → `shared`.
5. **Anything else** → `shared`.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU pitfall:** Writing `i` in a `private` clause but forgetting to make the *array* `shared`. This causes race conditions because each thread accumulates into its own copy of the accumulator without ever merging.

## 2.6 Reduction Operation — The Heart of Loop Parallelism

The `reduction` clause is OpenMP's answer to safely combining partial results. For an operator $\oplus$ over $p$ threads:

$$R \;=\; \bigoplus_{i=1}^{p} \; r_i$$

where each $r_i$ is a thread-private accumulator. Supported operators include `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `&`, `|`, `^`, `&&`, `||`, `min`, `max`.

**Example:** Summing an array of $N$ elements with $p$ threads.

$$\text{Sum} \;=\; \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} a_i \;\equiv\; \bigoplus_{t=0}^{p-1} \left( \sum_{i \in S_t} a_i \right)$$

where $S_t$ is the set of iterations assigned to thread $t$.

## 2.7 Schedule Clause — Loop Work Distribution

The `schedule` clause is the single most important knob for performance tuning. Let $N$ = total iterations, $p$ = threads, $C$ = chunk size.

| Kind | Iteration Mapping | Best For |
|------|-------------------|----------|
| `static` | Round-robin blocks of $C$ (last block may be smaller) | Uniform per-iteration cost |
| `dynamic` | Threads grab chunks of $C$ from a shared queue | Highly non-uniform work |
| `guided` | Chunk size starts large, shrinks geometrically | Non-uniform + load-balancing needed |
| `runtime` | Determined by `OMP_SCHEDULE` environment variable | Tunable without recompiling |
| `auto` | Compiler/runtime chooses | Portable "let the system decide" |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** `static` is a "meal pre-portion" — you decide who eats what before service begins. `dynamic` is a "buffet" — whoever is hungry grabs the next plate. `guided` is a "smart buffet" — large plates first, small ones later.

## 2.8 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | LaTeX Expression | Meaning |
|---------|------------------|---------|
| Speedup | $S_p \;=\; \dfrac{T_s}{T_p}$ | Ratio of serial to parallel runtime |
| Efficiency | $E_p \;=\; \dfrac{S_p}{p}$ | Fraction of ideal speedup achieved |
| Amdahl's Law | $S_p \;=\; \dfrac{1}{f \;+\; \dfrac{1-f}{p}}$ | Speedup limited by serial fraction $f$ |
| Gustafson's Law | $S_p(p) \;=\; p - f \cdot (p - 1)$ | Scaled speedup when problem grows with $p$ |
| Karp–Flatt Metric | $e \;=\; \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{S_p} \;-\; \dfrac{1}{p}}{1 \;-\; \dfrac{1}{p}}$ | Empirical serial fraction revealing parallelism overhead |
| Parallel Overhead | $T_o(p) \;=\; p \cdot T_p \;-\; T_s$ | Total time spent on non-useful parallel work |
| Cost | $C(p) \;=\; p \cdot T_p$ | Product of processors and parallel time |
| Isoefficiency | $\Theta(W) \;=\; \Theta(T_o(p))$ | Work required to keep efficiency constant as $p$ grows |
| Strong Scaling Limit | $\displaystyle\lim_{p \to \infty} S_p \;=\; \dfrac{1}{f}$ | Maximum achievable speedup (Amdahl) |
| Weak Scaling Limit | $\displaystyle\lim_{p \to \infty} S_p(p) \;=\; \infty$ (for $f<1$) | Gustafson: scaled speedup grows linearly |
| Granularity | $G \;=\; \dfrac{T_{\text{comp}}}{T_{\text{comm}}}$ | Ratio of computation to communication time |
| Barrier Sync Cost | $\tau_b \;\approx\; O(\log p)$ to $O(p)$ | Time for $p$ threads to synchronise |
| Fork-Join Total | $T_{\text{FJ}} \;=\; \tau_{\text{fork}} + \tau_{\text{par}} + \tau_{\text{join}}$ | Full parallel region wall-clock time |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used in HPC compiler optimisation passes (e.g., LLVM's OpenMP runtime), in data-centre capacity planning (AWS, Azure HPC tiers), and in autotuners like OpenTuner to choose the best thread count and chunk size for a given workload.

## 2.9 Synchronisation Primitives — When and Why

| Primitive | Granularity | Cost | Typical Use |
|-----------|-------------|------|-------------|
| `barrier` | Whole team | High | Step boundaries in iterative methods |
| `critical` | Named/unnamed region | Medium (mutex) | Updating shared data structures |
| `atomic` | Single statement | Lowest (HW instruction) | `x++`, `sum += a[i]` |
| `ordered` | Per-iteration | High | Preserving source-order side effects |
| `flush` | Memory fence | Low | Ensuring memory visibility across threads |
| `lock` (runtime API) | User-controlled | Medium | Fine-grained mutual exclusion |

> [!WARNING]
> **Anti-pattern:** Using `critical` for an accumulation inside a loop causes **serialisation of the entire loop body**, destroying parallelism. Use `reduction` or `atomic` instead.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Amdahl's Law from First Principles

**Setup:** A program has a serial fraction $f$ and a parallel fraction $(1-f)$. On $p$ processors, the parallel fraction executes in $\dfrac{1-f}{p}$ time units, while the serial fraction is unchanged.

**Step 1:** Write the serial execution time on 1 processor.

$$T_s \;=\; T_{\text{serial}} + T_{\text{parallel}} \;=\; f \cdot T_s + (1-f) \cdot T_s$$

**Step 2:** On $p$ processors, only the parallel portion benefits from parallelism.

$$T_p \;=\; f \cdot T_s \;+\; \dfrac{(1-f) \cdot T_s}{p}$$

**Step 3:** Form the speedup ratio $S_p = T_s / T_p$.

$$S_p \;=\; \dfrac{T_s}{f \cdot T_s \;+\; \dfrac{(1-f) \cdot T_s}{p}}$$

**Step 4:** Cancel $T_s$ from numerator and denominator.

$$S_p \;=\; \dfrac{1}{f \;+\; \dfrac{1-f}{p}}$$

**Step 5:** Take the limit as $p \to \infty$.

$$\lim_{p \to \infty} S_p \;=\; \dfrac{1}{f + 0} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{f}$$

**Interpretation:** Even with infinite processors, the serial fraction caps the speedup. For example, $f = 0.05$ (5% serial) caps speedup at $20\times$ no matter how many cores are used.

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical example:** $f = 0.1$, $p = 16$.

$$S_{16} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{0.1 \;+\; \dfrac{0.9}{16}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{0.1 \;+\; 0.05625} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{0.15625} \;\approx\; 6.4$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Gustafson's Scaled Speedup

**Setup:** Instead of fixing the problem size, scale the problem so that parallel runtime stays constant as $p$ grows.

**Step 1:** Let $W$ be the total work on $p$ processors and $W_s$ the serial work. Scaled workload:

$$W \;=\; W_s \;+\; p \cdot W_p$$

where $W_s = f \cdot W$ and $W_p = (1-f) \cdot W / p$.

**Step 2:** Scaled speedup is the ratio of scaled work to serial work on one processor.

$$S_{\text{scaled}}(p) \;=\; \dfrac{W_s \;+\; p \cdot W_p}{W_s}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $W_s = f \cdot W$ and $W_p = (1-f) \cdot W / p$.

$$S_{\text{scaled}}(p) \;=\; \dfrac{f \cdot W \;+\; p \cdot \dfrac{(1-f) \cdot W}{p}}{f \cdot W} \;=\; \dfrac{f \cdot W + (1-f) \cdot W}{f \cdot W}$$

**Step 4:** Simplify.

$$S_{\text{scaled}}(p) \;=\; \dfrac{W}{f \cdot W} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{f}$$

Wait — that constant value reflects *one* scaling path. The standard Gustafson derivation expresses scaled speedup in terms of $f$ and $p$ by re-parameterising the scaled time as $T' = f \cdot T_p + (1-f) \cdot p \cdot T_p / p = T_p$.

**Step 5:** Standard form.

$$S_{\text{scaled}}(p) \;=\; f \;+\; (1-f) \cdot p \;=\; p \;-\; f \cdot (p-1)$$

**Interpretation:** When the problem is allowed to grow, speedup is *linear* in $p$ minus a constant penalty. This is why "weak scaling" experiments in HPC report nearly linear speedup for well-designed codes.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Karp–Flatt Empirical Serial Fraction

**Setup:** When measured parallel runtime is worse than Amdahl predicts, parallelism overhead is suspect. The Karp–Flatt metric $e$ extracts this hidden serial fraction from measured data.

**Step 1:** Start from Amdahl's Law and solve for the parallel fraction $(1-f)$.

$$S_p \;=\; \dfrac{1}{f + (1-f)/p} \;\Longrightarrow\; f + \dfrac{1-f}{p} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{S_p}$$

**Step 2:** Multiply both sides by $p$.

$$f \cdot p + 1 - f \;=\; \dfrac{p}{S_p}$$

**Step 3:** Group $f$ terms.

$$f(p - 1) + 1 \;=\; \dfrac{p}{S_p}$$

**Step 4:** Isolate $f$.

$$f \;=\; \dfrac{\dfrac{p}{S_p} - 1}{p - 1} \;=\; \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{S_p} - \dfrac{1}{p}}{1 - \dfrac{1}{p}}$$

**Interpretation:** A growing $e$ with increasing $p$ means the code has *parallelism overhead* (synchronisation, thread creation, cache misses) — a critical diagnostic for OpenMP performance tuning.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Isoefficiency of an OpenMP Reduction Loop

**Setup:** Consider a parallel reduction over $N$ elements using $p$ threads with `schedule(static, 1)`.

**Step 1:** Serial time.

$$T_s \;=\; \Theta(N)$$

**Step 2:** Parallel time = max computation + barrier synchronisation.

$$T_p \;=\; \Theta\!\left(\dfrac{N}{p}\right) \;+\; \tau_b$$

**Step 3:** Cost (processor-time product).

$$C(p) \;=\; p \cdot T_p \;=\; \Theta(N) \;+\; p \cdot \tau_b$$

**Step 4:** Efficiency.

$$E_p \;=\; \dfrac{T_s}{C(p)} \;=\; \dfrac{\Theta(N)}{\Theta(N) + p \cdot \tau_b}$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $N$ that maintains a target efficiency $E$ (i.e., $N = \Theta(p \cdot \tau_b)$).

$$\boxed{N \;=\; \Theta(p)}$$

**Interpretation:** This loop has **linear isoefficiency** — to keep efficiency constant as $p$ doubles, the problem size $N$ must also double. This is the hallmark of a *well-balanced* OpenMP program.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — False Sharing Penalty

**Setup:** Two threads write to two different variables $x$ and $y$ that lie on the same 64-byte cache line.

**Step 1:** Without false sharing, each write hits L1 cache. Per-write latency $\tau_L$.

**Step 2:** With false sharing, every write by thread 1 invalidates thread 0's cache line (and vice versa). Total cost per write pair:

$$T_{\text{fs}} \;=\; 2 \cdot \tau_{\text{invalid}} \;+\; 2 \cdot \tau_{\text{refill}}$$

**Step 3:** Ratio of false-sharing to true cost.

$$\rho \;=\; \dfrac{T_{\text{fs}}}{2 \cdot \tau_L} \;=\; \dfrac{\tau_{\text{invalid}} + \tau_{\text{refill}}}{\tau_L}$$

**Step 4:** For typical values $\tau_{\text{invalid}} \approx 100$ ns, $\tau_{\text{refill}} \approx 50$ ns, $\tau_L \approx 1$ ns:

$$\rho \;=\; \dfrac{100 + 50}{1} \;=\; 150\times$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Trap:** Many students confuse *true sharing* (intentional data exchange between threads) with *false sharing* (cache-line ping-pong due to unrelated writes). False sharing is a *performance bug*; true sharing is *semantic correctness*.

## 3.6 Performance-Tuning Decision Matrix

| Symptom (Observed) | Likely Cause | OpenMP Fix |
|--------------------|--------------|------------|
| Speedup plateaus early | Load imbalance | `schedule(dynamic, C)` or `guided` |
| Speedup decreases after $p$ grows | Fork-join overhead too high | Fuse loops, increase granularity |
| Threads idle during `reduction` | False sharing on accumulator | Pad per-thread buffers to cache-line size |
| Inconsistent timing across runs | Non-deterministic scheduling | Use `schedule(static)` |
| Threads waiting at barrier | Stragglers | `schedule(dynamic)` or rebalance work |
| Wrong results | Race condition | Add `#pragma omp critical` / `atomic` / `reduction` |
| Memory bandwidth saturation | Working set too large | Block the iteration space, improve locality |
| NUMA imbalance | Threads on far node | `OMP_PROC_BIND=close` or `spread` |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Fork–Join Execution Model (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Program Start] --> B[Master Thread Executes Serial Code]
    B --> C{Encounter parallel region pragma omp parallel}
    C --> D[FORK Create thread team of size p]
    D --> E1[Worker Thread 0]
    D --> E2[Worker Thread 1]
    D --> E3[Worker Thread p-1]
    E1 --> F1[Execute work-sharing construct for sections single]
    E2 --> F2[Execute work-sharing construct for sections single]
    E3 --> F3[Execute work-sharing construct for sections single]
    F1 --> G{Implicit Barrier except nowait}
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[JOIN Master thread resumes alone]
    H --> B
    H --> I[Program End]
```

## 4.2 OpenMP Directive Hierarchy (Tree Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[pragma omp parallel] --> R1[pragma omp for]
    ROOT --> R2[pragma omp sections]
    ROOT --> R3[pragma omp single]
    ROOT --> R4[pragma omp master]
    ROOT --> R5[pragma omp critical name optional]
    ROOT --> R6[pragma omp barrier]
    ROOT --> R7[pragma omp atomic]
    R1 --> R1A[clauses schedule reduction private shared collapse ordered]
    R2 --> R2A[pragma omp section block 1]
    R2 --> R2B[pragma omp section block 2]
    R2 --> R2C[pragma omp section block N]
    R3 --> R3A[clauses private firstprivate nowait]
    R4 --> R4A[no implicit barrier]
```

## 4.3 Performance Tuning Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start with serial baseline] --> S2[Measure T serial on 1 thread]
    S2 --> S3[Identify hot loops via profiler]
    S3 --> S4[Insert pragma omp parallel for]
    S4 --> S5[Measure T parallel on p cores]
    S5 --> S6{Compute speedup Sp and efficiency Ep}
    S6 --> S7{Ep greater than 0.5}
    S7 -->|Yes| S8[Try schedule variants static dynamic guided]
    S7 -->|No| S9[Diagnose bottleneck]
    S9 --> S10{Load imbalance}
    S9 --> S11{False sharing}
    S9 --> S12{Synchronisation overhead}
    S9 --> S13{NUMA effects}
    S10 --> S8
    S11 --> S14[Pad accumulators to cache line]
    S12 --> S15[Use nowait or coarsen regions]
    S13 --> S16[Set OMP PROC BIND and OMP PLACES]
    S8 --> S17{Improved Ep}
    S17 -->|Yes| S18[Document final configuration]
    S17 -->|No| S9
    S14 --> S5
    S15 --> S5
    S16 --> S5
```

## 4.4 Reduction Operation — Flow Across Threads

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Initial array a 0 to N-1] --> T0[Thread 0: partial sum S0]
    A --> T1[Thread 1: partial sum S1]
    A --> T2[Thread 2: partial sum S2]
    A --> T3[Thread 3: partial sum S3]
    T0 --> BARRIER[Implicit Barrier at end of for]
    T1 --> BARRIER
    T2 --> BARRIER
    T3 --> BARRIER
    BARRIER --> RED[Reduction operator combines S0 plus S1 plus S2 plus S3]
    RED --> RESULT[Global accumulator updated atomically]
```

## 4.5 Synchronisation Primitives — Comparison Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SYNC[Synchronisation Primitives in OpenMP] --> B1[barrier whole team waits]
    SYNC --> B2[critical mutual exclusion mutex]
    SYNC --> B3[atomic hardware supported single update]
    SYNC --> B4[ordered enforce source order inside loop]
    SYNC --> B5[flush memory fence]
    SYNC --> B6[locks omp init lock omp set lock omp unset lock omp destroy lock]
    B1 --> B1A[Cost high use sparingly]
    B2 --> B2A[Cost medium prefer reduction]
    B3 --> B3A[Cost lowest for simple updates]
    B4 --> B4A[Cost high rarely needed]
    B5 --> B5A[Cost low use for visibility]
    B6 --> B6A[Cost medium flexible]
```
