<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the fork-join model of parallel execution in OpenMP. How does an implicit barrier at the end of a parallel region affect program correctness?**

**Model Answer:**

The **fork-join model** is the parallel execution paradigm adopted by OpenMP. The program begins with a single **master thread** executing the serial portions. When the master encounters a `#pragma omp parallel` directive, it **forks** — spawning a team of worker threads that execute the parallel region concurrently. At the end of the region, an **implicit barrier** enforces a **join** — all threads synchronise, and only the master continues beyond the region.

The implicit barrier is critical for **correctness** because it guarantees that all side-effects (writes to shared variables, file I/O) performed inside the parallel region are visible to the master thread before it resumes serial execution. Without this guarantee, the master might read stale or partially-updated data. The barrier is removed only when the `nowait` clause is specified, in which case the programmer takes responsibility for any required synchronisation.

**Marking scheme (3 marks):** Fork definition (1), Join definition (1), Role of implicit barrier for correctness (1).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Explain the difference between `private`, `firstprivate`, and `shared` data-sharing clauses in OpenMP with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

| Clause | Storage | Initial Value | Use Case |
|--------|---------|---------------|----------|
| `shared` | One copy for all threads | Latest value | Read-only data, output arrays |
| `private` | One copy per thread | **Undefined** | Per-thread temporaries, loop counters |
| `firstprivate` | One copy per thread | Initialised from original before the region | Per-thread accumulators needing a starting value |

**Example:**

```c
int counter = 10;        // shared by default outside parallel
int sum   = 0;           // shared

#pragma omp parallel private(counter) firstprivate(sum)
{
    // counter here is uninitialised; each thread gets its own
    // sum here is initialised to 0 (the original value)
    sum += omp_get_thread_num();
}
```

After the region, the outer `counter` is unchanged; the outer `sum` is unchanged because `sum` inside the parallel region was thread-local.

**Marking scheme (3 marks):** Three clause behaviours with correct semantics (2), example (1).

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the **fork-join parallel execution model** used by OpenMP. Discuss the role of the **runtime library** and **environment variables** in controlling OpenMP execution. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A matrix-vector multiplication program has a serial runtime of $T_s = 100$ s on one processor. The parallel portion accounts for $92\%$ of the runtime. Compute the speedup, efficiency, and Karp–Flatt serial fraction when running on $p = 8$ processors. Comment on the implications for further scaling. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4]`

**(a)** Explain the OpenMP **work-sharing constructs** `#pragma omp for`, `#pragma omp sections`, and `#pragma omp single`. For each, state when an implicit barrier is enforced and how the `nowait` clause modifies this behaviour. Give a C code snippet for parallel reduction of an array sum. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider an OpenMP parallel region with the following profile: serial runtime $T_s = 240$ s, parallel runtime on 16 threads $T_{16} = 30$ s. Compute (i) speedup, (ii) efficiency, (iii) the Karp–Flatt empirical serial fraction, and (iv) the parallel overhead $T_o(16)$. What does the empirical $e$ tell you about the scalability of this program? **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solutions

---

#### Solution to Question A(a) — Fork-Join Model (7 marks)

The **fork-join execution model** is the backbone of OpenMP's design.

**Components:**

1. **Master thread** — The single thread that exists from program start. It executes all serial code.
2. **Fork** — When the master thread encounters `#pragma omp parallel`, it creates a **team** of $p$ threads. Each thread executes the parallel region independently.
3. **Parallel region** — The structured block of code delimited by `{ ... }` immediately following the directive. Every thread in the team executes this block.
4. **Implicit barrier / Join** — At the end of the parallel region, an implicit `barrier` synchronises all threads. Once every thread reaches the barrier, only the master thread continues past the closing brace.

**Block-level diagram (already shown in Section 4.1):**

```
  Master ──┐
           ├──► [PARALLEL REGION] ──► BARRIER ──► Master ──► ...
  T0 ──────┤
  T1 ──────┤
  ...
  T(p-1)──┘
```

**Runtime library (OpenMP API functions):**

The OpenMP runtime library (`libgomp` on GCC, `libomp` on LLVM) provides functions callable from C/C++ code:

| Function | Purpose |
|----------|---------|
| `omp_get_num_threads()` | Number of threads in current team |
| `omp_get_thread_num()` | ID of the calling thread (0 to p-1) |
| `omp_set_num_threads(n)` | Set team size for upcoming parallel regions |
| `omp_get_num_procs()` | Number of processors available to the device |
| `omp_get_wtime()` | High-resolution wall-clock timer |
| `omp_in_parallel()` | Returns 1 if inside parallel region, 0 otherwise |
| `omp_init_lock` / `omp_set_lock` / `omp_destroy_lock` | User-managed locks |

**Environment variables:**

| Variable | Function |
|----------|----------|
| `OMP_NUM_THREADS` | Sets the default number of threads |
| `OMP_SCHEDULE` | Sets the default loop schedule (e.g., `dynamic,4`) |
| `OMP_DYNAMIC` | Enables/disables dynamic adjustment of thread count |
| `OMP_PROC_BIND` | Binds threads to physical cores (`true`, `close`, `spread`) |
| `OMP_PLACES` | Defines the abstract places (cores, sockets, threads) |
| `OMP_STACKSIZE` | Sets per-thread stack size |
| `OMP_WAIT_POLICY` | Active or passive waiting at barriers |

**Why they matter:** Runtime functions give programs explicit programmatic control; environment variables give users and operators deployment-time control without recompilation.

**Mark split:** Fork-Join description (2), Diagram (1), Runtime library (2), Environment variables (2).

---

#### Solution to Question A(b) — Amdahl + Karp-Flatt (7 marks)

**Given:** $T_s = 100$ s, parallel fraction $(1-f) = 0.92$, hence serial fraction $f = 0.08$, $p = 8$.

**(i) Speedup using Amdahl's Law:**

$$S_8 \;=\; \dfrac{1}{f + \dfrac{1-f}{p}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{0.08 + \dfrac{0.92}{8}}$$

Step-by-step:

$$\dfrac{0.92}{8} \;=\; 0.115$$

$$0.08 + 0.115 \;=\; 0.195$$

$$S_8 \;=\; \dfrac{1}{0.195} \;\approx\; 5.13$$

**(ii) Efficiency:**

$$E_8 \;=\; \dfrac{S_8}{p} \;=\; \dfrac{5.13}{8} \;\approx\; 0.641 \;=\; 64.1\%$$

**(iii) Karp–Flatt empirical serial fraction:**

$$e \;=\; \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{S_p} - \dfrac{1}{p}}{1 - \dfrac{1}{p}}$$

Step-by-step:

$$\dfrac{1}{S_8} \;=\; 0.195$$

$$\dfrac{1}{p} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{8} \;=\; 0.125$$

$$1 - \dfrac{1}{p} \;=\; 0.875$$

$$e \;=\; \dfrac{0.195 - 0.125}{0.875} \;=\; \dfrac{0.070}{0.875} \;\approx\; 0.080$$

**(iv) Comment on further scaling:**

The Karp–Flitt value $e \approx 0.080$ matches the declared serial fraction, indicating *negligible parallelism overhead*. The maximum possible speedup with $p \to \infty$ is:

$$S_{\infty} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{f} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{0.08} \;=\; 12.5\times$$

Beyond $p = 12$ or so, additional cores yield diminishing returns. **Recommendation:** Reduce the serial fraction $f$ by identifying serial I/O, serial sections, or critical-path bottlenecks before scaling further.

**Mark split:** Amdahl application (2), Efficiency (1), Karp–Flatt (2), Interpretation (2).

---

#### Solution to Question B(a) — Work-Sharing Constructs + Reduction Code (7 marks)

**`#pragma omp for`** — Distributes the iterations of the immediately following `for` loop among the threads in the team.

- **Implicit barrier:** Yes, at the end of the loop (unless `nowait` is given).
- **Restrictions:** Loop counter must be of integer or pointer type; loop variable must be `private`; loop bounds, step, and body must be invariant with respect to the loop variable.

```c
#pragma omp parallel for
for (int i = 0; i < N; i++) a[i] = b[i] + c[i];
```

**`#pragma omp sections`** — Distributes independent code blocks (each marked `#pragma omp section`) among threads. Each section is executed exactly once by one thread.

- **Implicit barrier:** Yes, at the end of the `sections` construct.
- **Use case:** Task-parallel work where the units of work are distinct functions.

```c
#pragma omp parallel sections
{
    #pragma omp section
    { compute_part_A(); }
    #pragma omp section
    { compute_part_B(); }
    #pragma omp section
    { compute_part_C(); }
}
```

**`#pragma omp single`** — The enclosed block is executed by **exactly one thread** (typically thread 0, but unspecified); all other threads in the team wait at the implicit barrier.

- **Implicit barrier:** Yes (unless `nowait` is given).
- **Use case:** Initialisation code, I/O, or work that must not be duplicated.

```c
#pragma omp parallel
{
    #pragma omp single
    printf("Setup done by thread %d\n", omp_get_thread_num());
    // ... parallel work ...
}
```

**`nowait` clause:** Removes the implicit barrier of any work-sharing construct. Threads that finish early can immediately proceed to the next statement without waiting for slower threads. The programmer must ensure that no data race results.

**Reduction code snippet:**

```c
#include <omp.h>
#include <stdio.h>

int main(void) {
    const int N = 1000000;
    double a[N], sum = 0.0;
    for (int i = 0; i < N; i++) a[i] = (double)(i + 1);

    #pragma omp parallel for reduction(+:sum) schedule(static)
    for (int i = 0; i < N; i++) {
        sum += a[i];
    }

    printf("Sum = %.2f\n", sum);   // Expected 500000500000
    return 0;
}
```

Each thread accumulates into its own private `sum`, and at the implicit barrier the partial sums are combined using `+`.

**Mark split:** `for` (1.5), `sections` (1.5), `single` (1), `nowait` (1), code (2).

---

#### Solution to Question B(b) — Performance Metrics Computation (7 marks)

**Given:** $T_s = 240$ s, $T_{16} = 30$ s, $p = 16$.

**(i) Speedup:**

$$S_{16} \;=\; \dfrac{T_s}{T_{16}} \;=\; \dfrac{240}{30} \;=\; 8.0$$

**(ii) Efficiency:**

$$E_{16} \;=\; \dfrac{S_{16}}{p} \;=\; \dfrac{8.0}{16} \;=\; 0.50 \;=\; 50\%$$

**(iii) Karp–Flatt empirical serial fraction:**

$$e \;=\; \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{S_p} - \dfrac{1}{p}}{1 - \dfrac{1}{p}} \;=\; \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{8.0} - \dfrac{1}{16}}{1 - \dfrac{1}{16}}$$

Step-by-step:

$$\dfrac{1}{8.0} \;=\; 0.125$$

$$\dfrac{1}{16} \;=\; 0.0625$$

$$1 - \dfrac{1}{16} \;=\; 0.9375$$

$$e \;=\; \dfrac{0.125 - 0.0625}{0.9375} \;=\; \dfrac{0.0625}{0.9375} \;\approx\; 0.0667$$

**(iv) Parallel overhead:**

$$T_o(16) \;=\; p \cdot T_p - T_s \;=\; 16 \cdot 30 - 240 \;=\; 480 - 240 \;=\; 240 \text{ s}$$

**Interpretation:**

- The empirical $e \approx 0.067$ reveals that the *effective* serial fraction (including all parallel overheads such as fork-join, barrier synchronisation, cache misses, false sharing) is about $6.7\%$.
- With **only 50% efficiency on 16 cores**, this program is not scaling well. The overhead $T_o(16) = 240$ s equals the original serial runtime, meaning every thread spends on average $15$ s on overhead alone.
- **Diagnostic action:** Profile to identify whether overhead is due to (a) thread creation in many small parallel regions, (b) excessive barriers, (c) lock contention in `critical` regions, or (d) NUMA effects.

**Mark split:** Speedup (1), Efficiency (1), Karp–Flatt (2), Overhead (1), Interpretation (2).

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Losing Pitfalls in OpenMP Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing `private` and `firstprivate`** — `private` gives an *uninitialised* copy; students often claim it inherits the original value. ❌ Lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting that `reduction` creates a *new* variable** — The original shared variable is *not* modified inside the parallel region; the combined value is assigned back *after* the barrier. ❌ Lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Wrong Karp–Flatt algebra** — Skipping the `1 - 1/p` denominator or computing `1/S_p - 1/p` without normalising. ❌ Lose 2 marks.
> 4. **Stating "speedup is linear with p"** — This violates Amdahl's Law. Always include the serial fraction $f$. ❌ Lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Missing the `nowait` clause explanation** — Examiners expect explicit mention of when the barrier is removed and why synchronisation must be re-established manually. ❌ Lose 1 mark.
> 6. **Mixing up `critical` and `atomic`** — `atomic` works only for single assignments of the form `x op= expr` or `x++`/`x--`; complex blocks need `critical`. ❌ Lose 1 mark.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**
>
> - **OpenMP** = directive-based shared-memory parallel programming API.
> - **Core model** = **fork-join**: master thread forks a team at `parallel`, joins at implicit barrier.
> - **Three pillars** = compiler directives (`#pragma omp`), runtime library (`omp_*`), environment variables (`OMP_*`).
> - **Default scope** outside a parallel region = `shared`; loop iteration variable = `private`.
> - **Work-sharing constructs** = `for`, `sections`, `single` — each has an implicit barrier unless `nowait`.
> - **`reduction(op:var)`** = per-thread private accumulator combined at barrier; supports `+ - * / & | ^ && || min max`.
> - **`schedule` clause** = `static` (predictable), `dynamic` (load-balanced), `guided` (compromise), `runtime` (env-configurable).
> - **Synchronisation primitives** = `barrier` (whole team), `critical` (mutex), `atomic` (HW atomic), `ordered` (source order), `flush` (memory fence).
> - **Amdahl's Law** = $S_p = \dfrac{1}{f + (1-f)/p}$; upper bound = $1/f$.
> - **Gustafson's Law** = $S(p) = p - f(p-1)$ — linear in $p$ for weak scaling.
> - **Karp–Flitt metric** = $e = \dfrac{1/S_p - 1/p}{1 - 1/p}$ — exposes hidden serial overhead.
> - **Efficiency** = $S_p / p$; good programs maintain $E_p > 0.5$ across the scaling range.
> - **Isoefficiency** of reduction loop = $\Theta(p)$ — work must scale linearly with thread count.
> - **False sharing** = unrelated writes to same cache line → 100×+ penalty; pad per-thread buffers.
> - **NUMA awareness** = use `OMP_PROC_BIND=close|spread` and `OMP_PLACES=cores|sockets`.
> - **Compiler flag** = `gcc -fopenmp prog.c -o prog`; runtime = `OMP_NUM_THREADS=8 ./prog`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parallel Programming with MPI: Introduction, Message Passing, and Advanced Features
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST759 - PARALLEL ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Parallel Programming with MPI: Introduction, Message Passing, and Advanced Features

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of MPI

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Message Passing Interface (MPI)** is a **standardized, portable, language-independent message-passing library specification** (not a programming language or compiler) designed to function on a wide variety of parallel computing architectures. It defines the **syntax and semantics of core library routines** that allow processes executing on single or multiple computers to communicate by sending and receiving messages.

MPI is the **de-facto industry standard** for distributed-memory parallel programming and forms the backbone of nearly all large-scale High-Performance Computing (HPC) applications running on supercomputers today. It is maintained by the **MPI Forum** (an open standardization body) and follows a **SPMD (Single Program Multiple Data)** execution model where every process runs the same program but operates on different data segments.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction — KTU Exam Favourite**
> - **MPI** is a **library specification**, not a language.
> - It is implemented by libraries such as **OpenMPI, MPICH, Intel MPI, MVAPICH2, MS-MPI**.
> - It supports **C, C++, Fortran**, and bindings for Python (`mpi4py`) and Java.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Postal Network"

Imagine a parallel program as a **large corporate office** where every employee (process) sits in a **separate cabin** (a private memory space with no shared variables). No employee can simply reach over and borrow a pen from a colleague — they are physically isolated.

To collaborate, employees must **send letters through the postal system**:
- The **letter** = the **message buffer** (data to share).
- The **envelope** = the **MPI Datatype** and **Communicator**.
- The **recipient's address** = the **rank** (process ID) of the destination process.
- The **postal rules** = the **MPI communication functions** (`MPI_Send`, `MPI_Recv`).

The postal department guarantees delivery (or notifies failure), routes letters optimally, and provides special services like **broadcasting a memo to everyone at once** (collective communication). This is precisely how MPI works.

### 1.3 Anatomy of an MPI Program

Every MPI program in **C** contains **six mandatory building blocks**:

| Block | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `#include <mpi.h>` | Includes the MPI header file with all declarations |
| `MPI_Init(&argc, &argv)` | Initializes the MPI execution environment |
| `MPI_Comm_size(MPI_COMM_WORLD, &size)` | Determines the total number of processes |
| `MPI_Comm_rank(MPI_COMM_WORLD, &rank)` | Determines the rank (ID) of the calling process |
| `MPI_Finalize()` | Terminates the MPI execution environment |
| **Parallel work + Communication calls** | The actual computation |

### 1.4 Canonical "Hello World" MPI Program

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <mpi.h>

int main(int argc, char *argv[]) {
    int rank, size;

    MPI_Init(&argc, &argv);                       // Initialize MPI
    MPI_Comm_rank(MPI_COMM_WORLD, &rank);         // Get my ID
    MPI_Comm_size(MPI_COMM_WORLD, &size);         // Get total processes

    printf("Hello from process %d of %d\n", rank, size);

    MPI_Finalize();                               // Shut down MPI
    return 0;
}
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compilation & Execution Pattern (Exam Tip)**
> - **Compile:** `mpicc -o hello hello.c`
> - **Run with 4 processes:** `mpirun -np 4 ./hello`
> - Output order is **non-deterministic** because processes execute asynchronously.

### 1.5 Why MPI? — Advantages & Limitations

**Advantages:**
- **Portability** across distributed-memory systems (laptops to petascale clusters).
- **Scalability** to **hundreds of thousands of processes**.
- **Rich functionality** (point-to-point, collective, one-sided, parallel I/O).
- **Mature ecosystem** with debuggers (TotalView), profilers (TAU, Score-P), and tracing tools.

**Limitations:**
- Programmer **must explicitly manage data distribution** and movement.
- **Higher learning curve** than shared-memory models (OpenMP).
- **Communication overhead** can dominate if used inefficiently.
- **Deadlock risk** if send/receive order is mismatched.

## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 MPI Programming Model — The SPMD Foundation

MPI is founded on the **SPMD (Single Program Multiple Data)** model:

1. **Single Executable File** — All processes load and execute the **same binary**.
2. **Multiple Data Streams** — Each process gets its own address space, stack, heap, and program counter.
3. **Process Differentiation** — Processes branch using `if (rank == 0) { ... }` constructs to perform different work.
4. **Communication via Messages** — Data sharing happens **only through explicit message-passing routines**.

### 2.2 Communicators — The Hidden Backbone

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Communicator** in MPI is a named ordered group of processes that defines the **scope** of communication. Every communication call must specify a communicator.

| Concept | Description |
|---|---|
| **`MPI_COMM_WORLD`** | The **default communicator** containing ALL processes started by `mpirun` |
| **Group** | The set of processes in a communicator |
| **Rank** | A unique integer ID (0 to size-1) assigned to each process **within** its communicator |
| **`MPI_Comm_create`** | Creates a new communicator for a subgroup |
| **`MPI_Comm_split`** | Splits an existing communicator into multiple sub-communicators by color/key |

> [!NOTE]
> A rank of `0` in `MPI_COMM_WORLD` is conventionally called the **"master" or "root"** process — but this is purely by convention, not enforced by MPI.

### 2.3 Point-to-Point Communication — The Core Engine

Point-to-point operations involve **exactly one sender and one receiver**.

#### 2.3.1 Blocking Communication

**Blocking Send — `MPI_Send`:**
- The call **returns only after** the send buffer can be safely reused by the application.
- Implementation may **copy data to an internal system buffer** (buffered mode) or **wait** until the receiver starts receiving (synchronous mode).

**Blocking Receive — `MPI_Recv`:**
- The call returns only after the receive buffer contains the message.
- A matching receive **must be posted** for the communication to succeed.

```c
int data = 42;
if (rank == 0) {
    MPI_Send(&data, 1, MPI_INT, 1, 0, MPI_COMM_WORLD);
    printf("Process 0 sent %d to process 1\n", data);
} else if (rank == 1) {
    MPI_Recv(&data, 1, MPI_INT, 0, 0, MPI_COMM_WORLD, MPI_STATUS_IGNORE);
    printf("Process 1 received %d\n", data);
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Deadlock Warning:** If process 0 does `MPI_Send` and process 1 also does `MPI_Send` to 0 **before** posting its `MPI_Recv`, the system may **deadlock** because both are waiting for the other. KTU examiners frequently test this trap.

#### 2.3.2 Send Modes — The Four Flavors

| Mode | Function | Completion Semantics |
|---|---|---|
| **Standard** | `MPI_Send` | Returns when buffer is free (may or may not mean the message has reached the receiver) |
| **Buffered** | `MPI_Bsend` | Returns immediately after copying to an **MPI-internal buffer** (user allocates with `MPI_Buffer_attach`) |
| **Synchronous** | `MPI_Ssend` | Returns only after the **receive has started** at the destination |
| **Ready** | `MPI_Rsend` | May be called **only if a matching receive is already posted** (unsafe otherwise) |

#### 2.3.3 Non-Blocking Communication

Non-blocking calls (`MPI_Isend`, `MPI_Irecv`) **return immediately**, allowing computation to overlap with communication. A separate **wait operation** (`MPI_Wait`, `MPI_Waitany`, `MPI_Waitall`, `MPI_Waitall`) confirms completion.

```c
MPI_Request request;
int data = 100;
MPI_Isend(&data, 1, MPI_INT, 1, 0, MPI_COMM_WORLD, &request);
// ... do useful work here while message is in transit ...
MPI_Wait(&request, MPI_STATUS_IGNORE);
```

### 2.4 Collective Communication — One-to-Many, Many-to-One, Many-to-Many

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Collective operations** involve **all processes in a communicator** and are **synchronous barriers** — all processes must call the routine before any can proceed.

| Operation | Function | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Barrier** | `MPI_Barrier` | Synchronizes all processes (no data movement) |
| **Broadcast** | `MPI_Bcast` | One-to-all: root sends same data to everyone |
| **Scatter** | `MPI_Scatter` | One-to-all: root sends **different** chunks to each process |
| **Gather** | `MPI_Gather` | All-to-one: each process sends data to root |
| **Allgather** | `MPI_Allgather` | All-to-all gather — every process gets the assembled result |
| **Reduce** | `MPI_Reduce` | All-to-one reduction (sum, max, min, etc.) |
| **Allreduce** | `MPI_Allreduce` | All-to-all reduction — every process gets the final result |
| **Alltoall** | `MPI_Alltoall` | Each process sends distinct data to every other process |
| **Scan** | `MPI_Scan` | Prefix reduction across ranks |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Speedup** | $S_p = \dfrac{T_1}{T_p}$ | dimensionless | $T_1$ = sequential time, $T_p$ = parallel time |
| 2 | **Efficiency** | $E_p = \dfrac{S_p}{p} = \dfrac{T_1}{p \cdot T_p}$ | dimensionless | $p$ = number of processes |
| 3 | **Cost** | $C_p = p \cdot T_p$ | seconds | Total resource-time product |
| 4 | **Communication Time (LogP model)** | $T_{comm} = o + (m \cdot L) + g$ | seconds | $o$=latency overhead, $L$=latency, $g$=gap, $m$=message size |
| 5 | **Bandwidth** | $B = \dfrac{m}{T_{comm}}$ | bytes/sec | m = message size |
| 6 | **Amdahl's Law** | $S_{max} = \dfrac{1}{f_s + \dfrac{f_p}{p}}$ | dimensionless | $f_s$ = serial fraction, $f_p = 1-f_s$ |
| 7 | **Gustafson's Law** | $S(p) = p - f_s \cdot (p - 1)$ | dimensionless | Scaled speedup with problem size |
| 8 | **Isoefficiency** | $\Theta(p) = \dfrac{T_p \cdot p}{T_1/p}$ | scaled work | Work to maintain constant efficiency |
| 9 | **Total Parallel Time** | $T_p = T_{comp} + T_{comm} + T_{idle}$ | seconds | Includes sync and waiting time |
| 10 | **Communication-to-Computation Ratio** | $\text{RCR} = \dfrac{T_{comm}}{T_{comp}}$ | dimensionless | Should be << 1 for scalability |

> [!NOTE]
> **LogP Model Parameters (Frequently Tested):**
> - **L** — Latency: upper bound on time to deliver a message.
> - **o** — Overhead: time the processor is busy sending/receiving.
> - **g** — Gap: minimum time between successive send/receive operations.
> - **P** — Number of processors.

### 2.6 Engineering Utility of MPI

MPI powers applications across virtually every computational science domain:
- **Climate & Weather Modelling** (CESM, WRF, IFS)
- **Computational Fluid Dynamics** (ANSYS Fluent, OpenFOAM)
- **Molecular Dynamics** (GROMACS, NAMD, LAMMPS)
- **Astrophysics & Cosmology** (Gadget, ENZO)
- **Deep Learning** (Horovod, distributed TensorFlow/PyTorch)
- **Cryptanalysis & Lattice-Based Simulation** (MILC)

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Latency-Bandwidth Communication Cost

For a message of size $m$ bytes sent between two processes across a network, the **total communication time** can be modelled as:

$$
T_{comm}(m) = \alpha + \beta \cdot m
$$

**Where:**
- $\alpha$ = **latency** (fixed startup cost in seconds).
- $\beta$ = **inverse bandwidth** (seconds per byte).
- $m$ = message size (bytes).

**Detailed Step-by-Step Derivation for a Reduce Operation:**

Consider an `MPI_Reduce` operation on $n$ values distributed across $p$ processes. A naïve implementation requires $\log_2(p)$ communication stages using a **binomial tree pattern**.

**Step 1 — Number of stages:**
$$
s = \log_2(p)
$$
Each stage halves the number of active participants.

**Step 2 — Work per stage:**
At stage $k$, there are $\dfrac{p}{2^k}$ active pairs. Each pair sends $\dfrac{n}{p}$ values (assumed equal distribution) to its partner. The communication cost of that stage is:
$$
T_k = \alpha + \beta \cdot \dfrac{n}{p} \cdot \text{datatype\_size}
$$

> For simplicity, assume the values are doubles (8 bytes), and the total reduction payload at each stage remains $\dfrac{n \cdot 8}{p}$ bytes.

**Step 3 — Total Reduce Time (assuming no contention):**
$$
T_{reduce} = \sum_{k=0}^{s-1} T_k = \log_2(p) \cdot \left( \alpha + \beta \cdot \dfrac{8n}{p} \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example:**
Let $p = 8$ processes, $n = 1024$ doubles, $\alpha = 10\,\mu s$, $\beta = 0.01\,\mu s/\text{byte}$.

$$
\log_2(8) = 3
$$

$$
\text{Bytes per stage} = \dfrac{1024 \cdot 8}{8} = 1024 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
T_{reduce} = 3 \cdot \left( 10 + 0.01 \cdot 1024 \right)
$$

$$
T_{reduce} = 3 \cdot (10 + 10.24) = 3 \cdot 20.24 = 60.72\,\mu s
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Parallel Time for Vector Dot Product

**Problem:** Compute $\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} A_i \cdot B_i$ on $p$ processes.

**Step 1 — Local Computation:**
Each process handles $n/p$ elements.
$$
T_{comp} = \dfrac{n}{p} \cdot t_{multadd}
$$
where $t_{multadd}$ is the per-element cost.

**Step 2 — Local Reduction:**
Each process reduces its $n/p$ partial sums into a single scalar in $\log_2(n/p)$ steps.
$$
T_{local\_red} = \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{n}{p}\right) \cdot t_{add}
$$

**Step 3 — Global Reduction via `MPI_Allreduce`:**
Using a binomial tree over $p$ processes:
$$
T_{allreduce} = \log_2(p) \cdot \left( \alpha + \beta \cdot \text{sizeof(double)} \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Total Parallel Time:**
$$
T_p = \dfrac{n}{p} \cdot t_{multadd} + \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{n}{p}\right) \cdot t_{add} + \log_2(p) \cdot (\alpha + 8\beta)
$$

**Step 5 — Speedup:**
$$
S_p = \dfrac{T_1}{T_p} = \dfrac{n \cdot t_{multadd}}{T_p}
$$

**Step 6 — Efficiency:**
$$
E_p = \dfrac{S_p}{p} = \dfrac{n \cdot t_{multadd}}{p \cdot T_p}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — MPI Communication Functions

| Function | Type | Data Direction | Synchronization | Blocking? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `MPI_Send` | Point-to-point | One-to-one | Yes | Yes |
| `MPI_Recv` | Point-to-point | One-to-one | Yes | Yes |
| `MPI_Isend` | Point-to-point | One-to-one | No | Non-blocking |
| `MPI_Irecv` | Point-to-point | One-to-one | No | Non-blocking |
| `MPI_Bcast` | Collective | One-to-all | Implicit | Yes |
| `MPI_Scatter` | Collective | One-to-all (chunks) | Implicit | Yes |
| `MPI_Gather` | Collective | All-to-one | Implicit | Yes |
| `MPI_Allgather` | Collective | All-to-all (gather) | Implicit | Yes |
| `MPI_Reduce` | Collective | All-to-one (op) | Implicit | Yes |
| `MPI_Allreduce` | Collective | All-to-all (op) | Implicit | Yes |
| `MPI_Alltoall` | Collective | All-to-all (chunks) | Implicit | Yes |
| `MPI_Barrier` | Collective | None | Implicit | Yes |
| `MPI_Scan` | Collective | Prefix reduction | Implicit | Yes |
| `MPI_Wait` | Synchronization | N/A | Explicit | Blocks until request completes |
| `MPI_Waitall` | Synchronization | N/A | Explicit | Blocks for array of requests |
| `MPI_Gatherv` | Collective (Vector) | All-to-one (variable) | Implicit | Yes |
| `MPI_Scatterv` | Collective (Vector) | One-to-all (variable) | Implicit | Yes |

### 3.4 The LogP Model in Detail — A 4-Parameter Performance Model

For a message of size $m$ between two processors, the cost in the **LogP model** is:

$$
T_{LogP}(m) = o + L + o + g \cdot (m-1)
$$

**For a single word** ($m=1$):
$$
T_{single} = 2o + L
$$

**For an $m$-word message** assuming pipelined transmission:
$$
T_{msg}(m) = (m - 1) \cdot g + 2o + L
$$

**Step-by-step explanation:**

**Step 1 — Sender initiates send:** Takes $o$ time (overhead) to package the message. CPU is unavailable for other work during this time.

**Step 2 — Message travels over network:** Latency $L$. The CPU is now free on the sender side.

**Step 3 — Receiver receives:** Takes $o$ time. The receiver CPU is busy for $o$.

**Step 4 — Pipelining for multi-word messages:** The sender can issue the next message after a **gap** of $g$ time. For $m$ words:
- $m-1$ gaps occur between successive word transmissions.
- Total pipeline time = $(m-1) \cdot g$.

**Step 5 — Combine:**
$$
T_{msg}(m) = 2o + L + (m-1)g
$$

**Worked Example:** Send 1000 doubles (8000 bytes) with parameters $o = 2\,\mu s$, $L = 5\,\mu s$, $g = 0.001\,\mu s/\text{byte}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The gap parameter $g$ is sometimes expressed **per byte**, sometimes **per word**. Always clarify the unit on the KTU exam.

$$
T_{msg} = 2(2) + 5 + 999 \cdot 0.001 = 4 + 5 + 0.999 = 9.999\,\mu s
$$

For 1 MB messages, the gap term dominates:
$$
T_{1MB} \approx 4 + 5 + (1024 \cdot 1024 - 1) \cdot 0.001 \approx 1049\,\mu s
$$

This demonstrates the **fundamental bandwidth limit** of any network.

## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 MPI Program Lifecycle — High-Level Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start mpirun with P processes] --> B[Each process loads same executable]
    B --> C[MPI_Init argc argv]
    C --> D[Determine rank and size]
    D --> E{Rank Decision}
    E -- rank 0 --> F[Master Branch]
    E -- rank 1 to P-1 --> G[Worker Branch]
    F --> H[Send Data via MPI_Send]
    G --> I[Receive Data via MPI_Recv]
    H --> J[Perform Local Computation]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Collective Operations if needed]
    K --> L[MPI_Barrier or MPI_Finalize prep]
    L --> M[MPI_Finalize]
    M --> N[Each process exits independently]
    N --> O[mpirun collects and terminates]
```

### 4.2 MPI Communication Hierarchy — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AppLayer["APPLICATION LAYER"]
        App1[User Code in C or Fortran]
        App2[Domain Decomposition Logic]
        App3[Algorithm Implementation]
    end

    subgraph CommLayer["MESSAGING LAYER"]
        MPIColl[Collective Routines - MPI_Bcast - MPI_Reduce - MPI_Allgather]
        MPIPP[Point to Point Routines - MPI_Send - MPI_Recv - MPI_Isend - MPI_Irecv]
        MPIOne[One Sided Routines - MPI_Put - MPI_Get - MPI_Accumulate]
        MPIIO[Parallel IO - MPI_File_open - MPI_File_read - MPI_File_write]
    end

    subgraph InfraLayer["INFRASTRUCTURE LAYER"]
        Comm1[Communicator Management - MPI_Comm_create - MPI_Comm_split]
        Comm2[Datatype Engine - MPI_Type_contiguous - MPI_Type_vector]
        Comm3[Topology Interface - MPI_Cart_create - MPI_Graph_create]
        Comm4[Virtual Process Topologies]
    end

    subgraph HWLayer["HARDWARE LAYER"]
        Net1[High Speed Interconnect - InfiniBand - Omni Path - Ethernet]
        Net2[Node Level - Shared Memory - NVLink - CXL]
        Net3[CPU Cores and Memory Channels]
    end

    App1 --> MPIColl
    App2 --> MPIPP
    App3 --> MPIOne
    App3 --> MPIIO

    MPIColl --> Comm1
    MPIPP --> Comm1
    MPIOne --> Comm2
    MPIIO --> Comm2

    Comm1 --> Comm3
    Comm2 --> Comm4
    Comm3 --> Net1
    Comm4 --> Net2
    Net1 --> Net3
    Net2 --> Net3
```

### 4.3 Point-to-Point Communication Pattern

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant P0 as Process 0 (Sender)
    participant P1 as Process 1 (Receiver)
    participant P2 as Process 2 (Idle)

    Note over P0: MPI_Send initiated
    P0->>P0: Copy to system buffer OR wait
    P0->>P1: Network transmission
    Note over P1: MPI_Recv posted
    P1->>P1: Receive into buffer
    P1-->>P0: Acknowledge (implicit)
    Note over P2: No involvement in this point-to-point exchange
    Note over P0,P1: Both calls return control to application
```

### 4.4 Collective Operations — Broadcast and Reduce Patterns

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BCAST["MPI_Bcast Pattern"]
        direction LR
        Root[Rank 0] -- Stage 0 --> P1[Rank 1]
        Root -- Stage 0 --> P3[Rank 3]
        Root -- Stage 0 --> P5[Rank 5]
        Root -- Stage 0 --> P7[Rank 7]
        P1 -- Stage 1 --> P2[Rank 2]
        P1 -- Stage 1 --> P4[Rank 4]
        P3 -- Stage 1 --> P6[Rank 6]
        P5 -- Stage 1 --> P8[Rank 7 receiver]
    end

    subgraph REDUCE["MPI_Reduce Pattern"]
        direction LR
        R0[Rank 0 Local] -- Stage 1 --> P10[Rank 1 Partial]
        R2[Rank 2 Local] -- Stage 1 --> P11[Rank 3 Partial]
        R4[Rank 4 Local] -- Stage 1 --> P12[Rank 5 Partial]
        R6[Rank 6 Local] -- Stage 1 --> P13[Rank 7 Partial]
        P10 -- Stage 2 --> Final0[Rank 0 Final]
        P11 -- Stage 2 --> Final0
        P12 -- Stage 2 --> Final1[Rank 4 Final]
        P13 -- Stage 2 --> Final1
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Communication Tree Properties:** A binomial tree for $p$ processes uses exactly $\log_2(p)$ stages and $p-1$ communication operations. This is **optimal** for tree-based collectives.

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1: Define Message Passing Interface (MPI). List its key features.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** MPI is a standardized, language-independent **message-passing library specification** used for distributed-memory parallel programming, where processes communicate by explicitly sending and receiving messages via library routines.

> **Key Features (2 marks):**
> 1. **Portability** — runs on laptops to petascale clusters.
> 2. **SPMD model** — single program, multiple data streams.
> 3. **Rich communication** — point-to-point, collective, one-sided, parallel I/O.
> 4. **Standardized** — maintained by the MPI Forum across versions (MPI 1.0 → MPI 4.0).
> 5. **No shared memory** — every process has its own address space.
> 6. **Asynchronous support** — non-blocking calls overlap communication with computation.

---

**Question 2: Differentiate between `MPI_Send` and `MPI_Isend`.** `[KTU University Exam - July 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | `MPI_Send` (Blocking) | `MPI_Isend` (Non-blocking) |
|---|---|---|
| **Return behaviour** | Returns only when send buffer can be safely reused | Returns **immediately** with an `MPI_Request` handle |
| **Overlap with computation** | No — caller is blocked | Yes — caller can do other work in parallel |
| **Completion check** | Implicit on return | Explicit via `MPI_Wait`, `MPI_Test`, etc. |
| **Deadlock risk** | Higher (if no matching receive) | Lower (does not wait) |
| **Use case** | Simple scripts, no overlap needed | Performance-critical loops with overlap |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the **MPI programming model** with its architecture. Describe **communicators**, **ranks**, and the role of `MPI_Init` and `MPI_Finalize`. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write an MPI program in C to compute the **sum of $n$ integers** distributed across $p$ processes using `MPI_Reduce`. Explain the output for $p=4$ and $n=8$ where each process holds 2 numbers: Process 0 has [1,2], Process 1 has [3,4], Process 2 has [5,6], Process 3 has [7,8]. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) MPI Programming Model (7 Marks):**

1. **SPMD Foundation (2 marks):** MPI adopts the SPMD paradigm — every process runs an identical executable but operates on distinct data. The number of processes is fixed at launch time by `mpirun -np P`. The processes have **independent address spaces**; no shared global variables exist.

2. **Communicators (2 marks):** A communicator defines a communication universe. `MPI_COMM_WORLD` is the default communicator containing all `P` processes. Custom communicators may be built using `MPI_Comm_create` or `MPI_Comm_split` for sub-organization (e.g., row/column communicators in a 2D decomposition).

3. **Ranks (1 mark):** Each process receives a unique rank (an integer from $0$ to $P-1$) within each communicator. The root process conventionally has rank $0$.

4. **Initialization and Finalization (2 marks):**
   - `MPI_Init(&argc, &argv)` — sets up the MPI runtime, allocates internal resources, and must be the **first MPI call**. No other MPI call may precede it (except `MPI_Initialized`).
   - `MPI_Finalize()` — terminates the MPI environment, performs cleanup, and must be the **last MPI call**. After it returns, no MPI routine may be invoked.

**(b) Sum Reduction Program (7 Marks):**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <mpi.h>

int main(int argc, char *argv[]) {
    int rank, size, n = 8;
    int local_data[2];
    int local_sum, global_sum;

    MPI_Init(&argc, &argv);
    MPI_Comm_rank(MPI_COMM_WORLD, &rank);
    MPI_Comm_size(MPI_COMM_WORLD, &size);

    // Each process initializes its local data
    local_data[0] = 2 * rank + 1;       // 1, 3, 5, 7
    local_data[1] = 2 * rank + 2;       // 2, 4, 6, 8

    // Local sum computation
    local_sum = local_data[0] + local_data[1];

    printf("Process %d: local_sum = %d\n", rank, local_sum);

    // Global reduction to root (rank 0)
    MPI_Reduce(&local_sum, &global_sum, 1, MPI_INT, MPI_SUM, 0, MPI_COMM_WORLD);

    if (rank == 0) {
        printf("Total sum = %d\n", global_sum);
    }

    MPI_Finalize();
    return 0;
}
```

**Output for $p=4$, $n=8$ (3 marks):**
```
Process 0: local_sum = 3
Process 1: local_sum = 7
Process 2: local_sum = 11
Process 3: local_sum = 15
Total sum = 36
```

The expected sum is $\dfrac{n(n+1)}{2} = \dfrac{8 \cdot 9}{2} = 36$ ✓. The order of process prints is **non-deterministic** due to asynchronous execution, but the root's `Total sum = 36` is always printed last.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - July 2022]`

**(a)** What are **blocking and non-blocking point-to-point communication** in MPI? Explain with suitable function signatures and a C program demonstrating non-blocking communication with overlap. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **LogP model** of parallel communication. Derive the time to send an $m$-word message and compute the total time for $p=8$ processes executing a binomial-tree `MPI_Allreduce` over 1024 doubles, given $\alpha=10\,\mu s$, $\beta=0.01\,\mu s/\text{byte}$, $L=5\,\mu s$, $g=0.001\,\mu s/\text{byte}$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Blocking vs Non-Blocking Communication (7 Marks):**

**Blocking (3 marks):** A blocking call **does not return** until the operation has completed and the buffer is safe to reuse. This does not necessarily mean the message has reached the receiver — only that the local buffer is free.

```c
int MPI_Send(const void *buf, int count, MPI_Datatype datatype,
             int dest, int tag, MPI_Comm comm);

int MPI_Recv(void *buf, int count, MPI_Datatype datatype,
             int source, int tag, MPI_Comm comm, MPI_Status *status);
```

**Non-Blocking (3 marks):** A non-blocking call returns **immediately**, even before the operation is complete. It returns a request handle that can be polled or waited on later.

```c
int MPI_Isend(const void *buf, int count, MPI_Datatype datatype,
              int dest, int tag, MPI_Comm comm, MPI_Request *request);

int MPI_Irecv(void *buf, int count, MPI_Datatype datatype,
              int source, int tag, MPI_Comm comm, MPI_Request *request);
```

**Example with Overlap (1 mark):**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <mpi.h>

int main(int argc, char *argv[]) {
    int rank, data = 100;
    MPI_Request req;

    MPI_Init(&argc, &argv);
    MPI_Comm_rank(MPI_COMM_WORLD, &rank);

    if (rank == 0) {
        MPI_Isend(&data, 1, MPI_INT, 1, 0, MPI_COMM_WORLD, &req);
        // Do useful computation here while message is in flight
        printf("Process 0 doing work while sending...\n");
        MPI_Wait(&req, MPI_STATUS_IGNORE);
    } else if (rank == 1) {
        MPI_Irecv(&data, 1, MPI_INT, 0, 0, MPI_COMM_WORLD, &req);
        MPI_Wait(&req, MPI_STATUS_IGNORE);
        printf("Process 1 received: %d\n", data);
    }

    MPI_Finalize();
    return 0;
}
```

**(b) LogP Model & Allreduce Derivation (7 Marks):**

**LogP Model Explanation (3 marks):**
- **L** (Latency) — upper bound on time for a message to travel from sender to receiver.
- **o** (overhead) — time the processor is occupied sending/receiving (CPU busy).
- **g** (gap) — minimum interval between consecutive send/receive operations.
- **P** — number of processors.

A message of $m$ words takes:
$$
T_{msg}(m) = 2o + L + (m-1)g
$$

**Step-by-step derivation for `MPI_Allreduce` (4 marks):**

**Step 1:** Number of stages in a binomial Allreduce = $\log_2(p)$.

For $p=8$:
$$
s = \log_2(8) = 3 \text{ stages}
$$

**Step 2:** Each stage performs one send-receive pair per active node. With 1024 doubles = 8192 bytes per process, the payload per stage is $m = 8192$ bytes.

**Step 3:** Per-stage time (combining latency, bandwidth, and overhead):
$$
T_{stage} = 2\alpha + L + (m - 1) \cdot \beta
$$

Substituting values ($L=5\,\mu s$, $\alpha=10\,\mu s$, $\beta=0.01\,\mu s/\text{byte}$):
$$
T_{stage} = 2(10) + 5 + (8192 - 1)(0.01) = 20 + 5 + 81.91 = 106.91\,\mu s
$$

**Step 4:** Total Allreduce time:
$$
T_{allreduce} = s \cdot T_{stage} = 3 \cdot 106.91 = 320.73\,\mu s
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to include overhead on BOTH sides** ($2o$ not $o$) — loses **1 mark**.
> 2. **Wrong unit for $g$** — if given per-byte, ensure multiplication by message size in bytes, not words. Loses **1 mark**.
> 3. **Mixing up $\alpha$ and $L$** — $\alpha$ is the **send-side cost**, $L$ is **network latency**.
> 4. **Using $\log_2(p)$ stages for Allgather** — Allgather uses $p-1$ sends in some implementations, not $\log_2$. Read the question carefully.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions** — always explicitly mention "no network contention" and "uniform bandwidth" in your answer.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **MPI is a library, not a language.** It is implemented in C, C++, Fortran, Python (`mpi4py`), Java, and more.
- **Every MPI program needs `MPI_Init` and `MPI_Finalize`** — no MPI call may precede Init or follow Finalize.
- **SPMD model** — single program, multiple processes, branched by rank.
- **Communicators** define communication scope; `MPI_COMM_WORLD` is the default universe.
- **Rank 0 is convention**, not enforced — there is no "master" in MPI.
- **Blocking sends** may deadlock without matching receives; **non-blocking sends** return immediately with a request handle.
- **Collective operations** are implicit barriers — all processes in the communicator must participate.
- **`MPI_Reduce`** → root only; **`MPI_Allreduce`** → every process gets the result.
- **LogP Model**: $T_{msg}(m) = 2o + L + (m-1)g$ — memorize this!
- **Speedup**: $S_p = T_1 / T_p$ | **Efficiency**: $E_p = S_p / p$ | **Cost**: $C_p = p \cdot T_p$.
- **Amdahl's Law**: $S_{max} = \dfrac{1}{f_s + f_p/p}$ — assume fixed problem size.
- **Gustafson's Law**: $S(p) = p - f_s(p-1)$ — assume scaled problem size.
- **Compilation:** `mpicc -o prog prog.c` | **Execution:** `mpirun -np P ./prog`.
- **Deadlock trap**: Two processes both calling `MPI_Send` to each other without an intervening receive → **deadlock**.
- **Output order is non-deterministic** unless `MPI_Barrier` or explicit ordering is used.
- **MPI versions**: MPI 1.0 (1994), 2.0 (1997), 3.0 (2012), 4.0 (2021). MPI 4.0 added large-count support and persistent collectives.
- **One-sided communication** (MPI 3.0+) uses `MPI_Put`, `MPI_Get`, `MPI_Accumulate` with **remote memory windows**.
- **Process topologies** (Cartesian, Graph) allow logical organization matching the problem structure.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parallel Numerical Algorithms: Linear Systems, LU Decomposition, and Eigenvalue Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST759 - PARALLEL ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Parallel Numerical Algorithms: Linear Systems, LU Decomposition, and Eigenvalue Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Parallel Numerical Algorithms** are computational procedures designed to solve mathematical problems involving continuous variables—such as systems of linear equations, matrix factorizations, and eigenvalue computations—by exploiting **concurrency**, **data parallelism**, and **task parallelism** across multiple processors or computing nodes. According to the KTU 2024 scheme, these algorithms are evaluated using three canonical performance metrics: **speedup**, **efficiency**, and **scalability** under a fixed problem size (strong scaling) or fixed workload per processor (weak scaling).

The three canonical problem classes in this module are:

1. **Linear Systems** — solving $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ where $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is dense or sparse.
2. **LU Decomposition** — factorizing $A = LU$ (or $PA = LU$ with partial pivoting) to enable forward/backward substitution.
3. **Eigenvalue Problems** — finding scalars $\lambda$ and vectors $\mathbf{v} \neq 0$ such that $A\mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:** A **parallel numerical algorithm** is a numerical method whose arithmetic operations, memory accesses, and communication patterns are explicitly mapped onto a set of $p$ processing elements to minimize the *parallel runtime* $T_p(n)$ while maintaining numerical stability.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **team of 100 chefs** in a massive commercial kitchen tasked with cooking dinner for 10,000 guests.

- **Sequential Algorithm** = One chef cooks every dish, one after another. It works, but is hopelessly slow.
- **Parallel Numerical Algorithm** = The head chef (the *controller/root process*) reads the recipe book and slices the cooking tasks: one chef chops vegetables, another grills meat, another plates desserts. The recipes themselves (the *math*) remain unchanged—only the **work distribution** changes.

For our three problems:

- **Linear Systems ($A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$)** is like finding the exact ingredient mix that yields a target flavor: each chef tests one row of the recipe matrix in parallel.
- **LU Decomposition** is like separating a complex recipe into a *Lower* (base) and *Upper* (garnish) layer so future variants can be assembled quickly without re-deriving the chemistry.
- **Eigenvalue Problems** find the "natural vibration modes" of the kitchen—the dominant flavors the kitchen gravitates toward no matter the menu.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Parallel numerical algorithms do *not* invent new mathematics. They *redistribute* the existing arithmetic onto multiple processors. The sequential work $T_1$ stays the same; the *parallel* work $T_p$ shrinks by exploiting the **parallelism granularity** of the underlying matrix operations.

## 1.3 Performance Metrics — The Trifecta of Parallel Evaluation

For every parallel algorithm in this module, KTU expects you to compute and justify three quantities. Master these—they appear in nearly every Part-B question.

**1. Speedup $S_p$** — how much *faster* the parallel run is versus sequential:
$$S_p = \frac{T_1}{T_p}$$

**2. Efficiency $E_p$** — how *productively* each processor is used:
$$E_p = \frac{S_p}{p} = \frac{T_1}{p \cdot T_p}$$

**3. Cost (Work) $W_p$** — total processor-time expended:
$$W_p = p \cdot T_p$$

An algorithm is **cost-optimal** if $W_p = \Theta(T_1)$ (i.e., efficiency $\to 1$ as $n$ grows for fixed $p$).

## 1.4 Communication Cost Fundamentals

When $p$ processors cooperate, the algorithm pays two extra costs beyond the arithmetic $T_{\text{comp}}$:

- **Communication cost $T_{\text{comm}}$** — time spent sending/receiving data.
- **Idle/synchronization cost $T_{\text{ idle}}$** — time a faster processor waits for a slower one.

The total parallel runtime follows the canonical decomposition:
$$T_p = T_{\text{comp}} + T_{\text{comm}} + T_{\text{idle}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **For the KTU board exam**, whenever a question asks "comment on the performance", explicitly write this three-term equation. Examiners award marks for showing you understand the *trade-off* between computation and communication.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Problem Class I — Dense Linear Systems

### 2.1.1 Sequential Baseline
A dense $n \times n$ system $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ solved by **Gaussian Elimination** requires:
$$T_1 = \Theta(n^3)$$
with $\Theta(n^2)$ memory. The dominant cost is the $n-1$ elimination steps, each touching an $O(n^2)$ submatrix.

### 2.1.2 Sources of Parallelism
Three granularities exist (KTU frequently tests the *right-most* column of this table):

| Parallelism Type | Granularity | Best For |
|---|---|---|
| **Task Parallelism** | Coarse (algorithm-level) | Different stages of elimination |
| **Data Parallelism** | Fine (element-level) | Inner-product / matrix-vector kernels |
| **Pipeline Parallelism** | Medium (row/strip) | Banded matrices |

### 2.1.3 Parallelization Strategies
1. **Row-wise striping** — distribute $n$ rows across $p$ processors; each owns $n/p$ rows.
2. **Block (2D) partitioning** — distribute an $n \times n$ matrix across a $p_r \times p_c$ processor grid; each block is $(n/p_r) \times (n/p_c)$.
3. **Cyclic distribution** — distributes load evenly but increases communication.

## 2.2 Problem Class II — LU Decomposition

### 2.2.1 Mathematical Foundation
Given $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ with non-singular leading principal minors, there exist unique lower-triangular $L$ (with $l_{ii} = 1$) and upper-triangular $U$ such that:
$$A = LU$$
With partial pivoting, a permutation matrix $P$ is introduced:
$$PA = LU$$

### 2.2.2 Why Factorize?
- **Reusability** — once $A = LU$ is computed, solving $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ for *multiple right-hand sides* costs only $O(n^2)$ each (forward + back substitution), not $O(n^3)$.
- **Determinant** — $\det(A) = \prod_i u_{ii}$ (cheap once $U$ is known).
- **Matrix inverse** — column-by-column back substitution.

### 2.2.3 Right-Looking vs. Left-Looking Algorithms
- **Right-looking**: After eliminating column $k$, update all columns to its right. Naturally parallel across columns.
- **Left-looking**: Column $k$ waits for updates from columns $<k$ before its own elimination. Lower communication, used in **ScaLAPACK**.

### 2.2.4 Parallel Complexity
With 2D block-cyclic distribution on a $\sqrt{p} \times \sqrt{p}$ grid:
- Computation: $T_{\text{comp}} = O\!\left(\frac{n^3}{p}\right)$
- Communication: $T_{\text{comm}} = O\!\left(\frac{n^2}{\sqrt{p}}\right)$
- Total: $T_p = O\!\left(\frac{n^3}{p} + \frac{n^2}{\sqrt{p}}\right)$

The algorithm is **cost-optimal** when the communication term is dominated, i.e., when $n \gg p^{1/3}$ approximately.

## 2.3 Problem Class III — Eigenvalue Problems

### 2.3.1 Mathematical Foundation
Find scalars $\lambda_i$ (eigenvalues) and non-zero vectors $\mathbf{v}_i$ (eigenvectors) satisfying:
$$A\mathbf{v}_i = \lambda_i \mathbf{v}_i$$
For symmetric $A$, all $\lambda_i$ are real and eigenvectors are orthogonal. For non-symmetric $A$, eigenvalues may be complex and right/left eigenvectors differ.

### 2.3.2 Power Method (Sequential)
The simplest iterative method finds the *dominant* eigenvalue:
$$\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} = A \mathbf{x}^{(k)}, \quad \lambda^{(k)} = \frac{(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})^T \mathbf{x}^{(k+1)}}{(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})^T \mathbf{x}^{(k)}}$$
Convergence rate: $O\!\left(\left|\frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_1}\right|^k\right)$.

### 2.3.3 Parallelization of Power Method
The dominant cost per iteration is one **matrix-vector product** ($O(n^2)$ for dense $A$), which parallelizes trivially: distribute rows of $A$ and corresponding entries of $\mathbf{x}$ across $p$ processors, compute partial inner products locally, then **All-Reduce** to sum.
$$T_p^{\text{MVP}} = O\!\left(\frac{n^2}{p}\right) + T_{\text{AllReduce}}$$

### 2.3.4 QR Algorithm (Sequential Reference)
Computes *all* eigenvalues by iteratively factoring $A^{(k)} = Q^{(k)} R^{(k)}$ and setting $A^{(k+1)} = R^{(k)} Q^{(k)}$. Under mild conditions, $A^{(k)} \to$ upper triangular (Schur form) with eigenvalues on the diagonal.

### 2.3.5 Parallel QR Iteration
Each QR factorization can be parallelized via **Householder transformations** or **Givens rotations**. The shift-and-deflate strategy yields multiple eigenvalues computed in parallel branches.

### 2.3.6 Divide-and-Conquer for Symmetric Tridiagonal Eigenvalue
- Reduce $A$ to tridiagonal $T$ in $O(n^3/p)$ parallel time.
- Split $T$ into $T_1$ and $T_2$ at the middle, compute their eigendecompositions independently (parallel), then merge via **rank-1 secular equation** solver.
- Recurrence: $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n^2) \Rightarrow T(n) = O(n^2 \log n)$ sequentially; in parallel, $T_p = O(n^2 \log n / p)$ plus merge overhead.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Day Use Only** — Memorize this table; KTU questions frequently ask for these exact formulas.

| Algorithm | Sequential $T_1$ | Parallel $T_p$ (ideal, $p$ PEs) | Speedup $S_p$ | Cost-Optimal Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Dense Linear System (Gaussian Elim.) | $\Theta(n^3)$ | $O(n^3/p)$ | $\Theta(p)$ | $n \gg p$ (so $T_{\text{comm}}$ negligible) |
| LU Decomposition (2D block) | $\Theta(n^3)$ | $O(n^3/p + n^2/\sqrt{p})$ | $\approx p$ for $n \gg p^{1/3}$ | $n \gg p^{1/3}$ |
| Matrix-Vector Product | $\Theta(n^2)$ | $O(n^2/p + \log p)$ | $\approx p$ for $n^2 \gg p \log p$ | $n \gg \sqrt{p \log p}$ |
| Power Method (per iter.) | $\Theta(n^2)$ | $O(n^2/p + \log p)$ | $\approx p$ | $n \gg \sqrt{p}$ |
| QR Iteration (one step) | $\Theta(n^3)$ | $O(n^3/p + n^2 \log p / \sqrt{p})$ | depends on shift strategy | $n \gg p^{1/3}$ |
| Sym. Tridiagonal Eigenvalue | $O(n^2 \log n)$ | $O(n^2 \log n / p)$ | $\Theta(p)$ for dense cases | $n \gg p$ |
| Jacobi Rotation (Sweep) | $\Theta(n^3)$ | $O(n^3/p^2)$ (cyclic) | sub-linear | rarely cost-optimal |

### 2.4.1 Amdahl's Law (Strong Scaling Limit)
$$S_p \le \frac{1}{f + \frac{1-f}{p}}$$
where $f$ is the inherently serial fraction. As $p \to \infty$: $S_p \to 1/f$.

### 2.4.2 Gustafson's Law (Weak Scaling)
$$S_p = p - f(p-1)$$
Linear speedup is preserved by growing the problem with $p$.

### 2.4.3 Isoefficiency Metric
A parallel system has isoefficiency function $\Theta(f_p(p))$: the problem size $W$ must grow as $f_p(p)$ to keep efficiency fixed. Lower is better.

### 2.4.4 Standard Bandwidth/Latency Model
$$T_{\text{comm}} = t_s + t_w \cdot m$$
where $t_s$ = startup (latency) cost, $t_w$ = per-word transfer cost, $m$ = message size.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application | Algorithm Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Structural Engineering** | FEM stiffness equation $K\mathbf{u} = \mathbf{F}$ | Parallel LU/Cholesky |
| **Power Systems** | Load-flow analysis | Parallel LU on sparse matrices |
| **VLSI Design** | Circuit simulation MNA equations | Parallel sparse LU (KLU, PARDISO) |
| **Control Systems** | Modal analysis, stability margins | Parallel QR / Lanczos |
| **Fluid Dynamics (CFD)** | Pressure-Poisson equations | Parallel iterative solvers |
| **Quantum Chemistry** | Electronic structure (Hartree-Fock) | Parallel dense eigensolvers |
| **Google PageRank** | Stationary distribution of web graph | Parallel Power Method |
| **Vibration Analysis** | Modal frequencies of bridges/turbines | Parallel Lanczos / Subspace Iteration |
| **Recommendation Systems** | SVD of user-item matrices | Parallel Halko et al. randomized SVD |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sequential Gaussian Elimination Complexity

We derive the $\Theta(n^3)$ sequential cost of Gaussian elimination on an $n \times n$ dense system.

**Step 1 — Identify the outer loop.**
Elimination proceeds for $k = 1, 2, \ldots, n-1$. For each $k$, we touch the submatrix of size $(n-k) \times (n-k)$ in the bottom-right corner.

**Step 2 — Count operations for the $k$-th step.**
Within the $k$-th step, we must:
- Compute the **multipliers** $m_{ik} = a_{ik}^{(k)} / a_{kk}^{(k)}$ for $i = k+1, \ldots, n$. This is $n-k$ divisions.
- Update the submatrix: $a_{ij}^{(k+1)} = a_{ij}^{(k)} - m_{ik} \cdot a_{kj}^{(k)}$ for $i,j = k+1, \ldots, n$. This is $(n-k)(n-k)$ multiply-subtract operations.

So operations at step $k$:
$$O_k = (n-k) + (n-k)^2 = (n-k)(n-k+1) = \Theta\!\left((n-k)^2\right)$$

**Step 3 — Sum across all steps.**
$$T_1 = \sum_{k=1}^{n-1} \Theta\!\left((n-k)^2\right) = \sum_{j=1}^{n-1} \Theta(j^2) = \Theta\!\left(\sum_{j=1}^{n} j^2\right) = \Theta\!\left(\frac{n(n+1)(2n+1)}{6}\right) = \Theta(n^3)$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion.**
This establishes the canonical sequential cost used in every subsequent speedup formula.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Parallel LU with 2D Block-Cyclic Distribution

**Problem Setup.** Distribute $A$ across a $\sqrt{p} \times \sqrt{p}$ process grid, with each process owning an $(n/\sqrt{p}) \times (n/\sqrt{p})$ block using **block-cyclic** mapping (ScaLAPACK's PDGETRF).

**Step 1 — Per-step cost on each process.**
At step $k$, each process performs:
- Local DGETRF on its active block of size $b \times b$: cost $O(b^3)$.
- Local DTRSM (triangular solve) on its row block: $O(b^2 \cdot n/\sqrt{p})$.
- Local DGEMM (matrix update) on the trailing submatrix: $O(b \cdot (n/\sqrt{p})^2) = O(b n^2 / p)$.

**Step 2 — Sum across all $n/b$ steps.**
Computation per process summed over all steps:
$$T_{\text{comp}} = \frac{n}{b} \cdot \left[ O(b^3) + O(b^2 n/\sqrt{p}) + O(b n^2 / p) \right]$$

Substitute $b = n/\sqrt{p}$ (load-balanced block size for square grid):
$$T_{\text{comp}} = \frac{n}{n/\sqrt{p}} \cdot \left[ O(n^3/p^{3/2}) + O(n^3/p^2) + O(n^3/p^{3/2}) \right] = O(n^3 / p)$$

**Step 3 — Communication cost.**
At each step, broadcasts occur along rows and columns of the process grid (panel broadcast + row update broadcast). Each broadcast on a $k$-processor subgrid has cost $O(t_s \log k + t_w \cdot k \cdot b^2)$. Summing over $n/b$ steps with $k = \sqrt{p}$:
$$T_{\text{comm}} = O\!\left( \frac{n}{b} \cdot (t_s \log \sqrt{p} + t_w \sqrt{p} \cdot b^2) \right) = O\!\left( t_s \cdot \frac{n}{b} \log p + t_w \cdot n \cdot b \cdot \sqrt{p} \right)$$

Substitute $b = n/\sqrt{p}$:
$$T_{\text{comm}} = O\!\left( t_s \cdot \frac{n \sqrt{p}}{n} \log p + t_w \cdot n \cdot \frac{n}{\sqrt{p}} \cdot \sqrt{p} \right) = O\!\left( t_s \sqrt{p} \log p + t_w n^2 \right) \approx O(n^2 / \sqrt{p})$$

**Step 4 — Total parallel runtime.**
$$\boxed{T_p^{\text{LU}} = O\!\left( \frac{n^3}{p} + \frac{n^2}{\sqrt{p}} \right)}$$

**Step 5 — Cost-optimality condition.**
The parallel algorithm is cost-optimal when $T_{\text{comp}} \gg T_{\text{comm}}$, i.e., $n^3/p \gg n^2/\sqrt{p}$, which simplifies to:
$$n \gg \sqrt{p} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad p \ll n^2$$

**Step 6 — Resultant speedup.**
$$S_p = \frac{T_1}{T_p} = \frac{\Theta(n^3)}{O(n^3/p + n^2/\sqrt{p})} = \frac{p}{1 + p/n\sqrt{p}} = \frac{p}{1 + \sqrt{p}/n}$$

For $n \gg \sqrt{p}$: $S_p \to p$, i.e., **linear speedup**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Parallel Matrix-Vector Product (Backbone of Power Method)

**Step 1 — Sequential cost.**
For $\mathbf{y} = A\mathbf{x}$ with $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$:
$$T_1 = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} 1 = n^2 = \Theta(n^2)$$

**Step 2 — Parallel partition.**
Distribute $n$ rows of $A$ across $p$ processors, each owning $n/p$ rows. Each processor $P_k$ computes $y_i$ for $i \in [\frac{kn}{p}+1, \frac{(k+1)n}{p}]$ locally:
$$T_{\text{comp}}^{(k)} = \frac{n}{p} \cdot n = \frac{n^2}{p}$$

**Step 3 — Communication cost.**
Each processor must broadcast its local $y$ partial, then collect contributions. This is a **reduction** across $p$ processors with messages of size $n/p$:
$$T_{\text{comm}} = t_s \log p + t_w \cdot \frac{n}{p} \cdot \log p \quad \text{(tree reduction, log}p\text{ levels)}$$

**Step 4 — Total parallel runtime.**
$$T_p^{\text{MVP}} = \frac{n^2}{p} + t_s \log p + t_w \cdot \frac{n}{p} \log p$$

**Step 5 — Cost-optimality.**
For cost-optimality, $p \cdot T_p \approx T_1$, so:
$$p \cdot \left( \frac{n^2}{p} + t_s \log p \right) \approx n^2 + p \, t_s \log p = n^2$$
This requires $p \cdot t_s \log p \ll n^2$, i.e., $n \gg \sqrt{p \log p}$.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Power Method Convergence Rate

**Step 1 — Expand initial vector in eigenbasis.**
For symmetric $A$ with orthonormal eigenvectors $\mathbf{v}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{v}_n$ and eigenvalues $|\lambda_1| > |\lambda_2| \ge \cdots \ge |\lambda_n|$:
$$\mathbf{x}^{(0)} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i \mathbf{v}_i$$

**Step 2 — Apply $A$ repeatedly.**
$$A^k \mathbf{x}^{(0)} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i \lambda_i^k \mathbf{v}_i = \lambda_1^k \!\left( c_1 \mathbf{v}_1 + \sum_{i=2}^{n} c_i \left(\frac{\lambda_i}{\lambda_1}\right)^k \mathbf{v}_i \right)$$

**Step 3 — Factor out dominant term.**
As $k \to \infty$, the ratio of the $i$-th to the $1$-st term is $O\!\left(\left|\frac{\lambda_i}{\lambda_1}\right|^k\right)$. The slowest-decaying error term is governed by:
$$\rho = \left| \frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_1} \right| < 1$$

**Step 4 — Convergence rate.**
$$\frac{\|A^k \mathbf{x}^{(0)} - \lambda_1^k c_1 \mathbf{v}_1\|}{\|A^k \mathbf{x}^{(0)}\|} = O(\rho^k)$$

**Step 5 — Iterations to $\epsilon$ accuracy.**
$$k \ge \frac{\log(1/\epsilon)}{\log(1/\rho)} = \frac{\ln(1/\epsilon)}{-\ln \rho}$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Speedup Bound for Parallel QR Algorithm

**Step 1 — Sequential cost of one QR iteration.**
Each QR step on an $n \times n$ matrix requires $O(n^3)$ flops (Householder) or $O(n^3)$ (Givens).

**Step 2 — Parallel cost of one QR step.**
Using the same 2D block-cyclic distribution as LU, panel factorization (Householder on a column) and update (DGEMM on trailing matrix) parallelize as:
$$T_p^{\text{QR step}} = O\!\left(\frac{n^3}{p} + \frac{n^2}{\sqrt{p}} \log p\right)$$

**Step 3 — Number of QR iterations.**
Under generic conditions, $O(n)$ iterations are needed for the full Schur form, giving:
$$T_p^{\text{QR total}} = O\!\left( n \cdot \left( \frac{n^3}{p} + \frac{n^2}{\sqrt{p}} \log p \right) \right) = O\!\left(\frac{n^4}{p} + \frac{n^3 \log p}{\sqrt{p}}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Resultant speedup.**
$$S_p^{\text{QR}} = \frac{n^4}{n^4/p + n^3 \log p / \sqrt{p}} = \frac{p}{1 + p \log p / (n \sqrt{p})}$$

For $n \gg \sqrt{p} \log p$, $S_p \to p$.

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Parallel vs. Sequential)

| Aspect | Sequential | Parallel | Key Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| Workload partition | One process | $p$ processes, blocks/rows/columns | Load balance vs. communication |
| Memory footprint | $O(n^2)$ on one node | $O(n^2/p)$ per node | Scalable to larger $n$ |
| Numerical stability | Standard pivoting | Needs global pivoting decisions | More communication |
| Code complexity | Simple loops | Message-passing / threads | Engineering cost |
| Best speedup | 1× | Up to $p$ (Amdahl ceiling) | $f$ (serial fraction) |
| Failure mode | Single point | $p$ points of failure | Reliability cost |
| Numerical error | Identical | Identical (same math) | None — math is invariant |

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example — Parallel Speedup for $n=1000$, $p=16$

**Given:** $n = 1000$, $p = 16$ (assumed 4×4 grid, $\sqrt{p}=4$).

**Step 1 — Sequential time.**
$$T_1 = \frac{2}{3} n^3 = \frac{2}{3} \times 10^9 = 6.67 \times 10^8 \text{ flops}$$

**Step 2 — Parallel computation time.**
$$T_{\text{comp}} = \frac{n^3}{p} = \frac{10^9}{16} = 6.25 \times 10^7 \text{ flops}$$

**Step 3 — Parallel communication time** (assume $t_s = 100 \mu s$, $t_w = 10$ ns/word, $b = n/\sqrt{p} = 250$):
$$T_{\text{comm}} = t_s \sqrt{p} \log p + t_w n^2 = 100 \times 10^{-6} \times 4 \times 4 + 10^{-8} \times 10^6 \approx 1.6 \times 10^{-3} + 10^{-2} \approx 0.0116 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4 — Speedup.**
$$S_p = \frac{T_1}{T_{\text{comp}} + T_{\text{comm}}} \approx \frac{6.67 \times 10^8}{6.25 \times 10^7 + T_{\text{comm-related}}}$$

If $T_{\text{comm}}$ is in flop-equivalent units where the machine does $\sim 10^9$ flops/s, then $T_{\text{comm}} \approx 10^7$ flops worth. Thus $T_p \approx 7.25 \times 10^7$ and $S_p \approx 9.2$, which is below $p = 16$ due to the *square-root* communication penalty. This is the typical "sub-linear" speedup you will see in KTU numerical-answer questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    D3 --> E3[Local Computation Phase]
    E1 --> F1[Communication Phase AllReduce or Broadcast]
    E2 --> F2[Communication Phase Panel Broadcast Row Update]
    E3 --> F3[Communication Phase Cyclic Shifts]
    F1 --> G1[Postprocessing Forward Back Substitution]
    F2 --> G2[Postprocessing Permutation Tracking]
    F3 --> G3[Postprocessing Deflation and Convergence Check]

    G2 --> H1
    G3 --> H1
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Initial Matrix A on Root] --> B1[Scatter A across p Processes]
    B1 --> C1[Process P1 Block 1 1]
    B1 --> C2[Process P2 Block 1 2]
    B1 --> C3[Process P3 Block 2 1]
    B1 --> C4[Process P4 Block 2 2]

    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    C4 --> D1
    D1 --> E1[Broadcast Panel L and U within Row]

```

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    B0 --> C0[Each Process Pi Computes Local yi Equals A Sub i times x]

    D0 --> E0[AllReduce Sum Across p Processes]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Matrix A of Size n by n] --> B1[Hessenberg Reduction Parallel Householder]
    B1 --> C1[Shifted QR Step Iteration k]
    C1 --> D1[Compute Wilkinson Shift mu k]
    D1 --> E1[Form Ak minus mu k times I]
    E1 --> F1[Factor A k minus mu k I Equals Q k times R k]
    F1 --> G1[Form A k plus 1 Equals R k times Q k plus mu k times I]
    G1 --> H1{Off Diagonal Norm less than eps}

    H1 -- Yes --> I1[Deflate Deflation: Extract Eigenvalue from Diagonal]
    I1 --> J1[Restart on Reduced Submatrix]
    J1 --> C1
    J1 --> K1[All Eigenvalues Computed]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Q:** Define *speedup*, *efficiency*, and *cost* of a parallel algorithm. State the mathematical conditions under which a parallel algorithm is called *cost-optimal*.

**Model Answer:**

> **Speedup** $S_p$ is the ratio of the sequential runtime $T_1$ to the parallel runtime $T_p$ on $p$ processors:
> $$S_p = \frac{T_1}{T_p}$$
>
> **Efficiency** $E_p$ is the average utilization of each processor:
> $$E_p = \frac{S_p}{p} = \frac{T_1}{p \cdot T_p}$$
>
> **Cost (Work)** $W_p$ is the total processor-time expended:
> $$W_p = p \cdot T_p$$
>
> A parallel algorithm is **cost-optimal** if its cost equals the asymptotic complexity of the best-known sequential algorithm, i.e., $W_p = \Theta(T_1)$. Equivalently, efficiency approaches 1 (or a constant) as the problem size $n$ grows with $p$ held fixed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Examiners give 1 mark each for the three definitions and 1 mark for the cost-optimality condition. Mentioning *efficiency* explicitly in the condition scores the full 3.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Distinguish between *right-looking* and *left-looking* variants of the LU decomposition algorithm. Which variant is preferred in distributed-memory parallel implementations and why?

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Right-Looking LU | Left-Looking LU |
|---|---|---|
| Update direction | After computing column $k$, update *all* columns $j > k$ to the right | Before computing column $k$, *receive* updates from columns $j < k$ on the left |
| Data dependency | Column $k$ does not wait for previous columns | Column $k$ waits for left-side updates |
| Communication pattern | Many small broadcasts (column-wise) | Fewer large broadcasts (block-wise) |
| Best suited for | Shared-memory, GPU | Distributed-memory (ScaLAPACK) |
| Numerical behaviour | Standard, well-understood | Equivalent numerically, more communication-efficient |

**Conclusion:** The **left-looking** variant is preferred in distributed-memory parallel implementations (e.g., ScaLAPACK's PDGETRF) because it groups communication into fewer, larger panel broadcasts, amortizing the high *latency* $t_s$ of message passing.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** A 2-row comparison table with the conclusion statement scores full marks. The phrase *amortizing latency* in the conclusion is the high-yield keyword KTU examiners love.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

**Q (a)** Derive the sequential time complexity of Gaussian elimination for solving an $n \times n$ dense linear system $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$. Show that $T_1 = \Theta(n^3)$. **(7 Marks)**

**Q (b)** For the same system, design a parallel Gaussian elimination algorithm on $p$ processors using 1D row-striping. Compute the parallel runtime $T_p$, speedup $S_p$, efficiency $E_p$, and discuss cost-optimality. Assume each processor holds $n/p$ rows and uses the standard $\log p$ reduction model for the broadcast step. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Step 1 — Outer loop structure.** Gaussian elimination runs for $k = 1, 2, \ldots, n-1$. At step $k$, the submatrix below and to the right of the pivot has size $(n-k) \times (n-k)$.

**Step 2 — Operations per step.**
- Multiplier computation: $n - k$ divisions.
- Submatrix update: $(n-k)^2$ multiply-subtract operations.
- Total at step $k$: $\Theta((n-k)^2)$ flops.

**Step 3 — Aggregate.**
$$T_1 = \sum_{k=1}^{n-1} \Theta((n-k)^2) = \sum_{j=1}^{n-1} \Theta(j^2) = \Theta\!\left( \frac{(n-1)n(2n-1)}{6} \right) = \Theta(n^3) \quad \blacksquare$$

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1 — Partition.** Distribute $n$ rows of $A$ across $p$ processors, each owning $n/p$ rows (assume $p \mid n$).

**Step 2 — Per-step computation.** At step $k$, the processor holding the pivot row performs:
- One **broadcast** of row $k$ to all other $p-1$ processors: $T_{\text{bcast}} = t_s \log p + t_w \cdot n \cdot (1) = O(t_s \log p + t_w n)$.

Each of the $p$ processors then updates its local $n/p$ rows:
- Local update: $(n/p) \cdot (n-k)$ flops per step.

**Step 3 — Aggregate computation over all steps.**
$$T_{\text{comp}} = \sum_{k=1}^{n-1} \frac{n}{p} (n-k) = \frac{n}{p} \cdot \Theta(n^2) = \frac{n^3}{p} = \Theta\!\left( \frac{n^3}{p} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Aggregate communication.**
$$T_{\text{comm}} = \sum_{k=1}^{n-1} (t_s \log p + t_w n) = (n-1) t_s \log p + (n-1) t_w n = \Theta(n \, t_s \log p + n^2 t_w)$$

**Step 5 — Total parallel runtime.**
$$T_p = \frac{n^3}{p} + n \cdot t_s \log p + n^2 \cdot t_w$$

**Step 6 — Speedup.**
$$S_p = \frac{\Theta(n^3)}{n^3/p + n \cdot t_s \log p + n^2 t_w} = \frac{p}{1 + \dfrac{p \, t_s \log p}{n^2} + \dfrac{p \, t_w}{n}}$$

**Step 7 — Efficiency.**
$$E_p = \frac{S_p}{p} = \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{p \, t_s \log p}{n^2} + \dfrac{p \, t_w}{n}}$$

**Step 8 — Cost-optimality discussion.** For $E_p \to \Theta(1)$, both communication terms must be $o(n^2)$:
- $p \, t_s \log p \ll n^2 \Rightarrow n \gg \sqrt{p \log p}$
- $p \, t_w \ll n \Rightarrow n \gg p$

Thus **cost-optimality requires $n \gg p$** (i.e., the problem is much larger than the number of processors). When $n \gg p$, $S_p \to p$ and $E_p \to 1$, giving linear speedup. When $n$ is comparable to $p$, communication dominates and the speedup collapses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Valuation Tip for 7-mark sub-parts:** Always end with an explicit *cost-optimality condition*. KTU examiners reserve 1–2 marks for it.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Q (a)** Explain the *Power Method* for finding the dominant eigenvalue of a matrix. Derive its convergence rate in terms of the ratio $|\lambda_2 / \lambda_1|$. **(7 Marks)**

**Q (b)** Parallelize the Power Method on $p$ processors using row-wise distribution. Show that each iteration takes $T_p = O(n^2/p + \log p)$ for the matrix-vector product and AllReduce step. State clearly how many parallel iterations are needed for $\epsilon$-accuracy and the overall cost. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Step 1 — Algorithm statement.** Given symmetric $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ with eigenvalues $|\lambda_1| > |\lambda_2| \ge \cdots \ge |\lambda_n|$, the Power Method iterates:
$$\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} = A \mathbf{x}^{(k)}, \quad \mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} \leftarrow \frac{\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)}}{\|\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)}\|}$$
The Rayleigh quotient gives the eigenvalue estimate:
$$\lambda^{(k)} = \frac{(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})^T A \mathbf{x}^{(k)}}{(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})^T \mathbf{x}^{(k)}}$$

**Step 2 — Eigenbasis expansion.**
$$\mathbf{x}^{(0)} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i \mathbf{v}_i, \quad c_1 \neq 0$$

Then:
$$A^k \mathbf{x}^{(0)} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i \lambda_i^k \mathbf{v}_i = \lambda_1^k \!\left[ c_1 \mathbf{v}_1 + \sum_{i=2}^{n} c_i \left( \frac{\lambda_i}{\lambda_1} \right)^k \mathbf{v}_i \right]$$

**Step 3 — Factor out the dominant mode.**
Dividing by $\lambda_1^k$ and normalizing, the residual after $k$ iterations is bounded by:
$$\left\| \frac{A^k \mathbf{x}^{(0)}}{\|A^k \mathbf{x}^{(0)}\|} - \text{sign}(c_1) \mathbf{v}_1 \right\| = O\!\left( \left| \frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_1} \right|^k \right)$$

**Step 4 — Convergence rate.**
$$\rho = \left| \frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_1} \right| < 1$$
For $\epsilon$-accuracy, we need $\rho^k \le \epsilon$, giving:
$$k \ge \frac{\log(1/\epsilon)}{-\log \rho} = \frac{\ln(1/\epsilon)}{-\ln \rho} \quad \blacksquare$$

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1 — Data distribution.** Distribute the $n$ rows of $A$ across $p$ processors, each holding $n/p$ rows. Replicate $\mathbf{x}^{(k)}$ on every processor (it is small, $O(n)$).

**Step 2 — Local matrix-vector product.** Each processor $P_i$ computes its local $y_i = A_i \mathbf{x}^{(k)}$ for its $n/p$ rows. Cost: $T_{\text{MVP-local}} = (n/p) \cdot n = n^2/p$.

**Step 3 — AllReduce for norm.** To normalize $\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)}$, all processors need $\|\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)}\|$. A tree-based AllReduce over $p$ processors with messages of size $O(1)$ per word:
$$T_{\text{AllReduce}} = t_s \log p + t_w \cdot n \cdot \log p / p = O(\log p + n \log p / p)$$

(For large $n$, the $n^2/p$ term dominates, so $T_{\text{AllReduce}} = O(\log p)$ in the big-O sense.)

**Step 4 — Total per-iteration cost.**
$$T_p^{\text{iter}} = \frac{n^2}{p} + O(\log p) = O\!\left( \frac{n^2}{p} + \log p \right)$$

**Step 5 — Number of iterations for $\epsilon$-accuracy.** From part (a):
$$K = \left\lceil \frac{\ln(1/\epsilon)}{-\ln \rho} \right\rceil$$

This is **independent of $p$ and $n$** (it depends only on the spectral gap and the desired tolerance).

**Step 6 — Total parallel runtime.**
$$T_p^{\text{total}} = K \cdot \left( \frac{n^2}{p} + \log p \right) = O\!\left( \frac{K n^2}{p} + K \log p \right)$$

**Step 7 — Cost and cost-optimality.**
$$W_p = p \cdot T_p^{\text{total}} = O(K n^2 + p K \log p)$$

For cost-optimality, $W_p = \Theta(T_1) = \Theta(K n^2)$, so we need:
$$p \log p \ll n^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad n \gg \sqrt{p \log p}$$

Under this condition, the parallel Power Method is **cost-optimal** and achieves **linear speedup** $S_p = \Theta(p)$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently forget to **normalize** $\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)}$ after the matrix-vector product, causing the vector to overflow or underflow. The Power Method **must** normalize at every iteration, even in parallel. Also, the *Rayleigh quotient* in the eigenvalue estimate requires a global reduction (AllReduce), not just local computation — this is a common mark-losing mistake.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Generic Warnings)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes in Module 4 Numerical Problems:**
>
> 1. **Confusing flops and wall-clock time.** $T_1$ and $T_p$ are usually in flop counts; converting to wall-clock requires dividing by the processor's flop rate.
> 2. **Ignoring communication in the big-O.** Always write $T_p = T_{\text{comp}} + T_{\text{comm}}$; quoting only $T_{\text{comp}}$ loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting pivoting in parallel LU.** Partial pivoting in parallel requires *global* column max computations (an AllReduce), changing the cost model. Without pivoting, the algorithm is numerically unstable.
> 4. **Conflating cost-optimal and linear-speedup.** Cost-optimality ($W_p = \Theta(T_1)$) is *weaker* than linear speedup ($S_p = \Theta(p)$). State which one you are proving.
> 5. **Power Method without convergence guarantee.** The Power Method converges *only* if the dominant eigenvalue is unique and the initial vector has a non-zero component along $\mathbf{v}_1$. Mention this assumption.
> 6. **QR vs. QL confusion.** The QR algorithm uses $A_{k+1} = R_k Q_k$ (right multiplication); the QL variant uses $L_k Q_k$ for non-symmetric matrices.

- [x] **Sequential baseline costs:** Gaussian Elim. = $\Theta(n^3)$, MVP = $\Theta(n^2)$, QR step = $\Theta(n^3)$, Power Method per iter = $\Theta(n^2)$.
- [x] **Parallel LU on 2D grid:** $T_p = O(n^3/p + n^2/\sqrt{p})$; cost-optimal when $n \gg \sqrt{p}$.
- [x] **Parallel Power Method:** per iter = $O(n^2/p + \log p)$; total = $K \cdot T_p^{\text{iter}}$ with $K = \lceil \log(1/\epsilon)/(-\log \rho) \rceil$.
- [x] **Convergence rate of Power Method:** geometric with ratio $\rho = |\lambda_2/\lambda_1|$.
- [x] **Three parallelization strategies:** row striping (1D), block partitioning (2D), block-cyclic (ScaLAPACK).
- [x] **Amdahl's Law:** $S_p \le 1/(f + (1-f)/p)$; serial fraction $f$ caps speedup.
- [x] **Gustafson's Law:** $S_p = p - f(p-1)$; weak scaling preserves linearity.
- [x] **Communication model:** $T_{\text{comm}} = t_s + t_w m$ (linear in message size with fixed latency).
- [x] **Right-looking vs. Left-looking LU:** right for shared memory, left for distributed memory.
- [x] **Cost-optimality:** $W_p = \Theta(T_1)$, equivalently $E_p = \Theta(1)$ as $n \to \infty$.
- [x] **Practical solvers referenced:** ScaLAPACK (PDGETRF), LAPACK (DGETRF), ARPACK, PARDISO, SLEPc, KLU.
- [x] **Numerical stability:** Always pivot (partial pivoting in LU, Wilkinson shift in QR); without pivoting, results are unreliable.
